commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when `stop` is `std::string::npos` causes wildcard recursion to restart from topic beginning
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `topic.find('/', start)` returns `std::string::npos` (last segment), the recursive call for the wildcard child passes `stop + 1` as the new `start`. Since `std::string::npos` is `(size_t)-1`, `stop + 1` wraps around to `0`. The recursive call receives `start=0, stop=npos`, which causes the loop to be skipped (condition `stop != npos` is false) and the function proceeds directly to the exact-match section after the loop, incorrectly associating the message with the wildcard child's root node. This can lead to messages being delivered to unintended subscribers, violating topic-based access control expectations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write to fixed-size `triggeredTopics` array if `drain()` does not fully reset `numTriggeredTopics`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks `if (numTriggeredTopics == 64)` before writing to `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]`. However, the recursive `publish` call for wildcard children can itself add entries to the same `triggeredTopics` array. If `drain()` does not reset `numTriggeredTopics` back to 0 (or resets it to a nonzero value), or if entries are added by the recursive call such that `numTriggeredTopics` jumps past 64 without hitting exactly 64, the equality check `== 64` would be bypassed, causing an out-of-bounds write. Even without recursion issues, the equality-only check is fragile; a `>=` check would be safer against any code path that could increment beyond 64 without triggering the drain.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `uxQueueLength * uxItemSize` is performed in UBaseType_t before casting to size_t, and the overflow check uses configASSERT which may be compiled out in release builds (it's a debug-only assertion in many FreeRTOS configurations). Additionally, `sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes` can overflow without any check. If either overflow occurs, a smaller-than-expected buffer is allocated, and subsequent queue operations will write beyond the allocated memory, leading to heap corruption and potential code execution.

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in dimension size multiplication used for Eigen matrix map construction
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` and `output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]` are computed using `int` arithmetic, which can overflow for large tensor dimensions. The overflowed (smaller) value is then used as the column count for the Eigen matrix map. This causes the map to reference a smaller region than the actual tensor data, and subsequent column accesses (e.g., `in_mat.col(in_offset)`) using properly computed `int64_t` offsets can read/write out of bounds of the mapped region, leading to heap buffer over-read or over-write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Debug-only assertion allows zero or negative output_size in release builds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `DCHECK_GT(output_size[i], 0)` is only enforced in debug builds. In production/release builds, if `pooling_ratio_[i]` is very large relative to `input_size[i]`, `output_size[i]` can be zero or negative. A zero output_size leads to allocating a zero-sized output tensor while the pooling loops may still attempt to access it (since `height_cum_seq.size() - 1` underflows for an empty sequence), or negative values lead to unexpected behavior in `TensorShape` construction and subsequent memory operations. An attacker can craft inputs with specific pooling ratios to trigger undefined behavior or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read/write via unchecked pooling sequence values used as matrix column indices
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values from `height_cum_seq` and `width_cum_seq` (generated by `GeneratePoolingSequence`) are used to compute `in_offset` and `out_offset` for column access into Eigen matrix maps. While `height_end` and `width_end` are clamped to `height_max`/`width_max`, the start values (`height_start`, `width_start`) are not validated to be non-negative. If `GeneratePoolingSequence` returns negative values or values that otherwise cause `in_offset` or `out_offset` to exceed the number of columns in the mapped matrices, out-of-bounds memory access occurs. This can lead to information disclosure or arbitrary memory corruption.

commit_message: Fixed handling of empty JPEG-LS scans.
cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow check relies on configASSERT which may be disabled in production builds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication overflow check uses configASSERT, which is typically compiled out (becomes a no-op) in release/production builds. If configASSERT is disabled, an attacker or buggy caller could pass uxQueueLength and uxItemSize values that overflow when multiplied, resulting in a small xQueueSizeInBytes. This leads to a heap allocation much smaller than expected, and subsequent queue operations would write beyond the allocated buffer, causing heap buffer overflow. The same issue applies to the addition overflow check for sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes.

commit_message:
Properly validate sparse tensor in `SparseTensorSliceDataset`

Existing validation was incomplete.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415375048
Change-Id: I14cd18f29ede73286f3ffac35171bd15828997e9
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that the number of index rows equals the number of values, allowing mismatched sparse tensor components.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks consistency between indices and values only when one of them is empty (zero elements). However, `num_indices` is `indices->NumElements()` (i.e., rows * cols of the matrix), not the number of rows. For the non-empty case, there is no check that `indices->dim_size(0) == values->dim_size(0)`. An attacker could supply an indices matrix with a different number of rows than the length of the values vector. This mismatch propagates into `sparse::SparseTensor::Create` and subsequent operations, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads or writes when iterating over the sparse tensor's entries, as one component may be shorter than expected.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(ctx, TensorShapeUtils::IsVector(values->shape()),
                errors::InvalidArgument(
                    "Input values should be a vector but received shape ",
                    indices->shape().DebugString()));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Error message for values shape validation incorrectly prints indices shape, masking debugging of malformed inputs.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The error message for the values shape check prints `indices->shape().DebugString()` instead of `values->shape().DebugString()`. While this is primarily a bug rather than a direct exploit, misleading error messages can hinder security auditing and debugging, making it harder to diagnose and respond to malformed or malicious inputs targeting input validation.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbound recursion in PI and comment handling can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When encountering processing instructions ('<?...?>') or comment-like constructs ('<!...>'), the function recursively calls itself without any depth limit. A malicious XML input with many consecutive PI or comment constructs can cause unbounded recursion, leading to stack exhaustion and a denial-of-service crash. The same applies to the recursive call for parsing sub-nodes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read by accessing curptr+1 without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple locations dereference *(scanner->curptr+1) without first verifying that the scanner has at least two characters remaining. If curptr points to the last valid character in the buffer (or to the end of the input), reading curptr+1 results in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker can craft an XML input that ends with '<' at the buffer boundary to trigger this, potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attribute parsing loop lacks EOF check, risking infinite loop or out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop that parses attributes checks for '>' and '/' but never checks for end-of-file/end-of-buffer. If the input is truncated (e.g., '<tag attr' with no closing '>'), the loop will continue reading beyond the buffer boundary or loop indefinitely, leading to a crash or denial of service.

commit_message: Fixed handling of empty JPEG-LS scans.
cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.

commit_message:
Add a check for pad width to be a positive value.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413275853
Change-Id: I261a8db9dabf5ce48a806a9e58129080c9fac619
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation from size_t/int64 to int bypasses validation, enabling out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple values derived from tensor sizes (size_t) or tensor elements (SPLITS_TYPE, which can be int64) are stored in plain `int` variables. If SPLITS_TYPE is int64, a crafted split value such as 0x100000000 would truncate to 0 when stored in `int prev_split`, passing the validation check (`prev_split == 0`), while the actual int64 value is used later for indexing (`input_data[splits_vec(i)]`), causing out-of-bounds memory reads. Similarly, `input_data_size` truncated from size_t to int means the bound check `splits_vec(i) <= input_data_size` can be bypassed. This can lead to heap buffer over-read/over-write and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in cumulative ngram count leads to undersized output buffer and heap overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cumulative sum `ngrams_splits_data[i] = ngrams_splits_data[i-1] + num_ngrams` is computed in a loop across all batch items. If the total number of ngrams overflows the SPLITS_TYPE (especially int32), the final value `ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items]` used to allocate the output tensor could be much smaller than the actual number of ngrams. The subsequent loop then writes ngrams into the undersized buffer via `ngrams_data[output_start_idx]`, resulting in a heap buffer overflow that could be exploited for arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded recursion via Processing Instructions or comments can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function handles PI ('<?...?>') and comment ('<!...>') constructs by recursively calling xml_parse_node. A malicious XML document with a very large number of consecutive PI or comment constructs will cause deep recursion without any depth limit, leading to stack exhaustion and a denial-of-service crash. Similarly, the sub-node parsing loop also recurses via xml_parse_node for nested elements with no depth bound.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read by accessing curptr+1 without checking for end of buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple locations dereference *(scanner->curptr+1) without first verifying that there is at least one more byte available in the scanner buffer. If the scanner input ends exactly at '<', reading curptr+1 accesses memory past the end of the buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker could craft truncated XML input to trigger this condition, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attribute parsing loop dereferences curptr without EOF check, risking null/OOB dereference on truncated input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The attribute-parsing while loop checks *scanner->curptr without first verifying that the scanner has not reached end-of-file. If the input is truncated (e.g., '<tag attr' with no closing '>'), the loop will read past the valid buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read or null-pointer dereference. An attacker can supply malformed XML to exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Infinite loop possible when PI or comment construct is never properly terminated
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the PI handling block, if the input contains '<?' but never '?>', pj_scan_get_until_ch will repeatedly fail or return empty results, and the loop may never break. Similarly the comment handling loop scanning for '>' can loop indefinitely on malformed input. This allows a denial-of-service attack with crafted unterminated constructs.

commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
pict,macrsrc: Fixed a bug that could cause division by 0

Found by F. Çelik.
cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow checks are ineffective due to mixed signed/unsigned comparisons, leading to potential heap buffer overflow.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The overflow checks compare `int` values (`integral`, `reslen`) with `size_t` values (`thousand_sep.size()`, `dec_point.size()`). Due to implicit promotion rules, the `int` operands are converted to `size_t` (unsigned), which means the comparisons effectively check for unsigned wraparound of `size_t`, not overflow of the `int` variables. When the result of the addition is assigned back to an `int` (e.g., `integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size()`), it can silently overflow the `int`, potentially producing a small or negative value. This causes `reslen` to be computed as too small, leading to an undersized buffer allocation via `String(reslen, ReserveString)`. Subsequent `memcpy` and pointer-based writes into `resbuf` then write out of bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control `dec` (to produce a very large number of decimal digits) and/or `thousand_sep` (a long separator string) could trigger this to corrupt heap memory.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB and OP_SUPER handlers
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the L_SENDB_SYM code path (handling OP_SEND, OP_SENDB, OP_SSEND, OP_SSENDB), the local variable 'blk' is declared but never assigned a value before being passed to prepare_missing() and later checked with mrb_proc_p(blk). The same issue exists in the OP_SUPER handler. Reading an uninitialized variable is undefined behavior in C. An attacker crafting malicious mruby bytecode that triggers method_missing or the block orphan check could cause the VM to operate on garbage stack data, potentially leading to memory corruption, information disclosure (if the uninitialized stack memory contains sensitive data), or code execution. The uninitialized value passed to prepare_missing() could cause it to misinterpret arguments, and mrb_proc_p() on garbage data could lead to dereferencing an invalid pointer via mrb_proc_ptr().

commit_message:
btrfs: fix NULL pointer dereference when deleting device by invalid id

[BUG]
It's easy to trigger NULL pointer dereference, just by removing a
non-existing device id:

 # mkfs.btrfs -f -m single -d single /dev/test/scratch1 \
				     /dev/test/scratch2
 # mount /dev/test/scratch1 /mnt/btrfs
 # btrfs device remove 3 /mnt/btrfs

Then we have the following kernel NULL pointer dereference:

 BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000000
 #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
 #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
 PGD 0 P4D 0
 Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
 CPU: 9 PID: 649 Comm: btrfs Not tainted 5.14.0-rc3-custom+ #35
 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 0.0.0 02/06/2015
 RIP: 0010:btrfs_rm_device+0x4de/0x6b0 [btrfs]
  btrfs_ioctl+0x18bb/0x3190 [btrfs]
  ? lock_is_held_type+0xa5/0x120
  ? find_held_lock.constprop.0+0x2b/0x80
  ? do_user_addr_fault+0x201/0x6a0
  ? lock_release+0xd2/0x2d0
  ? __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

[CAUSE]
Commit a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return
btrfs_device directly") moves the "missing" device path check into
btrfs_rm_device().

But btrfs_rm_device() itself can have case where it only receives
@devid, with NULL as @device_path.

In that case, calling strcmp() on NULL will trigger the NULL pointer
dereference.

Before that commit, we handle the "missing" case inside
btrfs_find_device_by_devspec(), which will not check @device_path at all
if @devid is provided, thus no way to trigger the bug.

[FIX]
Before calling strcmp(), also make sure @device_path is not NULL.

Fixes: a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return btrfs_device directly")
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.4+
Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <anand.jain@oracle.com>
Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
Reviewed-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
Signed-off-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message:
pict,macrsrc: Fixed a bug that could cause division by 0

Found by F. Çelik.
cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when bi->pixelsize is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: The field bi->pixelsize is read directly from untrusted file input via dbuf_getu16be. If the file contains a zero value for pixelsize, the division `(bi->rowbytes*8)/bi->pixelsize` triggers a division by zero, which on most platforms causes a crash (SIGFPE / undefined behavior). An attacker can craft a malicious file with pixelsize set to 0 to cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
for (int64_t i = 0; i < indices->dim_size(0); ++i) {
      int64_t next_batch_index = indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when indices matrix has zero columns
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `indices` is a matrix (rank 2) but does not verify that it has at least one column (i.e., `indices->dim_size(1) >= 1`). If an attacker provides an indices tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0, the loop iterates N times and accesses column 0 via `indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0)`, which is an out-of-bounds read. This can lead to reading uninitialized or adjacent memory, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when resource already exists
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the newly heap-allocated QuantileStreamResource pointed to by `result` is never freed. The code explicitly ignores the ALREADY_EXISTS error but does not call `delete result` or `result->Unref()` in that path. Each repeated call to this op with the same resource handle will leak a QuantileStreamResource object, which could be exploited to cause denial of service via memory exhaustion.

code_snippets:
auto result =
        new QuantileStreamResource(epsilon, max_elements_, num_streams);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of num_streams input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `num_streams` value is read from user-supplied input but is never validated before being passed to the QuantileStreamResource constructor. A zero, negative, or excessively large value could lead to undefined behavior, integer overflow during internal allocations, or denial of service depending on how QuantileStreamResource uses this parameter.

commit_message:
Fixes #4985: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299 (#4986)

* fix Division by zero in XMenuWidget() of MagickCore/widget.c

* Fix memory leak in AnimateImageCommand() of MagickWand/animate.c and DisplayImageCommand() of MagickWand/display.c

* fix Division by zero in ReadEnhMetaFile() of coders/emf.c

* Resolve conflicts

* fix issue: outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned char' at coders/psd.c:1025

* fix error: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299

Co-authored-by: zhailiangliang <zhailiangliang@loongson.cn>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

code_snippets:
(void) FormatLocaleString(command,MagickPathExtent,
    GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info),
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,density,options,
    read_info->filename,input_filename);
  ...
  status=ExternalDelegateCommand(MagickFalse,read_info->verbose,command,
    (char *) NULL,exception) != 0 ? MagickTrue : MagickFalse;
vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via delegate command construction and external execution
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function constructs a shell command by formatting a delegate command template (from GetDelegateCommands) with various parameters including filenames, density, and options strings, then executes it via ExternalDelegateCommand. While input_filename uses AcquireUniqueSymbolicLink and read_info->filename uses AcquireUniqueFilename to mitigate direct filename injection, the delegate command template itself comes from delegate configuration which could be influenced. Additionally, the command buffer is limited to MagickPathExtent; if the fully expanded command exceeds this, truncation could alter command semantics in a security-relevant way, potentially changing which program is executed or which arguments are passed. If an attacker can influence the delegate configuration (e.g., via crafted policy/delegate files), arbitrary command execution is possible.

code_snippets:
(void) FormatLocaleString(command,MagickPathExtent,
    GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info),
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,density,options,
    read_info->filename,input_filename);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Externally-controlled format string from delegate configuration
vulnerabilities.long_description: GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info) returns a format string that is used directly as the format argument to FormatLocaleString. The delegate commands are loaded from XML configuration files (delegates.xml). If an attacker can modify or influence these configuration files (e.g., through a local attack, a compromised config, or environment variable manipulation like MAGICK_CONFIGURE_PATH), they can inject arbitrary format specifiers. A mismatch between the expected format specifiers and the supplied arguments (6 arguments: two ints, two strings, two strings) could lead to reading or writing out-of-bounds stack data, potentially causing information disclosure or code execution.

commit_message:
btrfs: fix NULL pointer dereference when deleting device by invalid id

[BUG]
It's easy to trigger NULL pointer dereference, just by removing a
non-existing device id:

 # mkfs.btrfs -f -m single -d single /dev/test/scratch1 \
				     /dev/test/scratch2
 # mount /dev/test/scratch1 /mnt/btrfs
 # btrfs device remove 3 /mnt/btrfs

Then we have the following kernel NULL pointer dereference:

 BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000000
 #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
 #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
 PGD 0 P4D 0
 Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
 CPU: 9 PID: 649 Comm: btrfs Not tainted 5.14.0-rc3-custom+ #35
 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 0.0.0 02/06/2015
 RIP: 0010:btrfs_rm_device+0x4de/0x6b0 [btrfs]
  btrfs_ioctl+0x18bb/0x3190 [btrfs]
  ? lock_is_held_type+0xa5/0x120
  ? find_held_lock.constprop.0+0x2b/0x80
  ? do_user_addr_fault+0x201/0x6a0
  ? lock_release+0xd2/0x2d0
  ? __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

[CAUSE]
Commit a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return
btrfs_device directly") moves the "missing" device path check into
btrfs_rm_device().

But btrfs_rm_device() itself can have case where it only receives
@devid, with NULL as @device_path.

In that case, calling strcmp() on NULL will trigger the NULL pointer
dereference.

Before that commit, we handle the "missing" case inside
btrfs_find_device_by_devspec(), which will not check @device_path at all
if @devid is provided, thus no way to trigger the bug.

[FIX]
Before calling strcmp(), also make sure @device_path is not NULL.

Fixes: a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return btrfs_device directly")
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.4+
Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <anand.jain@oracle.com>
Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
Reviewed-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>

cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition when uuid_mutex is released and reacquired around btrfs_shrink_device
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function unlocks uuid_mutex before calling btrfs_shrink_device and re-locks it afterward. During this window, the device pointer (obtained while holding the mutex) could become stale if another thread concurrently modifies the device list. The device's state (e.g., BTRFS_DEV_STATE_WRITEABLE) was checked before the unlock and is relied upon after re-acquiring the lock (in error_undo path), but it could have changed. This could lead to use-after-free or inconsistent state manipulation, though exploitation depends on concurrent filesystem operations.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation due to mixed signed/unsigned arithmetic
vulnerabilities.long_description: The overflow checks compare `int` values with `size_t` values. When `integral` (an `int`) is implicitly promoted to `size_t` (unsigned) for the comparison, the check tests for overflow in the `size_t` domain. However, the result is then assigned back to `int` variables (`integral` and `reslen`). If the computed value exceeds INT_MAX but does not overflow `size_t`, the check passes but the assignment to `int` causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior), potentially resulting in a small or negative value. This leads to an undersized buffer allocation via `String(reslen, ReserveString)`, and the subsequent memcpy and pointer write operations will write out of bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker controlling `d` (large number with many digits), `dec` (large decimal precision), or `thousand_sep` (multi-byte separator string) can trigger this to corrupt heap memory.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message: Fixed handling of empty JPEG-LS scans.
cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to assert-only bounds check on m_ucCount
vulnerabilities.long_description: The arrays `line[4]`, `lp[4]`, `a[4]`, `b[4]`, `c[4]`, `d[4]`, `d1[4]`, `d2[4]`, `d3[4]` are all fixed-size arrays of 4 elements, indexed by `cx` which iterates up to `m_ucCount`. The only check that `m_ucCount < 4` is via `assert()`, which is compiled out in release/NDEBUG builds. If `m_ucCount` is 4 or greater (due to a malformed input or unexpected state), all these stack-based arrays will be written out of bounds, leading to stack buffer overflows. An attacker could craft input that sets `m_ucCount >= 4` to corrupt stack memory, potentially achieving code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write if DecodeRun returns a value larger than the line buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `run` value is decoded from the bitstream via `DecodeRun`. While `length` is passed as a parameter, there is no explicit validation that the returned `run` value is bounded by the remaining buffer space pointed to by `lp[cx]`. If a malformed bitstream causes `DecodeRun` to return a value that exceeds the actual allocated line buffer size, the `*lp[cx]++ = ...` writes will go past the buffer boundary. The `length` variable tracks the logical line width but the actual allocated buffer size may differ, and `length` itself can go negative (as a signed LONG) since it's decremented in the run loop without a lower-bound check before the `--length` in the outer do-while loop.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/5034
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, there is load of misaligned address for type 'double', which requires 8 byte alignment and for type 'float', which requires 4 byte alignment at MagickCore/property.c. Whenever crafted or untrusted input is processed by ImageMagick, this causes a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in number_bytes calculation can lead to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of 'components' (up to INT32_MAX, ~2^31) by 'tag_bytes[format]' (up to 8) can overflow on 32-bit platforms where size_t is 32 bits. The result is stored in ssize_t number_bytes. The overflow check 'number_bytes < components' compares signed values and may not catch all overflow cases. If number_bytes wraps to a small value, subsequent bounds checks (e.g., 'dir_offset+number_bytes > length') pass incorrectly, allowing the EXIFMultipleValues/EXIFMultipleFractions macros to read far beyond the allocated EXIF buffer based on the original large 'components' count. This could lead to information disclosure or crashes.

code_snippets:
p1=p; \
   for (component=0; component < components; component++) \
   { \
     length+=FormatLocaleString(buffer+length,MaxTextExtent-length, \
       format", ",arg); \
     if (length >= (MaxTextExtent-1)) \
       length=MaxTextExtent-1; \
     p1+=size; \
   }
vulnerabilities.short_description: EXIFMultipleValues/EXIFMultipleFractions macros lack per-iteration bounds checking on p1
vulnerabilities.long_description: The EXIFMultipleValues and EXIFMultipleFractions macros iterate 'components' times, advancing pointer p1 by 'size' bytes each iteration and reading data at p1. The bounds check before the switch statement only validates that p points to at least one element of tag_bytes[format] size within the buffer. While there is an earlier validation of the total number_bytes range for the offset case, if the integer overflow in number_bytes is exploited, these macros will read well beyond the valid EXIF data buffer. Even without overflow, if number_bytes <= 4 (inline case) the check relies on the directory entry having sufficient space, but format-specific edge cases with small element sizes and multiple components could potentially read adjacent memory.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU: stale `length` used after side-effect-capable `njs_value_to_integer` calls can cause out-of-bounds array access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function fetches the object's `length` early, then calls `njs_value_to_integer` on user-supplied arguments (args[1] and args[2]). These calls can trigger user-defined valueOf/toString callbacks that modify or shrink the underlying array. However, the previously-fetched `length` is still used to compute `start` and `delete`, and these stale values drive the subsequent fast-array memory operations (direct reads via `array->start[n]`, memcpy of new items, and `njs_array_copy_within`). If a callback shrinks the array, `start + delete` can exceed the actual allocated size of `array->start`, leading to an out-of-bounds read when copying deleted elements into the new array, and an out-of-bounds write when relocating elements or copying new items via `memcpy(&array->start[start], &args[3], items * sizeof(njs_value_t))`. An attacker who can control the splice arguments with exotic objects could exploit this to read or corrupt heap memory.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized variable 'blk' used in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB method dispatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the L_SENDB_SYM handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never assigned a value before being passed to prepare_missing() and later checked with mrb_proc_p(). Since 'blk' contains whatever stack garbage is present, this can cause prepare_missing to operate on an arbitrary mrb_value. Additionally, the later mrb_proc_p(blk) check may interpret stack garbage as a valid proc object, leading to dereferencing an invalid pointer when accessing mrb_proc_ptr(blk). This could lead to information disclosure, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution depending on stack contents.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized variable 'blk' used in OP_SUPER method dispatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to prepare_missing() when the method is not found. The uninitialized mrb_value will contain whatever data happened to be on the stack, which is then interpreted as a block argument. This could result in a crash or memory corruption if the garbage value is treated as a valid object pointer by prepare_missing or subsequent code.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when reading string into fixed-size buffer without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads bytes one at a time from the bitstream into a stack-allocated buffer `str[1024]` without checking if `i` exceeds 1023. A crafted input with more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator will overflow the buffer, allowing an attacker to overwrite adjacent stack memory including the return address, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when indices matrix has zero columns
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `indices` is a matrix (rank 2), but never checks that it has at least one column (i.e., `indices->dim_size(1) >= 1`). If an attacker provides an Nx0 indices matrix (where N > 0), the loop iterates `N` times and accesses column 0, which does not exist, resulting in an out-of-bounds memory read. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that number of index rows matches number of values when both are non-zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code only checks consistency between indices and values when one of them is empty. When both are non-empty, there is no check that `indices->dim_size(0) == values->dim_size(0)`. Furthermore, `num_indices` is the total element count of the indices matrix (rows * cols), not the number of rows, so even the empty check is semantically wrong — for example, a 3x2 indices matrix has 6 elements that would be compared against 6 values, even though the sparse tensor only has 3 entries. This mismatch can lead to out-of-bounds accesses or undefined behavior in downstream SparseTensor operations.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when copying type and class fields without bounds checking against max
vulnerabilities.long_description: After advancing the pointer past the name (p = start + name_part_len), the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) via pj_memcpy without verifying that p + 4 <= max. If the packet is truncated such that the name consumes all or nearly all remaining bytes, these reads will access memory beyond the packet buffer. An attacker can craft a DNS packet where the name fills the remaining buffer, causing a heap or stack buffer over-read, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

commit_message: Fixed handling of empty JPEG-LS scans.
cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow if DecodeRun returns a run length larger than remaining line length
vulnerabilities.long_description: DecodeRun decodes a run length from the bitstream, which could be attacker-controlled. If the decoded `run` value exceeds the current `length`, the inner `while(run)` loop will decrement `length` past zero (making it negative). The subsequent `if (length)` check is true for negative values, causing additional out-of-bounds writes via `*lp[cx]++`. Furthermore, the outer `do...while(--length)` loop would then wrap the negative value and continue for a very large number of iterations, causing massive out-of-bounds heap writes on the line data buffers. A crafted JPEG-LS stream could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Array bounds check relies on assert which is disabled in release builds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The fixed-size arrays `line[4]`, `a[4]`, `b[4]`, `c[4]`, `d[4]`, `d1[4]`, `d2[4]`, `d3[4]`, and `lp[4]` are all indexed by `cx` up to `m_ucCount`. The only check that `m_ucCount < 4` is via `assert()`, which is typically compiled out in release/production builds (when NDEBUG is defined). If `m_ucCount` is 4 or greater, all these arrays will be accessed out of bounds, leading to stack-based buffer overflows and potential code execution.

commit_message:
crypto: digest: use crypto_memneq()

When verifying a digest it is important not to leak timing information
through memcmp(). Use crypto_memneq() instead.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: crypto/digest.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because memcmp is used during digest verification.

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Wrong variable used in root_ssix cleanup: deletes seg_ssix instead of root_ssix, causing use-after-free and memory leak
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the MOOF case cleanup section, when `mov->root_ssix` is non-NULL, the code mistakenly calls `gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix)` instead of `gf_isom_box_del((GF_Box *) mov->root_ssix)`. This frees `seg_ssix` (which may have already been freed or may be used later) while leaking the memory of `root_ssix`. Then `mov->root_ssix` is set to NULL but `mov->seg_ssix` is not, leaving a dangling pointer in `mov->seg_ssix` that could be dereferenced later leading to use-after-free. Additionally, note that `mov->seg_ssix` is also freed a few lines below (`gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_styp)` is unrelated, but `seg_ssix` was already freed), potentially leading to a double-free depending on code flow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when accessing trak->Media->information->sampleTable chain without null checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the MOOV handler's non-debug path, the code iterates over tracks and dereferences `trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups` without checking if `trak->Media`, `trak->Media->information`, or `trak->Media->information->sampleTable` are non-NULL. A maliciously crafted file could have a track box that is missing one of these sub-boxes, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crashing the application. This could be exploited for denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized variable totSize when GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS is defined
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `totSize` is declared but only initialized inside the `#ifndef GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS` preprocessor block. When `GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS` is defined, `totSize` is used uninitialized in multiple `totSize += a->size` statements throughout the function. While `totSize` does not appear to be used after the loop in a security-critical way, reading an uninitialized variable is undefined behavior in C and could lead to unpredictable results.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

code_snippets:
auto loc = std::inner_product(g_truth.begin(), g_truth.end(),
                                      output_strides.begin(), int64_t{0});
        OP_REQUIRES(
            ctx, loc < output_elements,
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write due to missing check for negative loc values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The computed `loc` value (from `std::inner_product` over group indices and output strides) is only checked against `output_elements` as an upper bound (`loc < output_elements`), but there is no check that `loc >= 0`. If an attacker provides sparse tensor indices with negative values, the inner product could yield a negative `loc`, which would pass the bounds check but result in an out-of-bounds memory write when used to index `output_t(loc)`. This could lead to heap corruption, denial of service, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in malloc size calculation when s->size is maximal
vulnerabilities.long_description: If s->size is the maximum value for its type (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF for u64), the expression (s->size+1) wraps around to 0. gf_malloc(0) may return a small or non-NULL pointer, after which gf_bs_read_data writes s->size bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious box with a large size field to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when php_base64_decode fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: php_base64_decode can return NULL if the base64 decoding fails (e.g., on invalid input). The return value is directly passed to ZVAL_STR without any NULL check. This results in a NULL pointer dereference when the zval is subsequently accessed, potentially leading to a crash (denial of service). An attacker can trigger this by sending a WDDX packet with an invalid base64 binary element.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsafe deserialization allows instantiation of arbitrary PHP classes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function instantiates arbitrary PHP classes based on a user-controlled class name from the WDDX input (the PHP_CLASS_NAME_VAR field). An attacker can specify any class name available in the class table, causing object_init_ex to be called with that class. Combined with the __wakeup() call, this allows an attacker to trigger magic methods on arbitrary objects, potentially leading to remote code execution, file manipulation, or other attacks depending on the classes available in the application (POP chain gadgets).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential use-after-free due to freeing stack entry without nullifying the stack slot
vulnerabilities.long_description: When ent1->data is IS_UNDEF, the code frees ent1 with efree() and decrements stack->top. However, the stack slot still contains the pointer to the freed memory. If subsequent parsing operations cause wddx_stack_top to return this same memory region (e.g., if stack->top is manipulated back), the freed memory could be accessed, leading to a use-after-free condition. This could potentially be exploited for code execution or information disclosure by carefully crafting malicious WDDX input that manipulates the stack state.

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Mismatched variable check/free leads to use-after-free on seg_ssix and memory leak on root_ssix
vulnerabilities.long_description: After processing a MOOF box, the code checks `mov->root_ssix` but frees `mov->seg_ssix`, then sets `mov->root_ssix = NULL`. This has two consequences: (1) `mov->seg_ssix` is freed but its pointer is never set to NULL, leaving a dangling pointer that can be used-after-free later when the code accesses `mov->seg_ssix` (e.g., in `gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix)` during SIDX/SSIX processing). (2) `mov->root_ssix` is never actually freed, causing a memory leak. The likely intended code was either to check `mov->seg_ssix` instead of `mov->root_ssix`, or to free `mov->root_ssix` and set it to NULL. An attacker could craft a malicious MP4 file with specific fragment box ordering to trigger the use-after-free, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when accessing track sub-structures without null checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the non-debug MOOV processing path, the code iterates over tracks and accesses `trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups` without verifying that `trak`, `trak->Media`, `trak->Media->information`, or `trak->Media->information->sampleTable` are non-NULL. A malformed MP4 file with a track box missing its Media, MediaInformation, or SampleTable sub-boxes would cause a NULL pointer dereference, leading to a crash (denial of service). An attacker can craft a malicious file that includes a MOOV box with incomplete track structures to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when accessing mfhd in MOOF without null check
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing a MOOF box in OPEN_KEEP_FRAGMENTS mode, the code accesses `mov->moof->mfhd->sequence_number` without checking if `mfhd` is non-NULL. A malformed fragmented MP4 file with a MOOF box that lacks an MFHD sub-box would cause a NULL pointer dereference, resulting in a crash. An attacker could create a crafted MP4 with a MOOF box missing its mandatory MFHD child to trigger this denial of service.

commit_message:
Make SparseFillEmptyRows validate that the length of `values` must be equal to the number of index tuples.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399969549
Change-Id: I3c2f2ca1c1d2cc88bb5951c6958b38c16e9436c8
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `SparseFillEmptyRows` can be made to trigger a heap OOB access. This occurs whenever the size of `indices` does not match the size of `values`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

Add remaining validation to `sdca_internal.cc`

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/4986
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

code_snippets:
(void) FormatLocaleString(command,MaxTextExtent,
    GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info),
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,density,options,
    read_info->filename,input_filename);
  ...
  status=ExternalDelegateCommand(MagickFalse,read_info->verbose,command,
    (char *) NULL,exception) != 0 ? MagickTrue : MagickFalse;
vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via crafted filenames passed to external delegate command
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function constructs a shell command string by formatting user-influenced values (input_filename derived from image_info->filename, density, options) into a delegate command template, then passes it to ExternalDelegateCommand for execution. If the original filename contains shell metacharacters (e.g., backticks, semicolons, pipes), these could be interpreted by the shell, allowing arbitrary command execution. The input_filename is created via AcquireUniqueSymbolicLink from the user-supplied image_info->filename, which may preserve or reference the original path containing malicious characters. An attacker could craft a filename like `image;id;.pcl` to inject arbitrary commands.

code_snippets:
(void) FormatLocaleString(command,MaxTextExtent,
    GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info),
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,
    read_info->antialias != MagickFalse ? 4 : 1,density,options,
    read_info->filename,input_filename);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Format string derived from external delegate configuration used with user-controlled arguments
vulnerabilities.long_description: GetDelegateCommands(delegate_info) returns a format string from the delegates configuration, which is then used as the format specifier in FormatLocaleString with user-influenced arguments. If the delegate configuration is tampered with or contains unexpected format specifiers, or if the arguments (like density, options, filenames) contain format string characters, this could lead to a format string vulnerability causing crashes, information disclosure, or code execution. The format string itself comes from configuration rather than being hardcoded, adding an additional trust boundary concern.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero when N is zero in updates reshaping
vulnerabilities.long_description: Although the outer `if (N > 0)` guard should prevent N from being zero at this point, the value of N is derived from `indices.NumElements()` cast to `Index` type. If `Index` is a signed type and `N_big` equals `std::numeric_limits<Index>::max() + 1` (due to the `<=` check rather than `<`), the cast could produce zero or a negative value depending on overflow behavior, potentially leading to division by zero in `num_updates / N`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one in bounds check allows integer overflow when casting to Index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check uses `<=` instead of `<` when comparing N_big against `std::numeric_limits<Index>::max()`. If Index is a signed integer type (e.g., int32), N_big could equal 2^31 which, when cast to a signed 32-bit integer, overflows to a negative value or INT_MIN. This corrupted value of N then flows into scatter operations, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access. The same issue applies to the `params->dim_size(0)` check.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed-to-unsigned conversion of user-controlled index allows out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The input scalar is read as a signed `int` and then implicitly converted to `std::size_t` (unsigned). If a user provides a negative integer, the cast wraps it to a very large unsigned value. This large value is then passed to `buf->Peek(index, &tuple)`. Depending on how `Peek` validates the index, this could lead to an out-of-bounds read, information disclosure, or a crash. An attacker who controls the input tensor can supply a negative value to trigger this behavior.

commit_message:
Add remaining validation to `sdca_internal.cc`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387738010
Change-Id: I28eedcfd87a53aaf34deb075acea1f8c95470808
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can read from outside of bounds of heap allocated data by sending specially crafted illegal arguments to `tf.raw_ops.SdcaOptimizerV2`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/sdca_internal.cc#L320-L353) does not check that the length of `example_labels` is the same as the number of examples. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a4e138660270e7599793fa438cd7b2fc2ce215a6. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU: array length used for bounds may be stale after argument coercion side effects
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function retrieves the array length via njs_object_length() and then calls njs_value_to_integer() on user-supplied arguments to compute 'start' and 'delete'. These njs_value_to_integer() calls can invoke user-defined valueOf/toString callbacks which may modify the underlying array (e.g., shrink it by popping elements). The computed 'start' and 'delete' values are bounded against the original (now stale) length. When the code later enters the fast array path, it accesses array->start[n] for n up to start+delete-1 without re-checking the current array->length. This can result in out-of-bounds reads when copying to the deleted array, and out-of-bounds writes via the memcpy that copies new items into the array. An attacker could craft an object with a valueOf that shrinks the array, causing memory corruption or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Prevent segfault in `GetSessionHandle{,V2}`.

In eager mode, session state is null.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 332548597
Change-Id: If094812c2e094044220b9ba28f7d7601be042f38
cve_desc: In eager mode, TensorFlow before versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1 and 2.3.1 does not set the session state. Hence, calling `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandle` or `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandleV2` results in a null pointer dereference In linked snippet, in eager mode, `ctx->session_state()` returns `nullptr`. Since code immediately dereferences this, we get a segmentation fault. The issue is patched in commit 9a133d73ae4b4664d22bd1aa6d654fec13c52ee1, and is released in TensorFlow versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1, or 2.3.1.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when php_base64_decode fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: php_base64_decode can return NULL if the input is not valid base64. The return value is used directly in ZVAL_STR without a NULL check. This sets a NULL zend_string pointer in the zval, which will cause a crash (NULL pointer dereference) when the zval is subsequently accessed. An attacker can trigger this by providing malformed base64 data in a WDDX binary element.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary object instantiation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The class name used in object_init_ex is taken directly from attacker-controlled XML input (the PHP_CLASS_NAME_VAR field). This allows an attacker to instantiate arbitrary PHP classes. Combined with the subsequent __wakeup() call and property injection via zend_hash_merge, this can lead to property-oriented programming (POP chain) attacks, potentially resulting in arbitrary code execution, file operations, or other dangerous side effects depending on the classes available in the application.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory management issue in EL_FIELD handling: entry freed without cleaning up its contents
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling the closing EL_FIELD tag, the code frees the st_entry with efree() but does not call zval_ptr_dtor on ent->data or free ent->varname. This leads to memory leaks of the zval data and varname string. Additionally, the entry is freed but the stack slot may still reference freed memory if accessed before being overwritten, which could lead to use-after-free conditions depending on how the stack is implemented.

commit_message:
Prevent a division by 0 in division ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385223169
Change-Id: Ia4228960b5d2aa44480385f74bdd70d21a3613c3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of division in TFLite is [vulnerable to a division by 0 error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/div.cc). There is no check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 1e206baedf8bef0334cca3eb92bab134ef525a28. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
