commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized pointer `dequeue_node` when no dequeue op is found
vulnerabilities.long_description: The local variable `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If no node in `train_nodes` satisfies `IsDequeueOp`, the pointer remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads an indeterminate value, which is undefined behavior. Even if that check happens to be skipped, the later loop `input_node->name() != dequeue_node->name()` unconditionally dereferences the uninitialized pointer, leading to a crash or potential code execution. An attacker who can influence the graph (e.g., by providing a graph with no dequeue ops) can trigger this.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables `rowbytes` and `imagesize` are `unsigned int`. The multiplication `w * 4` or `h * rowbytes` can overflow, wrapping around to a small value. This causes a small heap buffer to be allocated via `new unsigned char[imagesize]`, while the PNG decoder later writes data based on the actual dimensions `w` and `h`, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. The check against `cMaxPNGSize` may not prevent this if `cMaxPNGSize` is large enough (e.g., >= 2^30 for w*4 overflow).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when delay_den is zero in fcTL chunk
vulnerabilities.long_description: `delay_den` is read directly from the fcTL chunk data. A crafted APNG file can set `delay_den` to 0, which leads to a division by zero in `delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`. The APNG specification states that a value of 0 should be treated as 100, but this code does not implement that check, resulting in undefined behavior / crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in fdAT chunk size calculation leads to out-of-bounds read/write
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing fdAT chunks, the code computes `chunk.size - 16` and `chunk.size - 4`. Since `chunk.size` is an `unsigned int`, if a crafted fdAT chunk has a size smaller than 16, `chunk.size - 16` underflows to a very large value. This corrupted size is written via `png_save_uint_32` and then a very large length is passed to `processing_data`, which can cause out-of-bounds memory reads when the PNG library processes the data.

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when dir->length is 0 but info->name.length is non-zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: When dir is not NULL but dir->length is 0, and info->name.length is greater than 0, the early return check (length == 0) is not triggered because length equals info->name.length which is non-zero. Execution then reaches `dir->start[dir->length - 1]` which evaluates to `dir->start[(size_t)-1]`, an extremely large index, causing an out-of-bounds memory read. An attacker who can control the dir parameter (e.g., through a configuration that sets an empty module path) could trigger this to read arbitrary memory or crash the process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition between realpath() and open()
vulnerabilities.long_description: The resolved path from realpath() is subsequently used in open(). Between these two calls, an attacker could manipulate the filesystem (e.g., replacing the resolved file or a symlink in the path) to point to a different file. This race condition could allow an attacker to trick the application into opening an unintended file, potentially leading to information disclosure or loading of a malicious module.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Copy-paste bug validates wrong tensor for requested_output_max, allowing unchecked input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The validation intended for `input_requested_max` mistakenly checks `input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1` instead of `input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1`. This means `input_requested_max` is never validated for being a scalar. If an attacker provides a non-scalar tensor for `input_requested_max`, the subsequent `input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0)` access may read from an unexpected tensor shape, and the lack of validation could lead to further unexpected behavior or crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when both requested min and max are zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: If both `input_requested_min_float` and `input_requested_max_float` are 0.0, then `requested_min_max` will be 0.0, causing a floating-point division by zero when computing `scales[i]`. While floating-point division by zero in IEEE 754 produces infinity rather than crashing, this results in corrupted scale values (infinity or NaN) being passed to the MKL-DNN reorder primitive, which could cause undefined behavior, crashes, or incorrect output data.

code_snippets:
const Tensor& input_min_vec = ctx->input(kInputMinVecIndex);
      size_t depth = input_min_vec.NumElements();
vulnerabilities.short_description: Depth derived from input_min_vec is not validated against the input tensor's channel dimension
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `depth` variable is derived from `input_min_vec.NumElements()` but is never validated against `input.dim_size(3)` (the channel/depth dimension of the 4D input tensor). The MKL-DNN per-channel reorder uses this `scales` vector whose size is `depth`. If `depth` does not match the actual channel dimension of the input, the reorder operation may read or write out of bounds, potentially causing memory corruption or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables `rowbytes` and `imagesize` are `unsigned int` (32-bit). The computation `w * 4` and `h * rowbytes` can overflow a 32-bit unsigned integer when `w` and `h` are large (but still under `cMaxPNGSize`). For example, if `w = 100000` and `h = 100000`, `rowbytes = 400000` and `imagesize = 100000 * 400000 = 40,000,000,000` which wraps around in a 32-bit unsigned int. This results in a much smaller heap allocation than intended, while libpng will write pixel data assuming the full image dimensions, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious APNG file with large dimensions to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect and potentially overflowing allocation size for row pointers array
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation `new png_bytep[h * sizeof(png_bytep)]` multiplies the element count `h` by `sizeof(png_bytep)` (typically 8 on 64-bit), but `new T[n]` already accounts for element size. This means it allocates `h * sizeof(png_bytep)` pointers instead of just `h` pointers. While this over-allocates on most platforms, on a 32-bit system where `sizeof(png_bytep) = 4`, the multiplication `h * 4` (computed as `unsigned int`) could overflow, resulting in an under-sized allocation. Subsequent writes to `frameRaw.rows[j]` for `j < h` would then write out of bounds, causing heap corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pixel count accumulation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `w0 * h0` is computed using `unsigned int` arithmetic and can overflow for large frame dimensions. Additionally, repeated accumulation via `+=` across many frames can cause `dec_pixels` to wrap around or be inaccurate, potentially bypassing any downstream pixel budget checks designed to prevent denial of service through excessive memory consumption.

commit_message:
Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387664237
Change-Id: Ia1700c34b5610873d63561abc86e23b46ead93b3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause undefined behavior via binding a reference to null pointer in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit be7a4de6adfbd303ce08be4332554dff70362612. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-vhxv-phmx-g52q

* Prevent OOB read/write when parsing RTCP FB RPSI

* Add log information

* Modification based on comments.
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when hdr->length is less than 2, leading to a massive rpsi_len value
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the attacker-controlled hdr->length field (after byte-swap) is 0 or 1, the subtraction `(pj_ntohs(hdr->length) - 2)` underflows because rpsi_len is pj_size_t (unsigned). This produces a very large value. Furthermore, `rpsi_len + 12` can then also wrap around to a small value, bypassing the subsequent bounds check, and leading to out-of-bounds memory access when `p` is used.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled padlen can cause unsigned underflow of rpsi_bit_len, resulting in out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The padlen byte is read directly from the packet with no validation. If padlen exceeds `rpsi_len*8 - 16`, the computation of rpsi->rpsi_bit_len underflows (assuming an unsigned type), producing an extremely large value. This large value is then used to compute the string length in pj_strset as `(rpsi->rpsi_bit_len + 7)/8`, causing the resulting string to reference memory far beyond the actual buffer. Any subsequent consumer of rpsi->rpsi will read out-of-bounds memory, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when num_inputs is 0 or only 1 (short-circuit failure path) due to unchecked index access on input_label_counts.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `num_inputs` is 0, the expression `num_inputs == 1` evaluates to false, causing evaluation to proceed to `(*input_label_counts)[0][label]`, which is an out-of-bounds access on an empty vector. Even if `num_inputs` is guaranteed to be at least 1, when `num_inputs == 1` the short-circuit evaluation prevents index 1 access, which is correct. However, if `ParseEinsumEquation` ever returns an empty `input_str` (0 inputs), or if the equation format changes, this code will read out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to a crash or information disclosure. Additionally, when `num_inputs > 2`, only inputs at indices 0 and 1 are checked, which is a logic error that could produce incorrect dimension type classification.

commit_message:
Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387664237
Change-Id: Ia1700c34b5610873d63561abc86e23b46ead93b3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause undefined behavior via binding a reference to null pointer in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit be7a4de6adfbd303ce08be4332554dff70362612. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286
Change-Id: If2073b36d4d5eedd386329f56729395fd7effee1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #2212
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to 2.1-DEV.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Untrusted bitstream value controls allocation size and loop iterations without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `NbBits` is read as a 5-bit value (0-31) from the bitstream, then `nbFields` is read using `NbBits` bits, allowing values up to 2^31-1. This attacker-controlled value is used directly as the count for `gf_sg_vrml_mf_alloc` (which allocates memory proportional to `nbFields`) and as the loop bound for subsequent iterations. A crafted bitstream can specify an extremely large `nbFields` value, causing either a massive memory allocation (denial of service / out-of-memory) or an excessive number of loop iterations processing nodes from the bitstream. There is no upper-bound validation on `nbFields` before it is used.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: When axis_ is 0, `axis_ - 1` evaluates to -1, causing an invalid argument to flat_inner_outer_dims.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The validation allows axis_ to be 0 (the condition `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` permits it). When axis_ is 0 and not -1, execution enters the else branch where `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1)` is called with the value -1. This negative index is not a valid split dimension for `flat_inner_outer_dims`, which can lead to a CHECK failure (denial of service crash) or potentially undefined behavior depending on the TensorFlow build. An attacker can trigger this by providing an op with axis=0 and an input tensor with at least one dimension.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing check for negative permutation indices allows out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `in_idx >= rank` but does not check for negative values of `in_idx`. Since `in_idx` is an `int64_t` derived from user-controlled tensor data, a negative value would bypass the bounds check and be passed to `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`, potentially causing an out-of-bounds read or other undefined behavior depending on the implementation of `Dim()`. An attacker could craft a permutation tensor with negative values to trigger this.

commit_message: fixed #2194
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free via dangling pointers after protobuf repeated field reallocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code first populates `all_nodes_` with raw `NodeDef*` pointers obtained from `graph_.mutable_node(i)`. Subsequently, `AddNodeDivConst()` and `AddNodeDiv()` presumably add new nodes to `graph_` (e.g., via `graph_.add_node()`). Adding elements to a protobuf `RepeatedPtrField` can cause reallocation of the underlying storage, invalidating all previously obtained pointers. The code then dereferences these potentially dangling pointers when it accesses `all_nodes_[apply_gradient_node_name]` and calls methods like `->op()`, `->input()`, and `->mutable_input()` on them. This constitutes a use-after-free condition that can lead to crashes, data corruption, or potentially arbitrary code execution if an attacker can influence the graph structure.

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in offset bounds check allows out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The offset values come from the file (untrusted input) and are of type tinyexr_uint64. When a very large offset value (e.g., close to UINT64_MAX) is added to sizeof(int)*5 or sizeof(int)*2, the addition wraps around to a small value, bypassing the size check. Subsequently, `head + offsets[y_idx]` produces an out-of-bounds pointer, and memcpy reads from arbitrary memory. An attacker can craft a malicious EXR file with specially chosen offset values to trigger this, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

code_snippets: bool invalid_data = false;  // TODO(LTE): Use atomic lock for MT safety.
vulnerabilities.short_description: Data race on shared `invalid_data` flag in OpenMP parallel region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `invalid_data` boolean is shared across all OpenMP threads without any synchronization (no atomic, no critical section, no lock). Multiple threads may simultaneously read and write this variable, constituting a data race which is undefined behavior per the C++ standard. In practice, this could cause error conditions to be silently missed, allowing processing to continue with corrupted or invalid data, potentially leading to further memory corruption downstream.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Overflow check for total_data_len is insufficient on 32-bit platforms
vulnerabilities.long_description: On 32-bit platforms (where size_t is 32 bits), the multiplication `size_t(data_width) * size_t(data_height) * size_t(num_channels)` can silently overflow and wrap around to a small value. The overflow guard `total_data_len_overflown` is explicitly set to `false` on non-64-bit platforms, so a wrapped-around small but non-zero value passes all checks. This leads to undersized allocations in AllocateImage followed by out-of-bounds writes during pixel decoding, enabling heap buffer overflow attacks via crafted EXR files.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Indices are checked for negativity but not for upper-bound validity, allowing out-of-bounds reads.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function validates that all index values are non-negative, but never checks that the index values are within the valid range of the corresponding dimensions of the `params` tensor. An attacker can craft indices that are positive but exceed the dimension sizes of `params`, causing the downstream `GatherNd` calls to perform out-of-bounds memory reads. This can lead to information disclosure or crashes. The fix should add upper-bound validation for each index value against the corresponding dimension size of `params`.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: When axis_ is 0, `flat_inner_outer_dims` is called with -1, causing undefined behavior or crash.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The validation allows `axis_` to be 0 (the check is `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()`). When `axis_` is 0 and not -1, the else branch executes and calls `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1)` which evaluates to `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(-1)`. Passing a negative value to `flat_inner_outer_dims` leads to undefined behavior, potentially causing a CHECK failure (denial of service) or out-of-bounds memory access. An attacker can trigger this by providing `axis=0` to the operation.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(
          ctx, input_min_tensor.dim_size(0) == depth,
          errors::InvalidArgument("min has incorrect size, expected ", depth,
                                  " was ", input_min_tensor.dim_size(0)));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Calling dim_size(0) on a scalar (0-dimensional) tensor causes out-of-bounds access.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `input_min_tensor.dims() == 0 || input_min_tensor.dims() == 1`, allowing scalar tensors. However, when `axis_ != -1`, the code unconditionally calls `input_min_tensor.dim_size(0)` and `input_max_tensor.dim_size(0)`. For a scalar tensor (dims() == 0), calling `dim_size(0)` is an out-of-bounds access on the shape's dimension array. An attacker can craft inputs where `axis_ > 0` and `input_min_tensor` is a scalar to trigger this issue, leading to a crash or information disclosure.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on input_label_counts when num_inputs is 0 or greater than 2
vulnerabilities.long_description: When num_inputs is 0, the short-circuit evaluation of `num_inputs == 1` is false, causing `(*input_label_counts)[0][label]` to be accessed on an empty vector, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, the code unconditionally accesses index 1 of input_label_counts without verifying that num_inputs >= 2 (only checking num_inputs != 1). If num_inputs is 0, both accesses at index 0 and 1 are out of bounds. Since num_inputs is derived from parsing user-controlled equation strings, an attacker could craft an equation with zero inputs (e.g., just an output part) to trigger a heap out-of-bounds read, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-vhxv-phmx-g52q

* Prevent OOB read/write when parsing RTCP FB RPSI

* Add log information

* Modification based on comments.
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on calloc return value before dereferencing exr_image->tiles
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calloc call for exr_image->tiles does not have its return value checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to a large num_tiles value derived from attacker-controlled offsets.size()), the subsequent loop immediately dereferences exr_image->tiles[tile_idx].images, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. An attacker can craft a malicious EXR file with a large number of tile offsets to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No upper-bound validation on line_no allows out-of-bounds write in DecodePixelData
vulnerabilities.long_description: After adjusting line_no by subtracting data_window[1], only a lower-bound check (line_no < 0) is performed. There is no check that line_no + num_lines <= data_height. Since line_no comes directly from attacker-controlled file data, a crafted EXR file can supply a large line_no that passes the >= 0 check but causes DecodePixelData to write pixel data past the allocated image buffer boundaries, leading to heap buffer overflow and potentially arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets: bool invalid_data = false;  // TODO(LTE): Use atomic lock for MT safety.
vulnerabilities.short_description: Data race on invalid_data flag when OpenMP parallelism is enabled
vulnerabilities.long_description: The boolean flag 'invalid_data' is read and written by multiple threads in the OpenMP parallel for loop without any synchronization or atomic operations. This is a data race (undefined behavior in C++). In practice, a thread may detect invalid data and set the flag, but other threads may not see the update and continue processing with corrupt or malicious data, potentially leading to further memory corruption. The code even acknowledges this with a TODO comment.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in offset bounds check on 32-bit platforms
vulnerabilities.long_description: The offsets are stored as tinyexr_uint64 (64-bit), but size is size_t, which is 32-bit on 32-bit platforms. The addition offsets[idx] + sizeof(int)*N is performed in 64-bit, and compared against a 32-bit size promoted to 64-bit. However, a crafted offset value could be smaller than size yet still point past the actual buffer when added to the head pointer (which is limited to 32-bit address space). Additionally, the subsequent pointer arithmetic 'head + offsets[tile_idx]' can wrap around the address space on 32-bit platforms, bypassing the bounds check and allowing out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
Don't constant-fold DT_RESOURCE constants.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 391803952
Change-Id: I0ea3ec31d3e7dfda0f03b4027a237f08d00a3091
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions during TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer phase, constant folding might attempt to deep copy a resource tensor. This results in a segfault, as these tensors are supposed to not change. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculation when checking against max_constant_size_in_bytes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of num_elements() by DataTypeSize() could overflow if both values are large, wrapping around to a small value. This would cause the size check against max_constant_size_in_bytes to pass when it should fail, allowing an oversized constant to be folded, potentially leading to excessive memory allocation or other resource exhaustion issues.

commit_message:
Don't constant-fold DT_RESOURCE constants.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 391803952
Change-Id: I0ea3ec31d3e7dfda0f03b4027a237f08d00a3091
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions during TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer phase, constant folding might attempt to deep copy a resource tensor. This results in a segfault, as these tensors are supposed to not change. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculation when checking constant size limit
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of num_elements() by DataTypeSize() could overflow before the comparison with max_constant_size_in_bytes. If num_elements() is very large, the product can wrap around to a small value, causing the size check to pass when it should fail. This could allow folding of extremely large constants, leading to excessive memory allocation and potential denial of service. Both operands should be cast to a safe wider type or checked for overflow before multiplication.

commit_message: Fix fido2 signature validation bug
cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

code_snippets: unsigned char data_signed[200], client_data_hash[32], cert_export[32], cert_export_b64[64];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack-based buffer overflow when building data_signed from externally-controlled lengths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer data_signed is a fixed 200-byte stack buffer, but it is populated by concatenating rpid_hash (rpid_hash_len), client_data_hash (32), credential_id (credential_id_len), cert_x (cert_x_len), and cert_y (cert_y_len) plus 2 constant bytes. None of these lengths are validated before the memcpy calls. For example, credential_id_len alone can be up to 1023 bytes per the WebAuthn spec, and rpid_hash_len is typically 32 but is caller-controlled. An attacker who provides a large credential_id or manipulated inputs can overflow data_signed, corrupting adjacent stack variables, return addresses, and potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow encoding base64 into undersized cert_export_b64 buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: cert_export_b64 is allocated as 64 bytes on the stack. A 32-byte input base64-encoded produces ceil(32/3)*4 = 44 characters plus a null terminator, which fits. However, cert_export_len is set by gnutls_x509_crt_get_key_id and could theoretically exceed 32 bytes if the initial size was insufficient (the function may truncate or error). If for any reason more than ~45 bytes of base64 output are produced, this 64-byte buffer could be overflowed. The risk is lower than the data_signed overflow but still represents insufficient output buffer size validation.

code_snippets: } else if (0 == o_strncmp((const char *)cbor_string_handle(key), "sig", MIN(o_strlen("sig"), cbor_string_length(key)))) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Prefix-only comparison allows keys like "x5cFOO" or "sigBAR" to be accepted
vulnerabilities.long_description: The comparison uses MIN(expected_len, actual_len) as the length for o_strncmp. This means a CBOR key string "x5cextra" with length 8 would be compared using only 3 characters (the minimum), passing the check. An attacker could craft a CBOR map with ambiguous keys that satisfy the prefix match but aren't actually the expected keys, potentially leading to incorrect parsing or logic bypass. The comparison should verify that both the content matches and the lengths are equal.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387231300
Change-Id: I3111b54b2f232638d795be17efc46abe4ede6bf8
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing upper-bound validation on gather indices allows out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code only checks that indices are non-negative but never validates that they are within the bounds of the input tensor's axis dimension. An attacker can supply large positive index values that pass the non-negative check but cause `optimized_ops::Gather` to read memory beyond the input tensor buffer. This out-of-bounds read can leak sensitive data or crash the process, and in some contexts could be chained for further exploitation.

commit_message: fixed #2194
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double unregister leads to use-after-free of node
vulnerabilities.long_description: The comment states the intent is to 'register TWICE' the node (once for the command and once for the scenegraph global QP), but the code calls gf_node_unregister twice instead of gf_node_register. The node created by gf_bifs_dec_node typically has a reference count of 1. Calling unregister twice will decrement the reference count below zero (or free the node after the first call), causing the node to be freed while still being referenced by codec->ActiveQP, codec->scenegraph->global_qp, and inf->new_node. Any subsequent access to the node through these pointers constitutes a use-after-free, which can lead to crashes or arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286
Change-Id: If2073b36d4d5eedd386329f56729395fd7effee1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free due to accessing `tss` pointer and shared data structures outside the mutex lock
vulnerabilities.long_description: The pointer `tss` is obtained under `mu_` but is dereferenced (`tss->shape()`) after the mutex is released. Similarly, `fname_to_index_` and `sss_[idx]` are accessed outside the lock scope. If another thread concurrently modifies or destroys the underlying TensorSliceSet, the fname_to_index_ map, or the sss_ vector (e.g., during LoadAllShards or object destruction), this results in a race condition that can lead to use-after-free, reading stale/corrupt data, or a crash. An attacker who can trigger concurrent checkpoint loading operations could exploit this to cause denial of service or potentially achieve code execution.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in ASCII string reverse iteration when 'from' or 'to' are not bounds-checked against the string length
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ASCII/byte string branch, the pointer 'p' is set to 'string_prop.start + from' without verifying that 'from' is less than 'string_prop.size' (or 'length'). If 'from' is greater than or equal to the string size, 'p' points past the end of the string buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, if 'to' is negative, the loop continues decrementing 'p' below 'string_prop.start', reading before the buffer. An attacker who can control the iterator arguments (e.g., through manipulated array-like objects with a large 'length' property) could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in UTF-8 string reverse iteration due to missing bounds validation on 'from' and 'to'
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the UTF-8 string branch, 'njs_string_offset' is called with 'from' without first checking that 'from' is within [0, length). If 'from' exceeds the actual character count, the offset computation can walk past 'end', and subsequent calls to 'njs_utf8_next' and 'njs_utf8_prev' operate on out-of-bounds memory. Likewise, if 'to' is negative, the loop iterates past the beginning of the string buffer via repeated 'njs_utf8_prev' calls. This can result in reading arbitrary memory, potentially causing crashes or information leaks.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in ASCII/byte string reverse iteration when 'from' exceeds string length
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ASCII/byte string branch, the pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from` without verifying that `from` is less than `string_prop.size` (i.e., the string length). If the caller supplies a `from` value greater than or equal to the actual string length, `p` will point beyond the string buffer, and the subsequent reads (`njs_string_new` reading from `p`) and decrements (`p--`) will access out-of-bounds memory. Similarly, if `to` is negative or zero and the loop runs long enough, `p` could be decremented past the start of the buffer. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in UTF-8 string reverse iteration via unbounded njs_utf8_prev
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the UTF-8 string branch, `njs_utf8_prev(p)` is called in a loop without checking that `pos` (the result) stays within the bounds of the string buffer (`>= string_prop.start`). If `to` is 0 or the loop iterates more times than there are characters, `njs_utf8_prev` can walk before the start of the string buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, if `from` is beyond the actual character count, `njs_string_offset` and `njs_utf8_next` may produce a pointer past `end`, leading to further out-of-bounds access.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix overflow CHECK issue with `tf.raw_ops.UnsortedSegmentJoin`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that num_segments is non-negative before use in output shape construction
vulnerabilities.long_description: The num_segments value is read from user input without checking that it is non-negative. If num_segments is negative (for signed types), it could lead to integer overflow or underflow when computing the output shape or when used as a bound in the segment_id validation check (flat_segment_id(i) < num_segments would always be true for positive segment ids compared against a large unsigned-reinterpreted value). This could result in out-of-bounds memory access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write via output_index computed from segment_id and big_stride
vulnerabilities.long_description: The output_index is computed as start_offset + flat_segment_id(i) * big_stride. The validation loop checks flat_segment_id values against num_segments, but uses flat_segment_id.size() (the full flattened segment tensor) while the inner loop iterates over relative_offset_set.size() which may differ. If relative_offset_set maps indices differently, the wrong segment_id entries could be accessed. Additionally, integer overflow in the multiplication flat_segment_id(i) * big_stride could produce an out-of-bounds output_index, leading to a heap buffer overflow when writing to output_flat.

commit_message:
ui/vnc: limit client_cut_text msg payload size

currently a malicious client could define a payload
size of 2^32 - 1 bytes and send up to that size of
data to the vnc server. The server would allocated
that amount of memory which could easily create an
out of memory condition.

This patch limits the payload size to 1MB max.

Please note that client_cut_text messages are currently
silently ignored.

Signed-off-by: Peter Lieven <pl@kamp.de>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT length calculation allows heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT, the code reads a uint32_t `dlen` from the client and computes `8 + dlen` as the required message length. If the client sends a `dlen` value close to UINT32_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFF8 or larger), the expression `8 + dlen` wraps around to a small value due to unsigned integer overflow. The function then returns this small value as the required number of bytes. On the subsequent call, `len` will not equal 1 or 8, so the code falls through to `client_cut_text(vs, read_u32(data, 4), data + 8)` which receives the original huge `dlen` value but operates on a buffer that contains far fewer bytes. This can lead to a large out-of-bounds read (or potentially write depending on `client_cut_text` implementation), causing information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
const int64_t idx = CoordinatesToFlatIndex(g.group(), output_strides);
      out_flat(idx) = reduced_val();
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write via unchecked flat index into output tensor
vulnerabilities.long_description: The flat index computed by CoordinatesToFlatIndex is derived from sparse tensor coordinates (g.group()) and output strides, but is never validated against the bounds of the output tensor (out_flat) before being used as a write index. A malicious or malformed sparse tensor with coordinates that do not match the declared input_shape could yield an index that is negative or exceeds the allocated output buffer size, resulting in an out-of-bounds write. This can lead to heap corruption, denial of service, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
TfLiteTensor* tensor = GetMutableInput(context, node, index);
  return tensor->is_variable ? tensor : nullptr;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when GetMutableInput returns nullptr
vulnerabilities.long_description: GetMutableInput may return nullptr (e.g., if the index is out of bounds or the input is unavailable). The code immediately dereferences the returned pointer via `tensor->is_variable` without a null check. An attacker or erroneous model could trigger this by providing an invalid node/index, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable null pointer dereference.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one in loop causes out-of-bounds read / NULL pointer dereference on argv
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates with `i` from 0 to `argc - first_arg - 1`, accessing `argv[first_arg + i + 1]`. On the last iteration (`i = argc - first_arg - 1`), the index becomes `first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1 = argc`. By the C standard, `argv[argc]` is NULL, so `xstrdup(NULL)` is called, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference and crash. The loop bound should be `argc - first_arg - 1` to skip both the container-name argument and the sentinel. Additionally, `process->args_len` is set to `argc` (the total argument count) instead of the actual number of exec arguments, which can cause downstream consumers to read beyond the valid entries in the array.

commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error causes NULL pointer dereference via argv[argc] access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates with i from 0 to argc - first_arg - 1, accessing argv[first_arg + i + 1]. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), the index becomes first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1 = argc. By the C standard, argv[argc] is NULL, so xstrdup(NULL) is called, resulting in undefined behavior (likely a crash / NULL pointer dereference). Additionally, process->args_len is set to argc instead of the actual number of arguments copied (argc - first_arg - 1), which is an incorrect length that could cause out-of-bounds reads in downstream consumers of the process struct.

commit_message:
Fix overflow CHECK issue with `tf.raw_ops.UnsortedSegmentJoin`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output_index computation can lead to out-of-bounds write
vulnerabilities.long_description: The computation `flat_segment_id(i) * big_stride` can overflow when both values are large, wrapping around to a negative or otherwise unexpected value. This overflowed result is then used to index into output_flat, which can lead to an out-of-bounds memory write. Although segment_ids are validated to be less than num_segments and non-negative, the multiplication with big_stride is not checked for overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that num_segments is non-negative before use in shape computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value of num_segments is read from user-controlled input and is only checked for the tensor being non-empty. If NUM_SEGMENTS_TYPE is a signed integer type and a negative value is supplied, it is passed directly to GetOutputShape to construct the output tensor shape. Depending on the implementation of GetOutputShape and TensorFlow's shape handling, this could lead to an improperly sized output allocation. Subsequent writes indexed by `flat_segment_id(i) * big_stride` into this incorrectly allocated buffer could result in heap buffer overflow or other memory corruption.

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Timing side-channel in password hash comparison using strncmp
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses strncmp to compare password hashes/derived keys. strncmp is not constant-time: it returns as soon as a difference is found, meaning the execution time leaks information about how many leading bytes of the hash match. An attacker who can measure response timing can perform a byte-by-byte brute-force attack against the stored password hash, significantly reducing the effort to bypass authentication. A constant-time comparison function (e.g., crypto_memneq or a hand-rolled loop) should be used instead.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Using strncmp on binary data that may contain null bytes
vulnerabilities.long_description: strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0'). Since passwd1_sum and passwd2_sum are binary hash digests (not null-terminated strings), any embedded null byte will cause strncmp to treat the remaining bytes as equal, effectively shortening the comparison. This weakens the authentication check because an attacker only needs to match bytes up to the first null byte in either buffer, drastically reducing the keyspace that must be matched. A memory comparison function like memcmp (or better, a constant-time variant) should be used for binary data.

commit_message: Fix possible buffer overflow
cve_desc: None

code_snippets: } else if (0 == o_strncmp((const char *)cbor_string_handle(key), "sig", MIN(o_strlen("sig"), cbor_string_length(key)))) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete string comparison allows prefix-based key matching, potentially bypassing attestation checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: The comparison uses MIN(expected_len, actual_len) as the comparison length, which means a shorter key will only compare that many characters. For example, a key of "s" (length 1) would match "sig" because only 1 character is compared. Similarly, a longer key like "sigfoo" (length 6) would also match "sig" because only 3 characters (the minimum) are compared. An attacker could craft a malicious CBOR attestation statement with ambiguous keys that are incorrectly matched to 'x5c' or 'sig', leading to incorrect parsing and potentially allowing forged or invalid attestation statements to be accepted. The correct approach is to check that the lengths are equal AND the content matches.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential stack buffer overflow when base64-encoding the certificate key ID
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cert_export buffer is 32 bytes and cert_export_b64 is 64 bytes. Base64-encoding 32 bytes produces 44 bytes (plus potential null terminator = 45 bytes), which fits in 64 bytes. However, gnutls_x509_crt_get_key_id may update cert_export_len. If gnutls_x509_crt_get_key_id were called with a different hash algorithm or if the output exceeded 32 bytes for any reason, both cert_export (stack buffer overflow during key ID extraction) and cert_export_b64 (during base64 encoding) could overflow. The buffers are stack-allocated with fixed sizes based on assumptions about output length rather than dynamically sized, making this fragile.

commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: ptr->ai_addr is accessed after freeaddrinfo() has freed the memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, all addrinfo nodes (including the one pointed to by `ptr`) are freed. The code then dereferences `ptr->ai_addr` and `ptr->ai_addrlen` in the getsockname() call and subsequent net_port_get/net_address_get calls. This is a use-after-free vulnerability. Depending on heap allocation patterns, an attacker who can influence the heap state (e.g., via concurrent operations) could potentially overwrite the freed memory, leading to information disclosure, crashes, or potentially code execution. Even without adversarial influence, the behavior is undefined and can cause intermittent crashes or memory corruption.

commit_message:
Improve to cover scale value greater than one

PiperOrigin-RevId: 433050921
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, certain TFLite models that were created using TFLite model converter would crash when loaded in the TFLite interpreter. The culprit is that during quantization the scale of values could be greater than 1 but code was always assuming sub-unit scaling. Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Don't do any work when reshaping 0 elements sparse tensor.

If reshaping to 0 elements tensor, check that input has no elements.
If reshaping no elements input, check that output has no elements.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in product computation when multiplying user-supplied dimension sizes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `product` (int64_t) accumulates the product of all specified target shape dimensions via `product *= size` without any overflow check. A user can supply multiple large dimension values in `target_shape_in` whose product overflows a 64-bit integer. This could wrap around to a small or even negative value, causing subsequent checks like `product * missing == dense_size` or `output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size` to incorrectly pass. This may allow crafting inputs that bypass shape validation, leading to out-of-bounds memory access in the subsequent reshape functor that operates on `input_indices_in.matrix<int64>()` and `result_indices->matrix<int64>()`, potentially enabling information disclosure or code execution.
