PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in product computation when accumulating target shape dimensions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `product` (int64_t) is multiplied by each user-supplied dimension `size` in a loop without overflow checking. If an attacker provides multiple large dimension values in `target_shape_in`, the multiplication can silently overflow, wrapping around. This corrupted `product` value is then used to compute the `missing` dimension (`dense_size / product`) and to validate the reshape (`product * missing == dense_size`). An overflow could cause these checks to pass with incorrect values, leading to a mismatch between the logical output shape and the actual dense size, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access in subsequent sparse tensor operations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in validation check `product * missing == dense_size`
vulnerabilities.long_description: Even if `product` itself doesn't overflow, the multiplication `product * missing` used in the validation check can overflow for large values. If this overflows to equal `dense_size`, the check passes incorrectly, allowing an invalid reshape. This means the output shape dimensions would be inconsistent with the actual number of dense elements, which can lead to buffer over-reads or over-writes when the reshaped sparse tensor indices are computed by ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor.

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message: Fix for #168
cve_desc: The ECDSA operation of the micro-ecc library 1.0 is vulnerable to simple power analysis attacks which allows an adversary to extract the private ECC key.

commit_message:
vnc: sanitize bits_per_pixel from the client

bits_per_pixel that are less than 8 could result in accessing
non-initialized buffers later in the code due to the expectation
that bytes_per_pixel value that is used to initialize these buffers is
never zero.

To fix this check that bits_per_pixel from the client is one of the
values that the rfb protocol specification allows.

This is CVE-2014-7815.

Signed-off-by: Petr Matousek <pmatouse@redhat.com>

[ kraxel: apply codestyle fix ]

Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: The set_pixel_format function in ui/vnc.c in QEMU allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a small bytes_per_pixel value.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Execution continues after vnc_client_error in audio format handling, modifying state on an errored connection
vulnerabilities.long_description: When an invalid audio format value is received, vnc_client_error(vs) is called to signal a client error, but the 'break' only exits the inner switch statement (the format switch). Execution then continues to set vs->as.nchannels and vs->as.freq with attacker-controlled data. Similarly, if nchannels is invalid, vnc_client_error is called but vs->as.freq is still set afterward. If vnc_client_error does not immediately tear down the VncState (e.g., it defers cleanup), the attacker can corrupt the audio settings of the session, potentially leading to unexpected behavior such as out-of-bounds memory access when the audio subsystem processes data with malicious nchannels or freq values.

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Dimension size accessed before rank validation, leading to potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code calls dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) on x_tensor, cs_prev_tensor, h_prev_tensor, w_tensor, and b_tensor before verifying that these tensors have the expected number of dimensions (rank checks happen much later in the function). If a tensor has rank 0 or rank 1, calling dim_size(1) is an out-of-bounds access on the tensor's shape array. An attacker who provides a scalar or 1-D tensor for 'x' or 'cs_prev' can trigger undefined behavior or a crash.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(ctx, b_tensor->dim_size(0) == cell_size * 4,
                errors::InvalidArgument(
                    "b.dim_size(0) != cell_size * 4: ", b_tensor->dim_size(0),
                    " vs. ", cell_size * 4));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing shape validation for wci, wcf, and wco peephole weight tensors
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates the shapes of w_tensor and b_tensor against cell_size, but never checks that wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor have dim_size(0) == cell_size. These tensors are later accessed as vec<T>() and passed to the LSTM kernel which expects them to have exactly cell_size elements. If an attacker provides peephole weight tensors with fewer elements than cell_size, the kernel will read out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in arithmetic on input_size and cell_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions `input_size + cell_size` and `cell_size * 4` are computed as int64_t arithmetic without overflow checks. While int64_t overflow is unlikely in practice, a crafted input with very large dimension values could cause these expressions to overflow, bypassing the shape validation checks. This could then lead to incorrect tensor shapes being used, causing out-of-bounds memory access in the LSTM computation kernel.

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing shape validation for wci, wcf, and wco peephole weight tensors
vulnerabilities.long_description: The tensors wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor are used as vec<T>() (1-D tensors) in the LSTMBlockCellFprop call, but their shapes are never validated. There is no check that they are 1-D or that their size equals cell_size. A malicious user could supply tensors of incorrect rank or size, leading to out-of-bounds memory reads during the LSTM computation, potentially causing crashes or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in arithmetic on int64 dimension values used for shape checks and allocations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions `input_size + cell_size` and `cell_size * 4` are computed using int64 arithmetic without overflow checks. If an attacker provides tensors with very large dimensions, these could overflow, causing the shape validation checks to pass incorrectly and subsequent tensor allocations to be undersized. This could lead to heap buffer overflows when the LSTM kernel writes to the allocated buffers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing rank validation for input tensors before accessing dimensions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code accesses dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) on input tensors without first verifying that they have at least 2 dimensions (rank >= 2). If an attacker supplies a scalar or 1-D tensor for x, cs_prev, h_prev, or w, accessing dim_size(1) would be an out-of-bounds access on the shape array, potentially causing undefined behavior or a crash. Similarly, b_tensor, wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor are never checked for having rank 1 before being accessed as vec<T>().

commit_message:
Validate dimensions of input tensor in `FractionalAvgPoolGrad`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388286227
Change-Id: Ieb7566155e92acc8993a2212c76deacadc0edc8a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds memory access via unchecked pooling sequence indices
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values read from row_seq_tensor_flat and col_seq_tensor_flat are used directly as row/column start indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) without any bounds validation against the input tensor dimensions (in_rows, in_cols). While in_row_end and in_col_end are clamped to the max index with std::min, in_row_start and in_col_start are not checked to be >= 0 or < in_rows/in_cols. A malicious user could supply negative or excessively large sequence values, causing in_index to reference memory outside the in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that out_backprop dimensions match orig_input_tensor_shape for batch and depth
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is no check that out_batch <= in_batch or that out_depth == in_depth. The loop iterates using out_batch for the batch dimension (indexing into in_backprop with b * in_rows) and out_depth for the depth dimension (indexing into in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat's rows). If out_batch > in_batch or out_depth > in_depth, the computed in_index or depth index d will exceed the allocated buffer size, resulting in heap buffer overflow reads/writes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero when pooling cell has zero or negative size
vulnerabilities.long_description: If in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (which can happen when user-controlled sequence tensor values cause in_row_end to be clamped below in_row_start), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can be zero or negative. Although the inner loops may not execute in the negative case, specific combinations of values could yield num_elements_in_pooling_cell == 0, causing a floating-point division by zero.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in matrix dimension computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The products in_cols * in_rows * in_batch and out_cols * out_rows * out_batch are computed without overflow checks. Since these values come from user-controlled inputs (orig_input_tensor_shape and out_backprop dimensions), carefully chosen large values could cause 64-bit integer overflow, resulting in an undersized Eigen matrix map that wraps around the actual buffer, leading to out-of-bounds memory access during subsequent element operations.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow via unchecked integer values in PORT command
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sscanf reads six integers from user-controlled input without any range validation (e.g., constraining them to 0-255). These integers are then formatted with sprintf into a fixed-size buffer of INET_ADDRSTRLEN (16 bytes). If an attacker sends large integer values (e.g., very large negative or positive numbers), the resulting string from sprintf can exceed 16 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow. This can lead to code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of sscanf is not checked, leading to use of uninitialized variables
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of sscanf is not checked. If the input string does not match the expected format, some or all of the variables a, b, c, d, e, f may remain uninitialized. These uninitialized values are then used in sprintf and arithmetic operations, leading to undefined behavior. An attacker could craft a malformed PORT command to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: FTP bounce attack: no validation that PORT address matches client address
vulnerabilities.long_description: The handler accepts any IP address and port from the PORT command without verifying that the address matches the connected client's address. This is the classic FTP bounce attack (CWE-441), where an attacker can instruct the FTP server to connect to arbitrary third-party hosts and ports, potentially enabling port scanning, bypassing firewalls, or attacking internal services.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow/misuse in port calculation with unchecked values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values e and f are read as arbitrary integers with no range validation (should be 0-255). The expression e * 256 + f can produce values outside the valid port range (0-65535), including negative values. Depending on how data_port is used later, this could lead to unexpected behavior such as connecting to unintended ports or triggering integer overflow issues.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded write to fixed-size msg->attr array leads to buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop parses attributes and stores them in msg->attr[msg->attr_count], incrementing attr_count each iteration, but never checks whether attr_count has exceeded the size of the msg->attr array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes could cause writes past the end of the array, leading to heap or stack corruption and potential remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when buf_len is smaller than sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)
vulnerabilities.long_description: If buf_len is less than sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr), the expression 'buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)' underflows (both are unsigned), producing a very large value. This causes the length validation check to behave incorrectly. If msg_len happens to match the wrapped value, parsing proceeds with out-of-bounds memory access. The function should validate buf_len >= sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr) before performing the subtraction.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message: Fix wolfmqtt-fuzzer: Null-dereference WRITE in MqttProps_Free
cve_desc: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 has a heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect (called from MqttClient_DecodePacket and MqttClient_WaitType).

code_snippets:
#ifdef WOLFMQTT_MULTITHREAD
            readLocked = 1; /* if in this state read is locked */
        #endif
vulnerabilities.short_description: Mutex unlock without corresponding lock in non-blocking + multithread path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When WOLFMQTT_MULTITHREAD and WOLFMQTT_NONBLOCK are both enabled, a problematic sequence occurs: (1) Function is called, enters MQTT_MSG_BEGIN, acquires lockRecv, sets readLocked=1. (2) Falls through to MQTT_MSG_WAIT, MqttPacket_Read returns MQTT_CODE_CONTINUE (rc <= 0), execution breaks out of the switch. (3) Because MQTT_CODE_CONTINUE is not equal to itself... actually it IS, so *mms_stat is NOT reset and stays MQTT_MSG_WAIT. (4) readLocked is 1, so lockRecv IS unlocked. (5) Function returns MQTT_CODE_CONTINUE. On the next call: *mms_stat is MQTT_MSG_WAIT, so the switch enters the WAIT case directly—lockRecv is never acquired (that only happens in MQTT_MSG_BEGIN). If MqttPacket_Read succeeds, execution falls through to MQTT_MSG_READ where readLocked is unconditionally set to 1. At cleanup, wm_SemUnlock(&client->lockRecv) is called on a mutex that was never locked in this invocation. This can corrupt mutex state, cause undefined behavior, or allow concurrent reads on the socket leading to data races and potential memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential infinite loop when non-matching packets are continuously received
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the received packet does not match the expected wait_type/wait_packet_id, waitMatchFound remains 0, and if rc >= 0, the function jumps back to wait_again. If a remote server or attacker continuously sends packets that don't match the expected type/id, this loop repeats indefinitely. While individual MqttPacket_Read calls are bounded by timeout_ms, the outer loop has no iteration limit or cumulative timeout, so the function can block the calling thread indefinitely. This constitutes a denial-of-service condition where a malicious or misbehaving broker can stall the client.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that buf_len is at least sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr), leading to out-of-bounds read and unsigned integer underflow.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function dereferences `buf` as a `pjstun_msg_hdr*` and reads its fields (type, length) without first verifying that `buf_len >= sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)`. If `buf_len` is smaller than the header size, reading `msg->hdr->type` and `msg->hdr->length` constitutes an out-of-bounds read. Furthermore, the length validation `buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` is performed on unsigned types (`pj_size_t`), so if `buf_len < sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` the subtraction wraps around to a very large value, potentially allowing the check to pass with a crafted `msg_len`, which then leads to further out-of-bounds parsing of attributes. An attacker supplying a very short buffer could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ldblk calculation leading to undersized heap buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The ldblk value is computed by multiplying MATLAB_HDR.SizeX (a 32-bit unsigned value read from the file) by 2, 4, or 8 without overflow checking. If SizeX is large enough, the multiplication can overflow the ssize_t type, resulting in a small or negative value. This truncated value is then used in AcquireQuantumMemory to allocate the buffer BImgBuff, which would be too small. Subsequent ReadBlob calls using ldblk as the size could then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a malicious MAT file to trigger this and potentially achieve remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds array access on z2qtype with unchecked index z
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable z is derived from file-controlled data (ReadBlobXXXLong). While there are some checks (z!=3 and z!=1 for DimFlag 16), the value of z is used as an index into the z2qtype array. During the do-while loop, z is decremented and used as index. If z2qtype has a fixed size and z exceeds its bounds (e.g., in the DimFlag==12 case only z==3 is accepted but during the loop z goes through 3,2,1,0), any index beyond the array size would cause out-of-bounds read, potentially leading to undefined behavior or crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in filepos calculation from attacker-controlled ObjectSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: MATLAB_HDR.ObjectSize is read directly from the file and added to filepos without overflow checking. A crafted file could set ObjectSize to a very large value causing filepos (MagickOffsetType) to overflow, leading to SeekBlob being called with an incorrect position. This could cause the parser to read from wrong offsets, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or processing of arbitrary data as MAT structures.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free / null pointer dereference when processing animation frames
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing multi-frame animations (Frames > 1), BImgBuff is freed and set to NULL, then execution jumps to NEXT_FRAME. However, NEXT_FRAME is inside the switch on CellType which recalculates ldblk but does NOT reallocate BImgBuff before the code that uses it (the allocation happens later). Additionally, quantum_info may have been allocated but not freed before the goto, leading to resource leaks. The buffer BImgBuff is freed but then the code at NEXT_FRAME eventually tries to use it before reallocation could happen in certain code paths.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Dangerous blob pointer swapping between image and rotated_image may lead to use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code swaps blob pointers between the original image and the rotated image, then deletes the original from the list. If DeleteImageFromList destroys the image, the blob that was swapped to it (originally from rotated_image) gets destroyed, but rotated_image now holds the original blob. This manual resource management is error-prone and could lead to double-free or use-after-free conditions depending on how the image list manipulation functions handle blob cleanup.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of quantum_info on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: quantum_info is allocated via AcquireQuantumInfo but on several early return paths (e.g., when SetImageExtent fails and returns DestroyImageList(image)), quantum_info is never freed with DestroyQuantumInfo. Similarly, BImgBuff and clone_info may leak on various error paths. This can lead to memory exhaustion when processing many malformed files.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

code_snippets: snprintf(addr, sizeof(addr), "%d.%d.%d.%d", a, b, c, d);
vulnerabilities.short_description: sscanf return value unchecked, leading to use of uninitialized variables
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of sscanf() is never checked. If the input string `str` is malformed or does not contain six comma-separated integers, some or all of the variables a, b, c, d, e, f will remain uninitialized. These uninitialized values are then used in snprintf() to build an address and to compute the data port. This can lead to undefined behavior and unpredictable values being used for network connections.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No range validation on PORT parameters allows out-of-range or negative values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The six integers parsed from the PORT command are never validated to be in the range 0–255. Since they are parsed as `int` with %d, they can be negative or arbitrarily large. The port calculation `e * 256 + f` can yield negative or very large port numbers, and the address octets can produce invalid or unexpected IP address strings. An attacker could supply crafted values to cause the server to attempt connections to arbitrary addresses and ports.

vulnerabilities.short_description: FTP bounce attack: PORT command address not validated against client's IP
vulnerabilities.long_description: The server accepts any IP address provided in the PORT command without verifying it matches the connected client's IP address. This is the classic FTP bounce attack (CWE-441 / CWE-918): an attacker can instruct the FTP server to open a data connection to an arbitrary third-party host and port. This can be used to port-scan internal networks, bypass firewalls, or relay attacks to hosts that trust the FTP server's IP address.

commit_message:
Validate dimensions of input tensor in `FractionalAvgPoolGrad`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388286227
Change-Id: Ieb7566155e92acc8993a2212c76deacadc0edc8a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read/write due to missing validation on pooling sequence values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values read from row_seq_tensor_flat and col_seq_tensor_flat are used directly as indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) without validating that they are non-negative or within the bounds of the input tensor dimensions (in_rows, in_cols). An attacker can supply crafted pooling sequence tensors with negative values or values exceeding in_rows/in_cols, causing out-of-bounds reads and writes on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. Note that only in_row_end and in_col_end are clamped via std::min, but in_row_start and in_col_start have no bounds checks at all.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that input and output batch/depth dimensions match, enabling out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code uses out_batch and out_depth to iterate over elements of out_backprop, and in_batch, in_rows, in_cols to compute indices into in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. However, there is no validation that in_batch == out_batch or in_depth == out_depth. If an attacker provides an orig_input_tensor_shape where batch or depth differs from the actual out_backprop dimensions, the computed indices (in_index, out_index) can go out of bounds, leading to heap buffer overflow reads and writes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero when pooling cell has zero or negative size
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the pooling sequence values are crafted such that in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (which can happen when in_max_row_index/in_max_col_index is small or the sequence values are adversarial), num_elements_in_pooling_cell could be zero or negative. When it equals zero, a division by zero occurs. Since in_row_start is not validated against in_rows (only in_row_end is clamped), an attacker could trigger this by providing a small in_rows value with large pooling sequence starts.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in dimension product calculations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The products in_cols * in_rows * in_batch and out_cols * out_rows * out_batch are computed without overflow checks. Since in_rows, in_cols, and in_batch come from user-controlled input (orig_input_tensor_shape), an attacker can provide large dimension values that overflow int64_t, resulting in incorrect matrix dimensions and subsequent out-of-bounds memory accesses when the Eigen matrix maps are used.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation that out_backprop is a 4-D tensor
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code assumes out_backprop is a 4-dimensional tensor and accesses dim_size(0) through dim_size(3) without first checking that out_backprop.dims() == 4. If a tensor with fewer dimensions is provided, dim_size calls on non-existent dimensions could lead to undefined behavior or crashes.

commit_message: Fix wolfmqtt-fuzzer: Null-dereference WRITE in MqttProps_Free
cve_desc: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 has a heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect (called from MqttClient_DecodePacket and MqttClient_WaitType).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded loop via goto wait_again when non-matching packets are continuously received
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses a `goto wait_again` loop that restarts the entire wait process whenever a received packet does not match the expected wait_type/wait_packet_id. The `timeout_ms` parameter is only applied per individual `MqttPacket_Read` call, not to the overall wait operation. An attacker (or a misbehaving broker) that continuously sends valid but non-matching packets (e.g., publishes on other topics, pings, etc.) within each read timeout window can cause this function to loop indefinitely, resulting in a denial of service. The caller's intended timeout is never enforced as an aggregate deadline.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Recv mutex lock state inconsistency in NONBLOCK+MULTITHREAD mode when re-entering MQTT_MSG_WAIT
vulnerabilities.long_description: When WOLFMQTT_MULTITHREAD and WOLFMQTT_NONBLOCK are both enabled, if the function enters MQTT_MSG_BEGIN, it acquires lockRecv and sets readLocked=1, then falls through to MQTT_MSG_WAIT. If MqttPacket_Read returns a non-continue error (rc <= 0 but not CONTINUE), the code breaks, state remains MQTT_MSG_WAIT (not reset since rc may still satisfy the NONBLOCK continue check — but more critically, if MqttPacket_Read returns MQTT_CODE_CONTINUE, rc<=0 triggers break, state stays MQTT_MSG_WAIT, and lockRecv IS unlocked). On the next call, execution enters MQTT_MSG_WAIT directly with readLocked=0 (re-initialized at wait_again), and MqttPacket_Read is called without holding lockRecv. Similarly, in MQTT_MSG_READ/MQTT_MSG_READ_PAYLOAD, `readLocked` is unconditionally set to 1 as an assumption, but if the state was entered without actually holding the lock, wm_SemUnlock will be called on an unheld mutex, causing undefined behavior. This creates race conditions where multiple threads could read from the socket concurrently, potentially corrupting packet data.

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

commit_message:
rsi: release skb if rsi_prepare_beacon fails

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when rsi_prepare_beacon fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() returns a failure, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb. This leads to a memory leak each time beacon preparation fails. An attacker or adverse conditions that repeatedly trigger this failure path could exhaust kernel memory (denial of service). The fix is to call dev_kfree_skb(skb) before returning -EINVAL.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds buffer access after charset re-encoding changes buffer length but loop bounds remain unchanged
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the RABIN2_CHARSET environment variable is set, the buffer is re-encoded and `len` is updated to `res` (the new encoded size), which may be smaller than the original `to - from`. However, the main scanning loop still iterates with `needle < to` and indexes the buffer as `buf + needle - from`. Since `to - from` can be larger than the new `len`, this leads to out-of-bounds reads (and potentially writes via subsequent processing). An attacker who controls the binary being analyzed and the RABIN2_CHARSET variable could trigger heap buffer over-reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing charset output buffer length
vulnerabilities.long_description: `len` is an `st64` that can be up to `ST32_MAX` (approximately 2^31 - 1). Multiplying by 4 and storing in an `int` (`int outlen = len * 4`) causes a signed integer overflow. While `calloc(len, 4)` separately computes the allocation size correctly, the overflowed `outlen` is passed to `r_charset_encode_str` as the output buffer size limit, potentially allowing writes far beyond what `outlen` (now a small or negative number) would suggest, or causing incorrect encoding behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read in optimization path when buffer was replaced by charset encoding
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 4-byte read optimization `r_read_le32(buf + needle - from)` checks `needle + 4 < to` which is based on the original `to` value. After charset encoding replaces the buffer with a potentially shorter one (updating `len` but not `to`), the index `needle - from` can exceed the actual allocated buffer size, causing a heap out-of-bounds read. This is part of the same root cause as the charset re-encoding issue but specifically affects the le32 optimization check.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double-free when InitFromSerialized fails: Unref() may delete the object while unique_ptr still owns it
vulnerabilities.long_description: When InitFromSerialized fails, the code explicitly calls result->Unref() which, for a reference-counted object starting with a refcount of 1, will delete the object. However, 'result' is a std::unique_ptr that still owns the pointer. When OP_REQUIRES triggers a return (or when the unique_ptr goes out of scope), it will call delete on the already-freed pointer, causing a double-free. This can lead to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS: released pointer is never freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: result.release() unconditionally relinquishes ownership of the allocated BoostedTreesEnsembleResource before the status is checked. If CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the code simply falls through without freeing the resource or taking any cleanup action, resulting in a memory leak. Repeated calls to this op could exhaust memory. Additionally, if CreateResource fails with another error, the released pointer is also leaked since OP_REQUIRES_OK only returns from the function without freeing the resource.

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized variable 'blk' used in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the L_SENDB_SYM block, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never assigned a value before being passed to prepare_missing() and later checked with mrb_proc_p(). The variable contains whatever stack garbage was present. If the method is undefined, 'blk' with garbage data is passed to prepare_missing(). After a C function method call, the uninitialized 'blk' is checked with mrb_proc_p(), which could incorrectly interpret stack garbage as a valid proc pointer, leading to memory corruption when accessing p->flags. An attacker who can craft mruby bytecode could trigger this to corrupt memory or leak information.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized variable 'blk' used in OP_SUPER handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never assigned before being passed to prepare_missing() when the super method is not found. This uninitialized value could contain arbitrary stack data and is passed directly as an argument. Depending on how prepare_missing() uses the blk parameter, this could lead to type confusion, invalid memory access, or other undefined behavior. Crafted mruby bytecode calling super on a nonexistent method could trigger this path.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the raw pointer obtained from result.release() is never freed. The unique_ptr has already relinquished ownership via release(), and since the resource manager did not accept ownership (it already has one), the BoostedTreesEnsembleResource object is leaked. An attacker could repeatedly trigger this code path to cause unbounded memory growth, leading to denial of service.

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in len+2 can lead to heap buffer overflow during fiber stack copy
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `status == MRB_FIBER_CREATED`, the function calls `mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2)` where `len` is an `mrb_int` (signed integer). If `len` is sufficiently large (close to the maximum value of `mrb_int`), the expression `len+2` can wrap around due to signed integer overflow, resulting in a small or negative value passed to `mrb_stack_extend`. This causes an undersized stack allocation. The subsequent while loop then copies `len` elements from `a` into the stack buffer starting at `c->stbase+1`, writing far beyond the allocated region. This is a heap buffer overflow that could be exploited for arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

commit_message:
RDMA/rtrs-clt: Fix possible double free in error case

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.

Fixes: eab098246625 ("RDMA/rtrs-clt: Refactor the failure cases in alloc_clt")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220217030929.323849-1-haris.iqbal@ionos.com
Reported-by: Miaoqian Lin <linmq006@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Md Haris Iqbal <haris.iqbal@ionos.com>
Reviewed-by: Jack Wang <jinpu.wang@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Gunthorpe <jgg@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: drivers/infiniband/ulp/rtrs/rtrs-clt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.12 has a double free related to rtrs_clt_dev_release.

commit_message:

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.
