commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect size check uses sizeof(pointer) instead of sizeof(struct), allowing out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `bh` is declared as `blobheader *bh`, so `sizeof(bh)` evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. This means the length check is insufficient — data buffers smaller than the actual `blobheader` struct but larger than a pointer size will pass the check, and subsequent accesses to `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->version`, `bh->hdrsize`, and `bh->flags` will read beyond the allocated buffer. The correct check should be `sizeof(*bh)` or `sizeof(blobheader)`. An attacker providing a short buffer could trigger out-of-bounds reads, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect size check uses sizeof(pointer) instead of sizeof(struct), enabling out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `bh` is declared as `blobheader *bh`, so `sizeof(bh)` evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. If the `blobheader` struct is larger than a pointer, the length check is insufficient and subsequent accesses to `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, and `bh->flags` can read beyond the bounds of the `data` buffer. An attacker providing a small crafted buffer could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. The correct check should be `sizeof(*bh)` or `sizeof(blobheader)`.

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the L_SENDB_SYM block, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never explicitly initialized before being passed to prepare_missing() when a method is not found, and before being checked with mrb_proc_p(blk) after a C function call returns. Reading an uninitialized stack variable leads to undefined behavior. An attacker who can trigger the method_missing path or the post-call block orphan check could exploit this to cause incorrect control flow, information disclosure (reading stack garbage), or potentially code execution depending on the platform and compiler behavior. The same pattern appears in the OP_SUPER handler where 'blk' is similarly declared but potentially used uninitialized.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ldblk calculation leads to undersized heap buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is an unsigned 32-bit integer read from the file. The multiplications (2 * SizeX, 4 * SizeX, 8 * SizeX) are performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before being cast to ssize_t, so they can silently wrap around. A large SizeX value (e.g., 0x80000000) could cause `4 * SizeX` to overflow to 0, resulting in a tiny BImgBuff allocation. However, image->columns is set to the original SizeX. When ImportQuantumPixels later processes the buffer, it reads based on image->columns, causing a heap buffer over-read or over-write. An attacker can craft a MAT file with a large SizeX to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior or integer overflow when shifting by image->depth (up to 64)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `one` is of type `size_t` (typically 64 bits). When image->depth is 64 (as set for miINT64/miUINT64/miDOUBLE cell types), the expression `one << 64` is undefined behavior in C, as shifting by the bit-width of the type or more is UB. For depth=32, on a 64-bit platform `1 << 32` produces 4294967296 which may be unintended for image->colors. This could lead to unpredictable behavior or incorrect memory allocations downstream.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Early return on SetImageExtent failure leaks quantum_info, clone_info, and BImgBuff
vulnerabilities.long_description: When SetImageExtent fails, the function returns DestroyImageList(image) without freeing quantum_info (allocated just before via AcquireQuantumInfo), clone_info (allocated earlier via CloneImageInfo), or potentially closing temporary files associated with clone_info. This causes memory and resource leaks. If triggered repeatedly, this could lead to resource exhaustion (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on z2qtype array if z value is not properly bounded
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable z is set from ReadBlobXXXLong(image2) for the 3D/4D matrix cases, and the code only checks if z==3 or z==1 for the 4D case. However, in the 3D case (DimFlag==12), z is checked against 3 but is used as an index into z2qtype[] array. After the `do { } while(z-- >= 2)` loop decrements z, values of z are used as indices. If z2qtype has a limited size and the control flow allows z to take unexpected values, this could result in an out-of-bounds array access.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-7fw8-54cv-r7pm
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions 2.11.1 and prior, parsing an incoming SIP message that contains a malformed multipart can potentially cause out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all PJSIP users that accept SIP multipart. The patch is available as commit in the `master` branch. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-7fw8-54cv-r7pm
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions 2.11.1 and prior, parsing an incoming SIP message that contains a malformed multipart can potentially cause out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all PJSIP users that accept SIP multipart. The patch is available as commit in the `master` branch. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow via repeated strcat into fixed-size buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses a 256-byte stack buffer and repeatedly concatenates capability strings using strcat without checking remaining space. The extensions list comes from server input (extensions_str), and while each individual strcat appends a known string, if a malicious or misconfigured server advertises all recognized capabilities, the accumulated length could exceed 256 bytes. More critically, the loop iterates over all extensions from the server, and duplicate capability names in the input would cause the same string to be appended multiple times (since each if-block is independent, not else-if). A server sending many duplicated recognized capability names could overflow the buffer, leading to stack corruption and potential code execution.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `orders` array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `orders` array is allocated before the `insnum` and `patnum` validation checks. If either check fails and the function returns `MP_LOADER_FAILED`, the `orders` array is never freed, causing a memory leak. An attacker could craft a malicious S3M file with invalid insnum/patnum values to trigger repeated memory leaks, potentially leading to resource exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow writing to `header->ord[]` without bounds checking on index `j`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop copies order entries from the `orders` array into `header->ord[]` without checking whether the index `j` exceeds the fixed size of the `ord` array in `TXMHeader`. If `header->ordnum` is large and the data contains no 0xFF sentinel values, `j` can grow beyond the bounds of `header->ord`, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a malicious S3M file with a large order list to overwrite adjacent memory, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Sample index `s` is never bounds-checked against the `smp` array size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `s` is incremented each time a valid sample is found, and is used to index into the `smp` array (e.g., `smp[s].name`, `smp[s].samplen`, etc.). However, there is no check that `s` stays within the bounds of the `smp` array (which is `module->smp`). If the S3M file contains many instruments with valid sample data, `s` could exceed the allocated size of the `smp` array, leading to out-of-bounds writes. A crafted file could exploit this for arbitrary memory corruption.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference of `obj` in the `beach` error handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `prelink_range` is NULL (the first `goto beach`), `obj` is still NULL. The `beach` label then dereferences `obj` via `obj->cache_buf = NULL`, causing a null pointer dereference. This leads to a crash (denial of service) when loading a malformed binary file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `rebase_info` on several error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `rebase_info` object allocated by `r_rebase_info_new_from_mach0` is never freed on any of the error paths that jump to `beach`. When `prelink_info` parsing fails, or `pending_bin_files` allocation fails, `rebase_info` is leaked. The `beach` handler also does not free it.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.short_description: Writing to a const buffer by casting away const qualifier to modify HTTP header field characters in-place
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function accepts `const char *buffer` but the Ragel-generated code casts away the const qualifier with `(char *)p` and calls `snake_upcase_char()` to modify the buffer contents in-place (states tr21 and tr23). If the caller passes a truly read-only memory region (e.g., mmap'd read-only page, string literal, or a buffer the caller does not expect to be modified), this results in undefined behavior, potentially causing a segfault/crash or silently corrupting shared data. An attacker sending HTTP requests with header field names could trigger this write to supposedly read-only memory.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access due to missing negative pos check in delete branch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the delete ('d') branch, when dir == '<', pos is decremented before any bounds check. If pos is 0 at that point, it becomes -1. The subsequent bounds check only validates the upper bound (d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX) and checks if l < 0, but with a negative pos, l = *decoded_size - pos becomes *decoded_size + 1 (positive), and d = decoded + pos points before the buffer. This allows an out-of-bounds read and write via the memmove and the decoded[pos] != c comparison. A crafted inflection rule can trigger this to read/write memory before the decoded buffer, potentially leading to information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing negative pos check in insert branch allows out-of-bounds write
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the insert ('i') branch, pos is not checked for being negative before being used to compute source, destination, and length for memmove, and to write decoded[pos] = c. While the position-setting block (c > 10 && c < 20) does check pos < 0, the insert and delete operations modify pos (e.g., pos++ in insert, pos-- in delete) without re-validating it against 0 before the next iteration. A carefully crafted rule sequence could manipulate pos to become negative through repeated operations, leading to writes before the start of the decoded buffer.

code_snippets: debug_print("Out of buffer in %s at pos: %i\n", decoded, pos);
debug_print("Character mismatch in %s at pos: %i (%c != %c)\n", decoded, pos, decoded[pos], c);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Passing potentially non-null-terminated buffer to printf-style %s format specifier
vulnerabilities.long_description: The decoded buffer is a raw unsigned char array whose size is tracked by decoded_size, and there is no guarantee it is null-terminated. Passing it as a %s argument to debug_print (a printf-style function) will cause it to read beyond the buffer until a null byte is found, leading to an out-of-bounds read. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Negative or zero length value leads to massive heap/stack read via fread
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the JPEG marker length field (data[2]<<8|data[3]) is 0 or 1, then `len` becomes -2 or -1. When passed to `fread`, the `int` is implicitly converted to `size_t` (an unsigned type), resulting in an extremely large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFE). This causes fread to attempt writing far beyond the 0x10000-byte stack buffer `data`, leading to a stack buffer overflow and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in 0xffda marker parsing via unchecked data[0] index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 0xffda (Start of Scan) case, `data[0]` is used as a multiplier to compute array indices `1+data[0]*2` and `3+data[0]*2`. Since data[0] can be up to 255, the computed index can be as large as 513, far exceeding the actual marker data length. This results in an out-of-bounds read from the stack buffer, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in calloc size computation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both `jh->wide` (up to 65535) and `jh->clrs` (derived from attacker-controlled data) are multiplied together before being passed to calloc. If the product overflows the integer width, a smaller-than-expected buffer is allocated. Subsequent writes to `jh->row` assuming the intended size would then overflow the heap buffer, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on data buffer when marker length is shorter than expected
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 0xffc3 and 0xffc0 cases access data[0] through data[7] without verifying that `len` is at least 8. A crafted JPEG with a short marker length could cause fread to read fewer bytes than expected, and subsequent accesses to data[5], data[7], etc. would read uninitialized or stale stack data, potentially influencing control flow or causing incorrect memory allocations.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow writing to fixed-size header->ord array without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop copies order entries from the file-controlled `orders` array into `header->ord[j++]` without verifying that `j` stays within the bounds of the `header->ord` array, which is typically a fixed-size buffer. Since `header->ordnum` is read directly from the file and can be up to 65535, an attacker can craft a file with a large ordnum and order values != 255, causing writes far past the end of the `ord` array, leading to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on pre-allocated instr[] and smp[] arrays using file-controlled insnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values `header->insnum` is read directly from the file and used to iterate over the `instr[]` and `smp[]` arrays, which are pre-allocated by `module` with a fixed size (e.g., 256). If a crafted file specifies a larger `insnum`, the loop will access `instr[i]` and `smp[s]` out of bounds, causing heap buffer overflow. The sample index `s` is also unbounded and increments independently. An attacker can exploit this to corrupt heap memory and potentially achieve code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on pre-allocated phead[] array using file-controlled patnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value `header->patnum` is read from the file and used to index the `phead[]` array, which is pre-allocated with a fixed size. A maliciously crafted S3M file with a large `patnum` value will cause out-of-bounds reads and writes on `phead[i]`, leading to heap corruption and potential arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'orders' array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `orders` array is allocated early in the function, but several code paths (e.g., when insParaPtrs or patParaPtrs allocation fails before orders is deleted, or if ordnum is 0 leading to issues) could potentially leak memory. More concretely, if `header->insnum` or `header->patnum` is 0, `new mp_uword[0]` might succeed, but the code continues without issues. However, looking at the flow, `orders` is deleted before the instrument/pattern pointer allocations, so this specific path is fine. The `orders` allocation happens after the type-16 check, so that's also fine. Actually reviewing more carefully, orders is always deleted. Let me retract this one.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table via unchecked LZW-decoded colour index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `colour` is an unsigned char (0–255) obtained from LZW-decoded data. It is used directly as an index into `colour_table` without any bounds check against `colour_table_size`, which can be as small as 4 (2 << 1). A maliciously crafted GIF can produce colour indices that exceed the size of the local or global colour table, resulting in an out-of-bounds heap read. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table via unchecked background_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the clear_image branch, `gif->background_index` is used to index into `colour_table` without verifying it is within `colour_table_size`. If a crafted GIF specifies a background index larger than the colour table, this results in an out-of-bounds heap read, potentially leaking memory or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Early return bypasses restoration of gif->buffer_position
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `bitmap_get_buffer` returns NULL (both in the main path and the GIF_FRAME_RESTORE sub-path), the function returns immediately without executing the cleanup code at `gif_decode_frame_exit` that restores `gif->buffer_position` from `save_buffer_position`. This leaves `gif->buffer_position` in a corrupted state, which could cause incorrect parsing or out-of-bounds accesses in subsequent operations on the GIF.

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded decrement of ci pointer without checking against cibase
vulnerabilities.long_description: When vmexec is true and the fiber status is MRB_FIBER_CREATED, c->ci is decremented without verifying it is greater than c->cibase. If c->ci already equals c->cibase, this results in an out-of-bounds pointer that points before the allocated callinfo stack. Subsequent reads and writes through c->ci (e.g., c->ci->proc, c->ci->pc in mrb_vm_exec) will access memory outside the allocated region, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write via c->ci[1] without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the else branch (fiber status is not MRB_FIBER_CREATED), when vmexec is true, the code writes to c->ci[1].stack[0] without verifying that c->ci[1] is within the bounds of the callinfo stack. If c->ci is at the top of the allocated ci stack, this writes past the end of the buffer, corrupting adjacent memory. An attacker who can control fiber state and trigger this code path could exploit this for arbitrary memory writes.

code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in len+2 passed to mrb_stack_extend
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameter len is of type mrb_int (signed). If a caller supplies a very large value for len (close to MRB_INT_MAX), the expression len+2 can overflow, resulting in a small or negative value passed to mrb_stack_extend. This could cause an undersized stack allocation, after which the loop copying len values (b = c->stbase+1; e = b + len; while(b<e) *b++ = *a++;) would write far past the allocated stack buffer, leading to heap corruption and potential code execution.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table via unchecked LZW-decoded colour index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `colour` is an unsigned char (0–255) obtained from LZW-decoded data. It is used directly as an index into `colour_table` without checking that it is less than `colour_table_size`. The local colour table size can be as small as 2 entries (when the lower 3 bits of flags are 0, `colour_table_size = 2 << 0 = 2`). A malicious GIF can craft LZW data that produces colour index values larger than the colour table, causing an out-of-bounds read. This can leak heap/stack memory or cause a crash. The same issue applies to `colour_table[gif->background_index]` in the clear-image path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion via disposal method chains can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function recursively calls itself to handle frame disposal methods (GIF_FRAME_CLEAR and GIF_FRAME_RESTORE). A crafted GIF with a long chain of frames whose disposal methods reference previous frames can cause deep recursion, exhausting the stack. For GIF_FRAME_RESTORE, the code walks back through frames but then recursively decodes a found frame, which itself may trigger further recursion. There is no recursion depth limit, enabling a denial-of-service via stack overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error in frame bounds check allows access to one-past-valid frame
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check uses `>` instead of `>=`. If `frame_count_partial` represents the count of frames (with valid indices 0 through frame_count_partial-1), then `frame == frame_count_partial` passes the check but accesses `gif->frames[frame]` out of bounds. This can lead to out-of-bounds reads on the frames array, potentially causing crashes or information disclosure.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in marker length calculation leads to oversized read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the JPEG marker length field is 0 or 1, subtracting 2 from the unsigned short causes an integer underflow, wrapping `len` to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFE or 0xFFFF). This causes `fread` to attempt to read up to 65535 bytes. While this fits in the stack buffer, it reads far more data than intended from the file, and subsequent parsing logic operates on uncontrolled data. A crafted JPEG file can trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in 0xFFDA marker parsing due to unchecked data[0] offset
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 0xFFDA case, `data[0]` is used as a multiplier to compute array indices (`1+data[0]*2` and `3+data[0]*2`). There is no validation that these computed indices are within the bounds of the actually-read `len` bytes. An attacker can craft a JPEG with a small marker length but a large `data[0]` value, causing reads of uninitialized stack memory, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing incorrect program behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in calloc size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both `jh->wide` (up to 65535) and `jh->clrs` (attacker-influenced) are derived from file data. Their multiplication `jh->wide*jh->clrs` is performed as an int and can overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent code that writes to `jh->row` assuming the full intended size will overflow the heap buffer, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: SOF marker parsing accesses fixed offsets without verifying len is sufficient
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 0xFFC0/0xFFC3 cases access data[0] through data[7] without verifying that `len` is at least 8. A crafted JPEG with a short SOF marker length could result in reading beyond the actually-read data in the stack buffer, accessing uninitialized stack memory. This could lead to incorrect values for image dimensions and other parameters, or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Writing to a buffer declared as const via cast, causing undefined behavior if memory is read-only
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parser receives the buffer as `const char *buffer`, establishing a contract that it will not modify the buffer. However, `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)` casts away the const qualifier and writes to the buffer in-place when processing HTTP header field names. If the caller provides a buffer backed by read-only memory (e.g., mmap'd read-only, or a string literal), this results in undefined behavior, potentially crashing the process or corrupting memory. An attacker who can send HTTP requests with header fields can trigger this write path.

code_snippets: assert(off <= len && "offset past end of buffer");
vulnerabilities.short_description: Safety-critical bounds checks rely on assert() which is disabled in release builds
vulnerabilities.long_description: All bounds validation—including that `off <= len`, that pointers don't overflow, that `nread <= len`, `body_start <= len`, `mark < len`, and `field_start < len`—is performed exclusively via `assert()` macros. When compiled with `-DNDEBUG` (standard for release/production builds), all these checks are removed. If `off > len`, then `p = buffer + off` points past the end of the buffer while `pe = buffer + len` points before `p`, leading to out-of-bounds reads or pointer arithmetic wrapping. Similarly, the post-parsing assertions that guard against buffer overflow, excessive nread, and out-of-bounds marks/field_starts provide no protection in production. An attacker supplying crafted input could exploit the absence of these checks to read out-of-bounds memory.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

vulnerabilities.short_description: USB device reference leak on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the error path jumps to err_free which only calls kfree(priv) but does not call usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) to release the reference acquired by usb_get_dev(). This leads to a reference count leak on the USB device, preventing it from ever being freed and causing a resource leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated probe failures (e.g., via crafted USB devices) could exhaust kernel memory over time.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient bounds check leads to out-of-bounds read on entry_table for moveable bundles
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check at the top of the for-loop only verifies that `tableat + off + 4` is within bounds. However, in the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), the loop body advances `off` by 6 bytes total (1 for flags, 2 for INT 3Fh, 1 for segnum, 2 for segoff), reading memory at each step. This means the code can read up to 2 bytes past the validated boundary of bin->entry_table, causing a heap buffer over-read. An attacker crafting a malicious NE binary could trigger this to leak information or crash the application.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on segment_entries array using unchecked segnum index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the moveable bundle case, `segnum` is read directly from the entry table and used to index `bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1]` with only a check that `segnum > 0`. There is no upper-bound check against the actual number of segment entries (e.g., bin->ne_header->SegCount). A crafted NE binary could set segnum to a large value, causing an out-of-bounds read on the segment_entries array, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one in fixed bundle segment index check allows out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: For fixed bundles, the check is `bundle_type < bin->ne_header->SegCount`, but bundle_type is used as a 1-based segment index and accessed as `segment_entries[bundle_type - 1]`. When bundle_type equals SegCount - 1, the code accesses the last valid entry, which is correct. However, if SegCount is 0 (malformed binary), the comparison of an unsigned bundle_type < 0 would never be true, so that specific case is safe. The more concerning issue is that when bundle_type is 0 it should never reach this branch, but if SegCount happens to be 0 and bundle_type is also 0, the subtraction `bundle_type - 1` wraps around for array indexing. More critically, no check ensures `off` is within `entry_table` bounds when reading `*(ut16 *)(bin->entry_table + off)` here.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of segments list on entry allocation failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the allocation of `entry` via R_NEW0 fails inside the `csEntryPoint` block, the function frees `entries` and returns NULL, but does not free the `segments` list that was allocated earlier via `r_bin_ne_get_segments`. This causes a memory leak. Similarly, the error path inside the for-loop that frees entries also leaks the segments list.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of rebase_info on all error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `rebase_info` object is allocated via `r_rebase_info_new_from_mach0` early in the function, but none of the error paths (goto beach) free it. The `beach` label only frees `fbuf` and `main_mach0`, leaving `rebase_info` leaked. Since this function processes potentially untrusted binary input, an attacker could craft malformed input that triggers repeated failures after `rebase_info` allocation, leading to resource exhaustion / denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Complex parsed structure freed with plain free() instead of proper destructor
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `prelink_info` object is created by `r_cf_value_dict_parse`, which returns a complex `RCFValueDict` structure likely containing nested allocations (child dictionaries, arrays, strings, etc.). In the error path for `!pending_bin_files`, it is freed using `R_FREE` which is a simple `free()` wrapper. This fails to release all internal/child allocations, resulting in memory leaks. More critically, if the structure's destructor performs additional cleanup (e.g., reference counting), using the wrong free mechanism can lead to inconsistent state.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in moveable entry table parsing due to insufficient bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the moveable bundle branch (bundle_type == 0xff), the offset is incremented by 1+2+1=4 before performing r_read_le16 at the new offset, which reads 2 bytes (off+4 and off+5 from the original). However, the bounds check at the top of the for-loop only ensures 'tableat + off + 4 >= buf_size', i.e., it guarantees access up to off+3 relative to the entry of the iteration. The r_read_le16 call at off+4 reads beyond the verified bound, causing an out-of-bounds read on bin->entry_table. Additionally, the bounds checks mix the file buffer size (r_buf_size) with accesses to the separately allocated bin->entry_table buffer, so there is no guarantee the entry_table allocation is large enough for the offsets being used.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of allocated RBinAddr entry when breaking out of parsing loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the moveable (0xff) and fixed bundle type branches, an RBinAddr 'entry' is allocated via R_NEW0 at the start of each loop iteration. If the subsequent bounds checks trigger a 'break', the already-allocated 'entry' is never freed, resulting in a memory leak. This occurs in at least two places: the 'off > EntryTableLength' check in the moveable branch and the 'off + 2 >= EntryTableLength' check in the fixed branch.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'segments' list on entry allocation failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: When R_NEW0(RBinAddr) fails inside the for-loop, the function frees 'entries' and returns NULL, but it does not free the 'segments' list that was obtained from r_bin_ne_get_segments(). This results in a memory leak of the segments list and all its contained elements.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds array access when bundle_type is greater than 0 but the subtraction underflows or index is invalid
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the fixed bundle branch, the code checks 'bundle_type < bin->ne_header->SegCount' but does not verify that bundle_type > 0 (the bundle_type == 0 check happens earlier and breaks, but if bundle_type were somehow 0 in an edge case, bundle_type - 1 would underflow). More importantly, even though a SegCount check exists, there is no validation that segment_entries array actually has SegCount entries allocated, which could lead to an out-of-bounds read if the NE header metadata is malformed.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in ne parser ##crash

* Reported by @cnitlrt via huntr.dev
* BountyID: d8b6d239-6d7b-4783-b26b-5be848c01aa1/
* Reproducer: nenull
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of making the radare2 crash, thus affecting the availability of the system.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in substitution buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `needed_len` is declared as `int`, but its value is computed from a sum of multiple unsigned/length values (`copy_len`, `STRLEN(p1)`, `sublen`, and later `len`). If these components are large enough (e.g., with very long lines or substitution strings), `needed_len` can overflow the signed `int` range, wrapping to a negative value. When the negative `needed_len` is compared against `(int)new_start_len`, the check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` will be false, causing the reallocation to be skipped. The subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` calls will then write data beyond the bounds of the `new_start` buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. This can be exploited by crafting a file or substitute command with sufficiently long lines/patterns to corrupt heap memory, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed/unsigned comparison mismatch between needed_len (int) and new_start_len (unsigned) can bypass buffer reallocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The comparison `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` casts the unsigned `new_start_len` to a signed int. If `new_start_len` exceeds `INT_MAX` (possible since it is `unsigned` and can grow with `needed_len + 50`), the cast `(int)new_start_len` produces a negative number, making the comparison always true regardless of actual sizes. More critically, if `needed_len` is negative due to integer overflow, this comparison will fail to detect that reallocation is needed. Both conditions can result in out-of-bounds writes to the heap-allocated `new_start` buffer.

Fix null deref in ne parser ##crash

* Reported by @cnitlrt via huntr.dev

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in substitution buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables `needed_len`, `copy_len`, and `len` are declared as `int`, but are computed from `unsigned` values (via STRLEN casts and additions). If the substitution involves very large strings, the arithmetic can overflow the signed `int`, causing `needed_len` to wrap to a negative value. This would bypass the buffer reallocation check (`needed_len > (int)new_start_len`), resulting in subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` calls writing beyond the allocated `new_start` buffer. Additionally, casting `new_start_len` (which is `unsigned`) to `int` in the comparison can produce incorrect results if `new_start_len` exceeds INT_MAX. An attacker could craft a substitute command with a pattern and replacement string that triggers this overflow, achieving arbitrary heap corruption and potentially code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned to signed cast in buffer size comparison can bypass reallocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer tracking variable `new_start_len` is `unsigned int` but is cast to `int` in the comparison `needed_len > (int)new_start_len`. If `new_start_len` grows beyond INT_MAX (2^31 - 1 on 32-bit int platforms), the cast to `int` produces a negative value, making the comparison always true in unexpected ways, or conversely if `needed_len` has overflowed to negative, a legitimately large `new_start_len` cast to negative int could make the check pass incorrectly. This type confusion between signed and unsigned integers in security-critical size checks can lead to undersized buffer allocations and subsequent heap buffer overflows during the substitution copy operations.

commit_message:
misc: fastrpc: prevent memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20190925152742.16258-1-navid.emamdoost@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when expand_string fails for host_number_string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When host_number_string is not NULL, expand_string() is called on it, but the return value is used directly in Ustrtol() without checking for NULL. If the expansion fails, expand_string returns NULL, and Ustrtol will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash. Other expand_string calls in this function (e.g., for spool_directory, log_file_path, pid_file_path) correctly check for a NULL return value before using the result.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow when appending suffix to filename in big_buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'filename' points into 'big_buffer', and 'suffix' is set to the end of the filename string. The code then uses sprintf to append a suffix containing the euid and/or nodename. While the nodename is limited to 256 characters via the format specifier, the combined length of the original filename content plus the appended suffix (a dot, a long integer which can be up to ~20 digits, another dot, and up to 256 characters of nodename) could exceed big_buffer_size, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow. This could allow an attacker who controls the configuration file list or nodename to corrupt memory.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing outlen for charset encoding buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `outlen` is declared as `int` and computed as `len * 4`. Since `len` is `st64` and can be up to `ST32_MAX` (2147483647), the multiplication `len * 4` can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a negative or unexpectedly small value. This overflowed `outlen` is then passed to `r_charset_encode_str(ch, out, outlen, buf, len)` as the output buffer size limit. If the function trusts this value for bounds checking, it could lead to a heap buffer overflow in the `out` buffer (which was correctly allocated via `calloc(len, 4)`), since the encode function may believe the buffer is much smaller (or negative-sized) than it actually is, causing unpredictable behavior. An attacker who controls the RABIN2_CHARSET environment variable and the scanned binary could trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read during wide string type detection
vulnerabilities.long_description: The wide32 detection reads `w[0]` through `w[8]` (9 bytes past `needle + rc - from`). The guard `(to - needle) > 8 + rc` ensures `needle + rc + 8 < to`, meaning the access is within the logical range `[from, to)`. However, after a charset conversion where `len` is reassigned to `res` (the encode result), the meaningful data length in `buf` could be smaller than `to - from`, while accesses still use the original `to` bound. Although the allocated buffer is large enough (`calloc(len, 4)`), the reads would be on uninitialized/zeroed heap memory beyond the encoded data, potentially causing incorrect string type detection and logic errors. In the non-charset case, `is_wide32le` checks `w[5]`, `w[6]`, `w[7]`, `w[8]` while only the outer guard checked up to `w[8]` — the condition `needle + rc + 2 < to` for the first access is a weaker check than needed for accessing `w[8]`, relying on the outer condition being checked first, which makes the code fragile to refactoring.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

code_snippets:
old_len = (long)STRLEN(ptr);
new_line = alloc(old_len - col + start_col + len + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size for `new_line` is computed as `old_len - col + start_col + len + 1` using `long` arithmetic. On systems where `long` is 32-bit, if the line is very long and the replacement whitespace (`len = num_spaces + num_tabs`) is large enough, this sum can overflow, wrapping to a small or negative value. When cast to `size_t` for `alloc()`, a negative `long` becomes a huge value (likely failing), but a small positive wrapped value would succeed, allocating a tiny buffer. The subsequent `mch_memmove` calls would then write the full line content into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a file with extremely long lines containing tabs to trigger this, potentially achieving code execution.

commit_message:
Bug 701819: fixed ordering in if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -r965 -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=pcx16 ../bug-701819.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when dereferencing `from` after it may have advanced past `end`
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `from += step`, `from` may equal or exceed `end`, making it an invalid pointer. The condition then evaluates `data != *from` before checking `from == end` (due to left-to-right evaluation of `||`). This dereferences memory beyond the buffer boundary. An attacker providing crafted input data could exploit this to read sensitive heap/stack data or cause a crash. The check should be reordered to `from == end || data != *from` so that the bounds check short-circuits the dereference.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read when `step > 1` causes `from` to skip past `end`
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `step` is greater than 1, `from += step` inside the inner while loop can advance `from` past `end` without ever being equal to `end`. On the next iteration, `from < end` uses pointer comparison which may still appear valid for a wrapped or misaligned pointer, or `from` may land on an address past the buffer but before the unsigned wraparound, leading to out-of-bounds reads. The same issue applies to the outer loop's `while (from < end)` condition.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when appending node name and euid suffixes to filename in big_buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'suffix' points to the end of the filename within big_buffer. The subsequent sprintf calls append up to ~280 additional bytes (a long integer, a dot, and up to 256 characters of nodename). If the original filename already occupies most of big_buffer, writing these suffixes will overflow big_buffer. There is no bounds check ensuring that the combined length of the filename plus the appended suffix fits within big_buffer_size.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when expand_string fails for host_number_string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When host_number_string is non-NULL, expand_string() is called and its result is immediately passed to Ustrtol() without a NULL check. If expand_string() fails (returns NULL), Ustrtol() will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash. Other calls to expand_string() in this function properly check for a NULL return value before using the result.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0260: using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively

Problem:    Using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively.
Solution:   Do not allow for recursion.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0260.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ReadBlob size calculation may cause heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `depth/8*image->columns` can overflow when image->columns (derived from the untrusted HDR.nRows field) is large. For example, with depth=64 (depth/8=8) and image->columns near SIZE_MAX/8, the multiplication wraps around to a small value. ReadBlob would then read fewer bytes than expected, but more critically, in certain quantum configurations the pixel buffer allocated by AcquireQuantumInfo may be smaller than what ImportQuantumPixels expects to process based on image->columns, leading to out-of-bounds memory access. This can be triggered by a crafted MAT v4 file with large dimension values.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SeekBlob offset calculation during ping mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ping code path, `HDR.nCols*ldblk` is computed without overflow checking. HDR.nCols is an attacker-controlled unsigned int from the file, and ldblk is derived from the type field (up to 9999). On 32-bit systems or when the multiplication overflows the signed long type of ldblk, this can wrap around to a small or negative value, causing SeekBlob to seek to an incorrect position. Subsequent parsing iterations would then process arbitrary data in the file as new image headers, potentially leading to further memory corruption or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return values of SetQuantum* functions are overwritten without being checked
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of SetQuantumFormat is immediately overwritten by SetQuantumDepth, which is then overwritten by SetQuantumEndian, without ever checking if any of them failed. If SetQuantumFormat or SetQuantumDepth fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure or invalid parameters), the quantum_info object may be in an inconsistent state. The code then proceeds to use it for pixel import operations via GetQuantumPixels and ImportQuantumPixels, which could result in use of uninitialized or incorrectly sized buffers, leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes.

commit_message:
virstoragetest: testBackingParse: Use VIR_DOMAIN_DEF_FORMAT_SECURE when formatting xml

We want to format even the secure information in tests.

Signed-off-by: Peter Krempa <pkrempa@redhat.com>

commit_message:
misc: fastrpc: prevent memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20190925152742.16258-1-navid.emamdoost@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'a' when dma_get_sgtable fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated 'a' structure. This leads to a kernel memory leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated failures of dma_get_sgtable could exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA labels_data_count calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation `labels_data_count = (shadow_data->num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) + 2` uses uint32 arithmetic. If `shadow_data->num_volumes` (controlled by the VFS backend) is sufficiently large, this multiplication can wrap around to a small value. This causes `*out_len = 12 + labels_data_count` to also be small, so the subsequent size check against `max_out_len` passes and a small heap buffer is allocated via `talloc_zero_array`. However, the loop iterates `num_volumes` times, writing `2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes per iteration, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can influence the shadow copy data (e.g., through a malicious VFS module or crafted snapshot environment) could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size when retabulating lines with large virtual column spans
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size `old_len - col + start_col + len + 1` is computed using `long` arithmetic. When expanding tabs to spaces (`curbuf->b_p_et` is set), `len` equals `vcol - start_vcol`, which can approach MAXCOL (typically INT_MAX). Combined with `old_len`, the sum can overflow a 32-bit `long`, wrapping to a small positive value. This results in an undersized heap buffer allocation. The subsequent `mch_memmove` calls then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a file with many tab characters and trigger `:retab` with a large tabstop value to exploit this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution. The `vcol >= MAXCOL` check occurs only after the column is advanced, meaning the retabulation and allocation happen before this guard is evaluated for the relevant whitespace span.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, `labels_data_count` is computed as `(shadow_data->num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) + 2` using uint32 arithmetic. If `shadow_data->num_volumes` (controlled by the VFS shadow copy backend) is sufficiently large, this multiplication can wrap around to a small value. Consequently, `*out_len` would also be small, the size check against `max_out_len` would pass, and a small heap buffer would be allocated via `talloc_zero_array`. The subsequent loop then writes `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. This could allow code execution or denial of service if a malicious or compromised VFS backend returns a crafted `num_volumes` value.

commit_message:
selinux,smack: fix subjective/objective credential use mixups

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:
clarify task subjective and objective credentials") where some LSM
hooks were attempting to access the subjective credentials of a task
other than the current task.  Generally speaking, it is not safe to
access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause
a number of problems.

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was
suffering from a similar problem brought about by a similar commit
1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective
task credentials").

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's
objective credentials in those cases where the task is other than the
current executing task.  Not only does this resolve the problem
reported by Jann, it is arguably the correct thing to do in these
cases.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: eb1231f73c4d ("selinux: clarify task subjective and objective credentials")
Fixes: 1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective task credentials")
Reported-by: Jann Horn <jannh@google.com>
Acked-by: Eric W. Biederman <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Acked-by: Casey Schaufler <casey@schaufler-ca.com>
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.14.8. A use-after-free in selinux_ptrace_traceme (aka the SELinux handler for PTRACE_TRACEME) could be used by local attackers to cause memory corruption and escalate privileges, aka CID-a3727a8bac0a. This occurs because of an attempt to access the subjective credentials of another task.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ReadBlob size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `depth/8*image->columns` can overflow when `image->columns` (derived from the untrusted `HDR.nRows` field) is large. For example, with depth=64 (so depth/8=8) and image->columns near UINT_MAX, the multiplication wraps around on 32-bit size_t, resulting in a much smaller value being passed to ReadBlob than the actual buffer size that ImportQuantumPixels expects. This mismatch can lead to reading uninitialized heap data or, conversely, if the quantum pixel buffer was sized based on the overflowed value, writing past the buffer boundary during ImportQuantumPixels. An attacker can craft a MAT v4 file with large nRows to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in seek offset during ping mode leads to incorrect file position
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ping code path, `HDR.nCols*ldblk` is computed where HDR.nCols comes from untrusted file input (up to ~4 billion) and ldblk is derived from the type field (up to 9999). This multiplication can overflow the unsigned int / long range, causing SeekBlob to seek to an incorrect position. This could cause subsequent loop iterations to parse arbitrary file data as MAT headers, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or other memory corruption when processing subsequent 'images' from incorrect file offsets.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked large image dimensions from untrusted input may cause excessive memory allocation or overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: HDR.nRows and HDR.nCols are read directly from the file as unsigned 32-bit integers and assigned to image->columns and image->rows with only a zero check. Extremely large but non-zero values (e.g., close to UINT_MAX) can cause SetImageExtent to allocate enormous amounts of memory, or cause subsequent arithmetic operations (like `depth/8*image->columns`) to overflow. While SetImageExtent may perform some checks, the lack of reasonable upper-bound validation on these dimensions before use creates a denial-of-service vector and enables the integer overflow vulnerabilities in subsequent calculations.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets:
const int l = *decoded_size - pos;
                if (pos < 0 || l < 0 || s + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer over-read in the delete branch's memmove
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the delete branch (mod == 'd'), the length computed as `l = *decoded_size - pos` is one byte too many. The source pointer `s` is `decoded + pos + 1`, so `s + l` equals `decoded + *decoded_size + 1`, reading one byte past the valid decoded data. The bounds check compares against INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX (the buffer allocation size) rather than *decoded_size, so this over-read passes the check whenever the buffer has spare capacity. This results in reading uninitialized or stale data from the heap, which is then written back into the decoded buffer, potentially leaking information or causing data corruption.

code_snippets: debug_print("Out of buffer in %s at pos: %i\n", decoded, pos);
debug_print("Character mismatch in %s at pos: %i (%c != %c)\n", decoded, pos, decoded[pos], c);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Passing a non-null-terminated unsigned char buffer to printf-style %s format specifier
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `decoded` buffer is a raw byte buffer whose length is tracked by `*decoded_size`, with no guarantee of null termination. When an error condition is hit, `decoded` is passed to `debug_print` with a `%s` format specifier, which will read memory until a null byte is found. This can cause an out-of-bounds read well past the allocated buffer (INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX), potentially crashing the program or leaking sensitive heap data. An attacker who can craft malicious input to trigger these error paths could exploit this for information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed integer underflow on pos can lead to out-of-bounds memory access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `pos` is a signed int that can be decremented by up to 9 (`pos -= c - 10` with c up to 19) and further decremented by 1 in the delete path (`pos--`) before the bounds check `pos < 0` is reached. While the bounds check exists in the insert/delete branches, if multiple position-adjustment commands (c in range 11-19) are processed consecutively without an intervening insert/delete, pos can accumulate a large negative value. In the delete branch, the subsequent `pos--` could then cause signed integer overflow (undefined behavior in C), and the computed length `l = *decoded_size - pos` could wrap to a negative value that, when cast to `size_t`, becomes a very large positive value passed to memmove, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

cve_desc: An information leak flaw was found due to uninitialized memory in the Linux kernel's TIPC protocol subsystem, in the way a user sends a TIPC datagram to one or more destinations. This flaw allows a local user to read some kernel memory. This issue is limited to no more than 7 bytes, and the user cannot control what is read. This flaw affects the Linux kernel versions prior to 5.17-rc1.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of rsi_dev on error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: When any error occurs (fail_eps or fail_rx), the function returns without freeing the `rsi_dev` structure allocated via `kzalloc` at the beginning. All error paths jump to `fail_eps` which does not call `kfree(rsi_dev)`, resulting in a kernel memory leak. An attacker could trigger this repeatedly (e.g., by plugging in a crafted USB device that causes endpoint discovery to fail) to exhaust kernel memory.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in ncurses through v6.2-1. _nc_captoinfo in captoinfo.c has a heap-based buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Never follow symlinks when setting file flags on Linux

When opening a file descriptor to set file flags on linux, ensure
no symbolic links are followed. This fixes the case when an archive
contains a directory entry followed by a symlink entry with the same
path. The fixup code would modify file flags of the symlink target.
cve_desc: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.
