commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: usb_set_intfdata stores pointer to dev that is later freed on error without clearing it
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) to associate the device struct with the USB interface before calling em28xx_init_dev(). If em28xx_init_dev() or subsequent operations fail, the code jumps to err_free which frees dev via kfree(dev), but never calls usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL) to clear the dangling pointer. This means the USB interface retains a pointer to freed memory. If the USB subsystem later accesses the interface data (e.g., during disconnect), it will dereference freed memory, leading to a use-after-free condition. An attacker with physical access could trigger this by using a specially crafted USB device that causes initialization to fail.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete cleanup on error: em28xx_init_dev resources and dev_next are not properly released on failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the second em28xx_init_dev() call (for dev->dev_next in dual-tuner mode) fails, the error path jumps to err_free which only frees dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc and dev itself. It does not undo the successful first em28xx_init_dev() call, does not free dev->dev_next or its resources, and does not free dev->dev_next->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc. Similarly, if operations after the first successful em28xx_init_dev() fail, the initialization done by em28xx_init_dev() is never torn down. This leads to resource leaks and potentially dangling references within the driver subsystem.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in malloc size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `get_st32` returns a large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF), `n1 + 1` or `n2 + 1` wraps around to 0 due to unsigned integer overflow. `malloc(0)` may return a small or zero-size buffer. The subsequent `rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1)` then writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into this tiny buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker crafting a malicious pyc file can control `n1`/`n2` to trigger arbitrary heap corruption and potentially achieve code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leaks of s1 and ret on multiple error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: After allocating `s1` and `ret`, several error paths return NULL without freeing them: (1) when the second `get_ut8`/`get_st32` call for `n2` fails with error, both `s1` and `ret` are leaked; (2) when `malloc(n2 + 1)` for `s2` fails, both `s1` and `ret` are leaked. Additionally, the first `malloc(n1+1)` failure path leaks `ret`. While primarily a resource issue, repeated exploitation in long-running processes could lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via EDITOR/VISUAL environment variable
vulnerabilities.long_description: The EDITOR or VISUAL environment variable is passed to system() without any sanitization. An attacker who can control these environment variables can inject arbitrary shell commands. For example, setting EDITOR to 'vi; rm -rf /' would execute the injected command. The code only falls through to the system() path when the editor string contains a space, but a malicious value like 'cmd --evil' would trigger this path and be executed by the shell.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Temporary file created in /tmp is vulnerable to race conditions between close and reopen
vulnerabilities.long_description: The file is created securely with mkstemp, but is then closed and later reopened with fopen(filename, "r"). Between the close and the fopen, an attacker could replace the file via a symlink or other attack (TOCTOU race condition), causing the program to read attacker-controlled content into pi->data.s.body.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsafe cast of long to int for allocation size may cause integer truncation/overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The file length is obtained as a long from ftell(), but is cast to int when passed to omAlloc. If the file is larger than INT_MAX, this cast truncates the value, potentially allocating a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent myfread call uses the original long len, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak and incorrect assignment of NULL to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: When pi->language != LANG_SINGULAR, the file is closed but the temporary file is never unlinked before returning (execution falls through to si_unlink at the end, but the filename variable is freed). Also, assigning NULL to an int variable f is semantically wrong. More critically, the early-error path closes the fd but doesn't return, so execution continues into the else-less path and falls through to si_unlink/omFree, but the temporary file content is never written, which is a logic error rather than a direct security issue.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when formatting PID into fixed-size filename string
vulnerabilities.long_description: The string "/tmp/sd000000" is 13 characters (+1 null = 14 bytes). Writing starts at offset 7, leaving 6 characters for the PID and null terminator. On systems where PIDs can exceed 99999 (e.g., Linux with pid_max up to 4194304, which is 7 digits), sprintf will write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. This can lead to heap corruption, crashes, or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insecure temporary file creation with predictable name in /tmp
vulnerabilities.long_description: The temporary file is created in /tmp with a predictable name based on the PID. This is vulnerable to symlink attacks (TOCTOU race condition): an attacker can create a symlink at the predicted path pointing to a sensitive file (e.g., /etc/passwd), and when the program writes to or reads from this file, it will operate on the attacker's target. The file is opened without O_EXCL or similar safeguards.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Command injection via EDITOR environment variable passed to system()
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the EDITOR environment variable contains spaces, the code passes it concatenated with the filename to system(), which invokes a shell. A malicious user can set EDITOR to something like 'rm -rf / ;' or include shell metacharacters, leading to arbitrary command execution. Even when execlp is used (no spaces in EDITOR), the environment variable is still user-controlled, but the system() path is particularly dangerous as it enables full shell injection.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation casting long to int for allocation size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The file length is stored as a long but cast to int when passed to omAlloc. If the file is larger than INT_MAX bytes, the cast truncates the value, potentially resulting in a very small or negative allocation. The subsequent myfread then writes the full file content into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: usb_set_intfdata stores pointer to dev which is then freed in error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) to store a pointer to the allocated device structure in the USB interface data. If em28xx_init_dev() or subsequent operations fail, the error path err_free frees dev with kfree(dev), but never clears the interface data pointer (usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)). This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface structure. If anything subsequently accesses the interface data (e.g., during disconnect callbacks or other USB subsystem operations), it will dereference freed memory, potentially leading to code execution or kernel crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete cleanup when second em28xx_init_dev fails: dev_next and first device's initialized resources are leaked
vulnerabilities.long_description: When em28xx_duplicate_dev succeeds and the second em28xx_init_dev (for dev->dev_next) fails, the error path err_free only frees dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc and dev itself. It does not clean up resources initialized by the first successful em28xx_init_dev call, does not free dev->dev_next or its resources, and does not clear the bit for dev->dev_next->devno in em28xx_devused. This leads to resource leaks and potentially leaves the first device in a partially initialized but unreachable state.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of media_device_register is ignored, function returns success on failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value from media_device_register() is assigned to retval but is never checked. The function unconditionally returns 0 (success) regardless of whether media_device_register() failed. This means the probe function reports success even when the media device could not be registered, leaving the device in an inconsistent state where some subsystems expect a registered media device that doesn't exist.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on palette array due to insufficient index validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the packed pixels and palette RLE code paths, the decoded index used to access the `palette[128]` array is not validated against `palSize`. In packed pixels, when palSize > 16, bppp is 8, allowing index values 0-127, but only `palSize` entries (e.g., 17) were populated. In palette RLE, `index &= 127` allows values up to 127 regardless of palSize. Accessing `palette[index]` where `index >= palSize` reads uninitialized stack memory. An attacker crafting a malicious ZRLE-encoded stream can trigger reads of uninitialized stack data, potentially leaking sensitive information (e.g., stack pointers, canaries) into the framebuffer visible to the user, or causing unpredictable behavior.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3564: invalid memory access when scrolling without valid screen

Problem:    Invalid memory access when scrolling without a valid screen.
Solution:   Do not set VALID_BOTLINE in w_valid.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of palette array in packed-pixels mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the packed-pixels decoding path, the computed index can be larger than palSize. For example, when palSize is 17, bppp is 8, meaning index can be 0–127, but the palette only has 17 valid entries. The mask `& 127` does not restrict the index to the actual palette size. Reading palette[index] with an out-of-range index reads uninitialized stack memory, which could leak sensitive data or cause undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of palette array in palette-RLE mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the palette RLE decoding path, `index` is masked to 0–127 but the palette array may contain far fewer valid entries (palSize can be as small as 2). A malicious server can send an index value that, after masking, exceeds palSize, causing an out-of-bounds read from the stack-allocated `palette[128]` array. This can leak stack data or be used as a primitive in a larger exploit chain.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow when reading stream length as signed int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The length is read as an unsigned 32-bit value via readU32() but stored in a signed `int`. Values above INT_MAX (0x7FFFFFFF) will result in a negative value being passed to setUnderlying(), which could bypass length checks or cause unexpected behavior in the zlib decompression stream, potentially leading to memory corruption or denial of service.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion can lead to stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses unbounded recursion to perform flood fill. For large images or adversarial inputs, the recursive calls scanning rows above and below can exhaust the call stack, causing a stack overflow and crash (denial of service). An attacker supplying a crafted image with large fillable regions can trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds validation on initial x and y parameters
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function does not validate that the initial x and y parameters are within the image bounds (0 <= x < im->sx, 0 <= y < im->sy) before using them in gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel calls. If x or y is negative or exceeds image dimensions, the initial pixel accesses occur out of bounds, potentially causing heap buffer overflow or out-of-bounds read/write.

patch 8.2.3564: invalid memory access when scrolling without valid screen

Problem:    Invalid memory access when scrolling without a valid screen.
Solution:   Do not set VALID_BOTLINE in w_valid.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded recursion can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses recursive calls to fill scanlines above and below the current line. For large images or adversarial patterns (e.g., a checkerboard-like arrangement that maximizes recursion depth), this can lead to extremely deep recursion, exhausting the stack and causing a crash (denial of service). An attacker who controls the image content and the fill parameters could craft an image that triggers this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation for negative x and y coordinates leads to out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function clamps x and y when they exceed image dimensions but does not check for negative values. If x or y is negative, the function proceeds to call gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel with negative coordinates, which can result in out-of-bounds memory reads and writes. An attacker providing negative coordinates could exploit this to read or corrupt memory outside the image buffer.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size when processing .gpb pattern data
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a pattern follows the brush data (obsolete .gpb format, bh.bytes == 1), the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes. The original sanitization check validates `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with bh.bytes=1, meaning bh.width * bh.height can be close to G_MAXSIZE. When this is multiplied by 4, it can overflow the integer type, resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. Subsequent writes into this undersized buffer (copying 4 bytes per pixel for width*height pixels) cause a heap buffer overflow, which can lead to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer truncation of header_size difference used for allocation and read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `bn_size` is declared as `gint` (signed 32-bit), but `bh.header_size` is an unsigned 32-bit value from the file. If `bh.header_size` is very large (e.g., close to UINT32_MAX), the subtraction `bh.header_size - sizeof(BrushHeader)` produces a large unsigned value that, when stored in the signed `gint bn_size`, could wrap or truncate. While the `> 0` check filters some cases, on platforms where `sizeof(BrushHeader)` is smaller than 32 bits of range, a carefully crafted header_size could produce a small positive bn_size that passes the check but doesn't match the actual header data, leading to incorrect parsing and potential information disclosure or further exploitation.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow of chain reference counter (use count) can lead to use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: The chain->use reference counter is incremented without any overflow check. An attacker who can repeatedly create rules with NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO verdicts referencing the same chain can eventually overflow the 'use' counter (e.g., wrapping a u32 back to 0). Once overflowed, the kernel would believe the chain has no references and allow it to be freed, while existing rules still hold pointers to it. This leads to a use-after-free condition that can be exploited for privilege escalation. This is a known class of vulnerability in the Linux nftables subsystem.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation when appending pattern data to brush
vulnerabilities.long_description: When bh.bytes is 1 and a valid pattern follows (the .gpb format path), the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes. However, the original sanitization check `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` was performed with bh.bytes=1, only ensuring width*height fits in gsize. The new allocation multiplies by 4, which can overflow, resulting in a small heap allocation. The subsequent loop then writes up to `4 * width * height` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a .gpb file with large width/height values that pass the original check but overflow when multiplied by 4.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak when header sanitization fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the header field validation fails (invalid width, height, bytes, or overflow check), the function returns -1 without calling close(fd). This leaks the open file descriptor. While not directly exploitable for code execution, repeated triggering of this path (e.g., processing many malformed files) can exhaust file descriptors, leading to denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Comparison of ssize_t return value against gsize may always be unequal for large sizes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `read()` system call returns `ssize_t`, while `size` is of type `gsize` (unsigned). If `size` exceeds `SSIZE_MAX`, `read()` may return -1 or a truncated value, but the comparison with the unsigned `size` would behave unexpectedly due to signed/unsigned mismatch. On 32-bit systems where GIMP_MAX_IMAGE_SIZE is large, this could allow a short read to go undetected or cause the check to be unreliable, potentially leading to use of uninitialized heap data.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will be at most BASENAMELEN characters long. However, `readdir()` can return filenames longer than BASENAMELEN (on many filesystems, filenames can be up to 255 bytes, and BASENAMELEN may be smaller). When `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies the directory entry name into `buf`, it can write past the end of the allocated buffer. Subsequently, `STRCPY(buf + len, "/**")` and `STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end)` append additional data, further exacerbating the overflow. An attacker who can create files with long names in a directory being glob-expanded could trigger this heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in loop counter causes near-infinite loop when stack_rev is true and to is 0
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop uses `i--` on a `size_t` (unsigned) variable and checks `i >= to`. If `to` is 0 (which happens when `fcn->cc` is NULL), the condition `i >= 0` is always true for an unsigned type. When `i` reaches 0 and is decremented, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX, and the condition remains true, causing an effectively infinite loop. This leads to out-of-bounds reads from `r_type_func_args_type`/`r_type_func_args_name` with huge index values, potentially crashing the application or leaking memory contents. An attacker could trigger this by crafting a binary where the analysis path reaches this code with `stack_rev` true and a NULL calling convention.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer 'buf' is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN characters. However, on many filesystems (e.g., ext4 allows 255 bytes), d_name can be up to NAME_MAX (typically 255) bytes, while BASENAMELEN may be smaller. When `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies a directory entry name into buf, and especially when the starstar path appends additional characters ("/**" plus path_end), the write can exceed the allocated buffer size. This heap-based buffer overflow can be triggered by crafting directory entries with long filenames, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size for LWPolyline vertices
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed using int arithmetic. If `maxVertices` (parsed from untrusted input via `toInt(groupValue)`) is large enough (e.g., greater than INT_MAX/4), the multiplication overflows, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent initialization loops and indexed writes use the original `maxVertices` value, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious DXF file with a large vertex count in group code 90 to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write due to unchecked groupCode-derived index
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `groupCode` is 10, 20, or 30, the index offset is computed as `groupCode/10-1`, yielding 0, 1, or 2 respectively. However, the bounds check only validates `vertexIndex` against `maxVertices`, not the overall array index `4*vertexIndex + offset`. Combined with the integer overflow vulnerability in the allocation, even within normal bounds the write can exceed the allocated buffer. Additionally, if `maxVertices` overflowed during allocation, writes at valid `vertexIndex` values will be far beyond the actual allocation, corrupting heap memory.

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: maxVertices is derived from user input (toInt(groupValue)). When maxVertices is large (e.g., close to INT_MAX/4 or SIZE_MAX/sizeof(double)/4), the expression `4*maxVertices` can overflow, causing a much smaller buffer to be allocated. The subsequent initialization loop and later vertex writes use the original (large) maxVertices as the bound, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a DXF file with a large vertex count to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when vertexIndex is -1 with groupCode 42
vulnerabilities.long_description: vertexIndex is initialized to -1 after allocation. If a group code 42 record appears before any group code 10 record, vertexIndex remains -1. The check `vertexIndex<maxVertices` passes (since -1 < any positive number), but `vertices[4*(-1) + 3]` computes to `vertices[-1]`, writing before the allocated buffer. This is an out-of-bounds heap write that can be triggered by a crafted DXF file and may lead to arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in reverse stack argument loop causes out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `stack_rev` is true and `to` is 0, the loop variable `i` (of type `size_t`, which is unsigned) will underflow when it reaches 0 and is decremented. After wrapping to SIZE_MAX, the condition `i >= to` (i.e., SIZE_MAX >= 0) remains true, causing the loop to iterate an enormous number of times. An attacker who can influence function metadata (e.g., calling conventions where `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0) can trigger this condition.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer underflow and out-of-bounds write if i_lenAlloc exceeds PAGE_SIZE
vulnerabilities.long_description: If iinfo->i_lenAlloc is greater than PAGE_SIZE (e.g., due to a corrupted on-disk inode), the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` underflows to a very large value (both are unsigned), causing memset to write far beyond the mapped page buffer. Additionally, `kaddr + iinfo->i_lenAlloc` would already point past the page boundary. An attacker crafting a malicious UDF filesystem image could trigger this to corrupt kernel memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow in error recovery path using inode->i_size as copy length
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path (when filemap_fdatawrite fails), the code copies `inode->i_size` bytes from the page into `iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr`. The buffer `iinfo->i_data` is a fixed-size inline area within the inode structure. If `inode->i_size` is larger than the available space (`sizeof(iinfo->i_data) - iinfo->i_lenEAttr`), this memcpy overflows the buffer, corrupting adjacent kernel heap memory. A crafted filesystem or race condition could exploit this for privilege escalation.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to wrong index variable in namespace deduplication loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the inner loop checking for duplicate namespace declarations among attributes, the code uses index 'j' to access element->attributes[j] instead of the outer loop's index 'i' (element->attributes[i]). Since 'j' iterates up to nspace_declarations_count, which can exceed element->attribute_count (it includes the element's own namespace declaration and previously processed attributes/namespaces), this can read past the end of the element->attributes array. An attacker providing crafted XML with multiple attributes and namespace declarations could trigger a heap out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when escaping xml:lang value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer for xml:lang is allocated as XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1 + 1 bytes total. After writing the 'xml:lang="' prefix (10 bytes), the remaining space is lang_len + 2 bytes. However, raptor_xml_escape_string is called with buf_length (= XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1) as the output size limit, which is larger than the actual remaining space. Furthermore, XML escaping can expand characters (e.g., '<' becomes '<', '&' becomes '&'), so the escaped output can be significantly larger than lang_len. This leads to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the xml:lang attribute value with characters requiring escaping can overwrite adjacent heap memory, potentially achieving code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if xml:lang buffer allocation fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: After allocating the buffer for the xml:lang attribute with RAPTOR_MALLOC, the code immediately uses it in memcpy without checking if the allocation returned NULL. If memory allocation fails, this results in a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash. While other allocations in this function are properly checked, this one is missing the NULL check and error handling.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via wrong index variable `j` used to index `element->attributes[]`
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the loop that checks for earlier namespace declarations, the code uses `element->attributes[j]` instead of `element->attributes[i]`. As `j` iterates up to `nspace_declarations_count` (which can exceed `element->attribute_count`), this results in an out-of-bounds read on the `element->attributes` array. An attacker providing crafted XML with multiple attributes and declared namespaces can trigger this, causing information disclosure or a crash.

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when escaping xml:lang value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer for the xml:lang attribute is allocated based on the raw length of `element->xml_language` (`XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1 + 1` bytes). However, `raptor_xml_escape_string` can expand characters (e.g., `&` becomes `&`, `<` becomes `<`), producing output significantly longer than the input. The available space parameter passed (`buf_length`) also incorrectly accounts for the prefix already written. If the xml_language string contains characters that expand during XML escaping, the write will overflow the heap buffer, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if malloc fails for xml:lang buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of `RAPTOR_MALLOC` for the `buffer` in the xml_language handling block is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, the subsequent `memcpy` dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service). Other allocation failures in the function are properly checked and handled via `goto error`.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when i_lenAlloc exceeds PAGE_SIZE due to missing bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value of iinfo->i_lenAlloc is used without validation that it is less than or equal to PAGE_SIZE. If a corrupted or malicious UDF filesystem image provides an i_lenAlloc value larger than PAGE_SIZE, the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` underflows (both are unsigned), producing a very large value for memset. Additionally, the subsequent memcpy copies i_lenAlloc bytes into a single page buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker crafting a malicious UDF image could exploit this to achieve kernel code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write in error recovery path using unbounded inode->i_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path (when filemap_fdatawrite fails), inode->i_size is used as the copy length into iinfo->i_data without any bounds check. The i_data buffer in the inode info structure has a fixed size. If inode->i_size is larger than the available space in i_data (after i_lenEAttr offset), this memcpy will write beyond the i_data buffer, corrupting adjacent kernel heap memory. This could be triggered by a crafted filesystem image and could lead to privilege escalation.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect post-match string size calculation in backward search leads to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: When performing a backward search, the size assigned to the post-match substring (op1) is calculated as `count + size - 1`, which is incorrect. At the point a match is found during backward iteration, `count` equals the number of remaining loop iterations (N - i for match at iteration i), and the actual post-match size should be `i` (the number of bytes after the match to the end of the haystack). However, the formula `count + size - 1` yields `N - i + size - 1`, which is significantly larger than the correct value in most cases. For example, searching backward for "X" in "abcXdefXghi" finds the last 'X' at index 7 with count=7, yielding a post-match size of 7 when only 3 bytes ('ghi') remain. This causes the resulting PostScript string object to reference memory well beyond the end of the original haystack buffer, enabling an out-of-bounds read when the string is subsequently accessed. An attacker could exploit this to leak sensitive heap/stack data or potentially cause a crash.

PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: strchr called on non-null-terminated buffer, reading uninitialized stack memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies `nbytes` bytes from `buf` into the stack buffer `drc_name` using `memcpy`, which does not null-terminate the destination. It then calls `strchr(drc_name, '\n')` which scans for a character until it finds the target or a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is not null-terminated after the `memcpy`, `strchr` will read past the copied data into uninitialized stack memory. This could cause an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, if `strchr` finds a '\n' byte in the uninitialized stack region beyond the copied data, the `end` pointer and subsequent null-termination will be incorrect, and `drc_name` could contain uninitialized data that is then passed to `dlpar_add_slot`. The fix would be to null-terminate `drc_name` before calling `strchr`, e.g., `drc_name[nbytes] = '\0';`.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Option payload not drained from socket before continuing, causing protocol desynchronization
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the client lacks NBD_FLAG_FIXED_NEWSTYLE support and sends an option other than NBD_OPT_EXPORT_NAME, or when in FORCEDTLS mode and the client sends a non-TLS/non-ABORT option, the code sends an error reply and continues to the next loop iteration without reading the `optlen` bytes of option payload from the socket. This leaves attacker-controlled data in the receive buffer. On the next iteration, `conn_recv_full(&new_option, ...)` will read this leftover payload data as if it were a new option header. An attacker can craft the payload so that the desynchronized read interprets it as a valid option header with arbitrary option type and length, potentially leading to further exploitation such as bypassing TLS requirements, triggering unintended code paths, or causing out-of-bounds memory access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT is sent multiple times
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT, the code assigns a new strndup'd string to `conn->exportname_from_set_meta_context` without first freeing any previously allocated value. A malicious client can repeatedly send NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT options (up to MAX_NR_OPTIONS times per connection), each time leaking memory. This constitutes a denial-of-service vector where an attacker could exhaust server memory by opening many connections and sending repeated SET_META_CONTEXT options.

red fixes (#468)

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow via unbounded strcpy into fixed-size sun_path field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies the user-supplied pipePath string into local.sun_path using strcpy without checking that the string fits within the fixed-size sun_path buffer (typically 104 or 108 bytes). If pipePath exceeds this size, a stack buffer overflow occurs, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution or crash.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: strchr called on a non-null-terminated buffer leading to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies 'nbytes' bytes from 'buf' into the stack buffer 'drc_name' using memcpy, which does not append a null terminator. It then calls strchr(drc_name, '\n'), which scans for a character in what it expects to be a null-terminated string. If the copied data contains no newline and no null byte, strchr will read beyond the copied data into uninitialized stack memory, and potentially past the end of the drc_name buffer entirely, causing a stack-based out-of-bounds read. While the fallback 'end = &drc_name[nbytes]' is correct if strchr returns NULL, the issue is that strchr may instead find a stale '\n' byte in uninitialized stack memory and return a pointer to it, causing the null terminator to be placed at the wrong position. This could lead to information disclosure (reading uninitialized stack data) or an incorrectly formed drc_name string being passed to dlpar_remove_slot.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Option data not consumed from socket before continuing causes protocol desynchronization
vulnerabilities.long_description: In two early-rejection paths—when the client lacks fixed newstyle support and when TLS is required but not yet negotiated—the code sends an error reply and continues to the next loop iteration without reading the `optlen` bytes of option payload from the socket. On the next iteration, the unconsumed option data will be misinterpreted as the next `nbd_new_option` header. A malicious client can craft option payloads such that the leftover bytes form a valid-looking option header, potentially causing the server to enter unexpected code paths, bypass TLS requirements, or process attacker-controlled data as protocol structures. Other rejection paths in the function (e.g., the default case, NBD_OPT_LIST with non-zero optlen) correctly drain the data with `conn_recv_full` before continuing.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow due to missing bounds check on image->colors against MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OpenMP parallel loop, image->colors is incremented each time a new unique intensity is found, and used to index into image->colormap which was allocated with MaxColormapSize entries via AcquireImageColormap. There is no bounds check ensuring image->colors < MaxColormapSize before writing to image->colormap[image->colors]. If the number of unique grayscale intensities exceeds MaxColormapSize, this results in a heap buffer overflow, potentially allowing code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential use of uninitialized memory when MaxMap exceeds MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The colormap_index array is allocated with MagickMax(MaxColormapSize+1, MaxMap) elements, but only MaxColormapSize elements are initialized via memset to -1. The intensity value from ScaleQuantumToMap can range up to MaxMap. If MaxMap > MaxColormapSize, indices between MaxColormapSize and MaxMap-1 will read uninitialized memory, leading to unpredictable behavior—potentially skipping colormap assignment or causing out-of-bounds writes to image->colormap.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition on colormap_index[intensity] check outside critical section
vulnerabilities.long_description: The first check `if (colormap_index[intensity] < 0)` is performed outside the OpenMP critical section, while the authoritative check and update happen inside the critical section. In a multi-threaded context, two threads with the same intensity value could both pass the outer check, but only one will pass the inner check thanks to double-checked locking. However, the outer read of colormap_index[intensity] is unsynchronized—it reads a value potentially being written by another thread in the critical section, constituting a data race with undefined behavior per the C standard.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when rewriting visual range with use_plus_cmd
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `has_visual_range` is TRUE and `use_plus_cmd` is TRUE (ex mode with an empty command after modifiers), the code moves `cmd_start` content (length `len`) to `orig_cmd`, then appends the string `"'<,'>+"` (6 chars + NUL = 7 bytes). The total bytes written are `len + 7`. However, the original buffer starting at `orig_cmd` had a total content length of `5 (for "'<,'>") + len + 1 (NUL) = len + 6` bytes. This means the write exceeds the original buffer by 1 byte, causing an off-by-one heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control command input in ex mode with a visual range and command modifiers (e.g., `'<,'>silent` with no following command) could trigger this overflow, potentially corrupting heap metadata or adjacent data, leading to code execution.

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   When displaying "$" check the column is not negative.
cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.short_description: strncpy may not null-terminate sun_path, leading to buffer over-read or undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: If pipePath is longer than or equal to sizeof(local.sun_path) (typically 108 bytes), strncpy will not null-terminate local.sun_path. Subsequent use of local.sun_path (e.g., the unlink() call, which treats it as a C string) will read beyond the bounds of the struct, potentially causing undefined behavior, crashes, or unintended file operations. An attacker who controls the endpoint name could supply a long path to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of listen() is not checked for errors
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of ::listen() is ignored. If the call fails, the code proceeds to chmod and registers the file descriptor as a server socket even though it cannot accept connections. While not directly exploitable for code execution, it can lead to unexpected application states and denial of service.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.short_description: No NULL check on buf returned from parse_attr_value, leading to NULL pointer dereference and pointer arithmetic on NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of parse_attr_value is assigned to buf but never checked for NULL before being reused in the next loop iteration. If parse_attr_value returns NULL (e.g., on a parsing error), the subsequent iteration computes `buf_end - buf` (pointer arithmetic with NULL) which is undefined behavior, and then passes NULL as the buffer to parse_attr_value again, likely causing a crash or exploitable memory corruption.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in buffer length calculation when buf advances past buf_end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `buf_end - buf` is used as a size parameter. If parse_attr_value advances buf beyond buf_end (e.g., due to a malformed DWARF entry), the subtraction `buf_end - buf` yields a negative value which, when interpreted as an unsigned size_t, becomes an extremely large value. This could allow parse_attr_value to read far out of bounds on the next call, potentially causing information disclosure or memory corruption.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < abbrev->count - 1; i++) {
			memset(&die->attr_values[i], 0, sizeof(die->attr_values[i]));
vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on die->attr_values array access against its allocated capacity
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates up to `abbrev->count - 1` times, writing to `die->attr_values[i]` each iteration, but there is no validation that die->attr_values was allocated with enough space to hold `abbrev->count - 1` entries. If abbrev->count exceeds the allocated capacity of die->attr_values, this results in a heap buffer overflow via the memset and subsequent writes, which could be exploited for arbitrary code execution through a crafted DWARF binary.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow due to missing bounds check on image->colors against MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OpenMP parallel loop, image->colors is incremented each time a new intensity is found, but there is no check that image->colors remains less than MaxColormapSize before writing to image->colormap[image->colors]. If the number of unique intensity values reaches MaxColormapSize, the next write goes out of bounds of the colormap buffer allocated via AcquireImageColormap(image, MaxColormapSize, ...). This can lead to heap corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colormap_index when image is PseudoClass
vulnerabilities.long_description: When image->storage_class is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with only image->colors+1 entries. However, in the final pixel-remapping loop, the index used is ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q)), which can return values up to MaxMap (typically 65535 or 255). If this scaled value exceeds image->colors, it results in a heap out-of-bounds read from the colormap_index array. An attacker could craft an image with a small colormap but pixel index values that scale beyond the allocated buffer, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition on colormap_index in OpenMP parallel region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The outer check `if (colormap_index[intensity] < 0)` is performed outside the critical section, while the inner check and update are inside. Between the outer check and entering the critical section, another thread may have already set colormap_index[intensity] and incremented image->colors. While the inner check mitigates double-insertion for the same intensity, the non-atomic read of colormap_index[intensity] outside the critical section and the non-atomic read of image->colors for array indexing create a data race. On architectures with relaxed memory ordering, this could lead to reading stale values and incorrect colormap index assignments.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when abbrev->count is 0 leads to massive loop and out-of-bounds write
vulnerabilities.long_description: If abbrev->count is 0, the expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows because both count and i are unsigned (size_t), wrapping to SIZE_MAX. This causes the loop to iterate billions of times, writing out-of-bounds via die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i]. An attacker who controls the DWARF abbreviation data could craft an entry with a count of 0, triggering heap corruption and potentially achieving code execution.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
			&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.short_description: No NULL check on buf after parse_attr_value may cause NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: If parse_attr_value returns NULL (e.g., due to malformed input or end-of-buffer), the returned NULL is assigned to buf and passed back into parse_attr_value on the next loop iteration. Additionally, the expression `buf_end - buf` would invoke undefined behavior with a NULL pointer. This could lead to a crash or exploitable condition when processing malformed DWARF data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pointer arithmetic underflow if buf exceeds buf_end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The size argument `buf_end - buf` is passed to parse_attr_value. If buf has been advanced past buf_end (e.g., by a previous call to parse_attr_value that didn't properly bounds-check), the subtraction wraps to a very large value when interpreted as an unsigned size, potentially allowing parse_attr_value to read far beyond the buffer boundary. This can result in an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.
