commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when reading BMP V4/V5 headers (biSize 108 or 124)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The local buffer is declared as 64 bytes, but when biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5), the code reads `biSize - 4` bytes (104 or 120 bytes) into this 64-byte buffer via ReadOK(fd, buffer, Bitmap_File_Head.biSize - 4). This causes a stack-based buffer overflow of up to 56 bytes, which can be exploited by a crafted BMP file to overwrite the return address or other stack variables, leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: fclose called on NULL file pointer when fopen fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When fopen() fails, fd is NULL. The code jumps to the cleanup label, which unconditionally calls fclose(fd). Passing NULL to fclose is undefined behavior per the C standard and will likely cause a crash (NULL pointer dereference). The cleanup block should check if fd is non-NULL before calling fclose.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting width/height to unsigned short
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers but are cast to unsigned short (16-bit) when initializing the bitmap. If a crafted BMP file specifies dimensions larger than 65535, the values silently wrap around. This creates a mismatch between the allocated image buffer size (based on actual dimensions) and the bitmap metadata (truncated dimensions), potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes when the bitmap is later processed.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation `data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can integer-overflow when data_len is very large (e.g., close to INT_MAX). This would result in a small value for outlen, causing emalloc to allocate a small buffer. The subsequent EVP_DecryptUpdate call would then write far beyond this buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could supply a large input (either raw or base64-encoded) to trigger this and potentially achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when base64 decoding fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If php_base64_decode fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), it returns NULL. The code does not check for this and proceeds to assign NULL to `data`, which is then passed to EVP_DecryptUpdate. This results in a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash (denial of service).

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read/write via unchecked depArray index and dep_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterating over depArray uses `k` starting from `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` and increments until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`, but never checks that `k < cache->accel->depListCount`. If the sentinel value is missing or the start index is corrupted, `k` will read past the allocated depArray buffer. Additionally, `dep_index` (derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff`) is used to index into both `deps` (sized `cache->hdr->imagesCount`) and `img` (sized `hdr->imagesCount`) without any bounds check, enabling an out-of-bounds write on `deps[dep_index]++` and an out-of-bounds read on `img[dep_index]`.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
	bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on deps array when subsequent headers have larger imagesCount
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., the first header's image count). However, the outer loop iterates over all headers (`cache->n_hdr`), and inner loops use each header's own `hdr->imagesCount`. If any subsequent header has an `imagesCount` larger than the first header's, accesses like `deps[j]` will read/write beyond the allocated buffer, causing heap corruption or information disclosure.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: If data_len is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), the addition of EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type) can cause an integer overflow, resulting in a small value for outlen. This leads to a small heap allocation via emalloc, while the subsequent EVP_EncryptUpdate writes up to data_len + block_size bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker supplying a large data input could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing IV only triggers a warning instead of failing, allowing encryption with a zeroed IV
vulnerabilities.long_description: When no IV is provided for a cipher that requires one, the function only emits a warning and proceeds with a zero-filled IV (via php_openssl_validate_iv). This is a cryptographic weakness: using a predictable/zero IV breaks the semantic security of CBC and other modes, enabling attacks such as plaintext recovery across messages encrypted with the same key. The function should reject the operation rather than continue.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when fclose is called on a NULL file descriptor
vulnerabilities.long_description: If fopen() fails, fd is NULL and the code jumps to the 'cleanup' label, which unconditionally calls fclose(fd). Passing NULL to fclose() is undefined behavior and will likely cause a crash (segmentation fault). The goto after the fopen failure check jumps directly to cleanup without any guard checking if fd is non-NULL before closing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation of width/height to unsigned short can cause buffer size mismatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers that are cast to unsigned short (16-bit) when creating the bitmap. If the validated width or height exceeds 65535, the cast silently truncates the value. The image data (image_storage) was allocated based on the full 32-bit dimensions in ReadImage, but the bitmap metadata now records much smaller dimensions. This mismatch can lead to out-of-bounds reads or other memory corruption when the bitmap is later processed.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in ColormapSize calculation with crafted bfOffs value
vulnerabilities.long_description: If Bitmap_File_Head.bfOffs is smaller than (Bitmap_File_Head.biSize + 14), the subtraction underflows. Depending on the signedness of the types involved, this could produce a very large positive number or a negative number. While ColormapSize is later capped at 256, a negative result divided by Maps could still yield a negative ColormapSize, which may be passed to ReadColorMap, potentially causing unexpected behavior such as reading zero or garbage entries from the file into the ColorMap buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow reading BMP header for biSize between 56-64
vulnerabilities.long_description: For the enhanced Windows format branch (biSize >= 56 && biSize <= 64), the code reads Bitmap_File_Head.biSize - 4 bytes into buffer[128]. While the values 56-64 are safe, the biSize value is read from the file and only loosely validated. The same pattern for biSize == 108 reads 104 bytes and biSize == 124 reads 120 bytes — both within bounds. However, this code pattern relies entirely on the branch conditions for safety. If the conditions were ever expanded without updating the buffer, a heap/stack overflow would result. More critically, the code at buffer offsets like 0x30 (48) accesses are safe for biSize=56 (reading 52 bytes), but this is borderline and fragile.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pipe buffer flags not cleared when initializing a new buffer entry (CVE-2022-0847 / Dirty Pipe)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a new pipe_buffer entry is initialized with a page cache page, the code sets ops, page, offset, and len but never clears buf->flags. If a previous use of this pipe buffer slot set the PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flag (e.g., via an anonymous pipe write through anon_pipe_buf_ops), that flag persists. A subsequent pipe_write() call will see PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE on a buffer backed by a page cache page and will write directly into the page cache, bypassing all permission checks. This allows an unprivileged user to overwrite data in arbitrary readable files (including read-only and SUID binaries), leading to trivial local privilege escalation. The fix is to add 'buf->flags = 0;' when initializing the new pipe buffer.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access via dep_index into deps array
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `deps` array is allocated with `cache->hdr->imagesCount` elements. The `dep_index` value is derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff`, which can be up to 32767. There is no check that `dep_index < cache->hdr->imagesCount` before using it to index into `deps`. An attacker crafting a malicious dyld cache could set dependency indices that exceed the bounds of the `deps` array, causing a heap buffer overflow (write) and potentially achieving code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on depArray due to missing bounds check on loop index k
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates over `depArray` starting at `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` and continues until a sentinel value `0xffff` is found. There is no check that `k` remains within `cache->accel->depListCount`. If the start index is beyond the array bounds or the sentinel is missing/corrupted, the loop will read out of bounds of the `depArray` heap allocation. A crafted cache file could trigger this to leak memory or crash the process.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
	bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on deps array when iterating over multiple headers with different imagesCount values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (the first header's image count). However, the outer loop iterates over `cache->n_hdr` headers, and the inner loop uses `hdr->imagesCount` from each respective header. If a subsequent header (`cache->hdr[i]` for `i > 0`) has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, the access `deps[j]` will be out of bounds. This can cause heap buffer over-read/over-write, potentially leading to code execution with a crafted dyld cache file.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4218: illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode

Problem:    Illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode.
Solution:   Reserve space for the trailing NUL.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when accumulating characters in buf without bounds checking on idx
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop accumulates characters into the stack buffer `buf` of size `NUMBUFLEN + MB_MAXBYTES`. Each iteration can add up to MB_MAXBYTES bytes (typically 4-6 bytes for a multi-byte character) to `buf` at offset `idx`, but there is no check that `idx` remains within the bounds of `buf` before writing. If the end-of-paste termcode (`end`) is NULL (which happens if the end code is too long or not found), or if the incoming data doesn't partially match `end` (causing `idx` to be reset to 0 only after a mismatch is fully processed in the non-drop path), the index can grow beyond the buffer size. Specifically, when characters are being accumulated because they partially match the end sequence (the `continue` path), `idx` keeps growing without any bounds check. If an attacker sends a long sequence of bytes that keep partially matching the end code pattern, `idx` will exceed `NUMBUFLEN + MB_MAXBYTES`, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. This can lead to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

code_snippets:
res = platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0);
	lp->base_addr = devm_ioremap_resource(&ofdev->dev, res);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer passed to devm_ioremap_resource without explicit NULL check on res
vulnerabilities.long_description: The second call to platform_get_resource() for IORESOURCE_MEM may return NULL, and the result is passed directly to devm_ioremap_resource() without an explicit NULL check. While modern kernels' devm_ioremap_resource handles a NULL resource internally (returning an ERR_PTR), older kernel versions did not have this guard, leading to a NULL pointer dereference inside devm_ioremap_resource. Even in modern kernels, this is considered a code defect since the pattern deviates from safe practice and could cause a NULL dereference if backported or used in an older kernel context.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5148: invalid memory access when using expression on command line

Problem:    Invalid memory access when using an expression on the command line.
Solution:   Make sure the position does not go negative.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when NUL-terminating the buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code writes a NUL terminator at `tp[len]` without verifying that this position is within the allocated buffer bounds. When `buf` is non-NULL, `tp = buf + offset` and `len = *buflen - offset`, so the write goes to `buf[*buflen]`. If `*buflen` equals `bufsize` (the allocated size of `buf`), this writes one byte past the end of the buffer. Similarly, when `buf` is NULL and the typebuf is used, the write targets `typebuf.tb_buf[typebuf.tb_off + typebuf.tb_len]`, which may be beyond the allocated region. An attacker who controls terminal input could trigger this off-by-one heap or stack buffer overflow, potentially corrupting adjacent memory, crashing the application, or achieving code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5148: invalid memory access when using expression on command line

Problem:    Invalid memory access when using an expression on the command line.
Solution:   Make sure the position does not go negative.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

code_snippets:
res = platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0);
	lp->base_addr = devm_ioremap_resource(&ofdev->dev, res);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference if platform_get_resource returns NULL for IORESOURCE_MEM
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0) is not checked for NULL before being passed to devm_ioremap_resource(). While devm_ioremap_resource in modern kernels handles a NULL resource and returns an ERR_PTR, in older kernel versions it may dereference the NULL pointer directly. This contrasts with the IRQ resource retrieval just above, which does explicitly check for NULL. The safer pattern is to check for NULL explicitly before use, similar to the IRQ path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Resource leak of phy_node and MDIO bus on register_netdev failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: If register_netdev() fails, execution jumps to the 'error' label which only calls free_netdev(). However, at that point of_parse_phandle() has already incremented the refcount on phy_node, and xemaclite_mdio_setup() may have registered an MDIO bus. Neither of_node_put(lp->phy_node) nor MDIO bus cleanup is performed in the error path, resulting in a device-tree node reference leak and potential MDIO bus resource leak. While primarily a resource leak, repeated triggering (e.g., via driver bind/unbind) could exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing tok->args[2] instead of tok->args[1]
vulnerabilities.long_description: When tok->n_args > 1 (e.g., n_args == 2), the code parses tok->args[1] as the flavor but then references tok->args[2] in the error log message. Since the check is only `tok->n_args > 1`, n_args could be exactly 2, meaning args[2] is out of bounds. This could lead to reading uninitialized or invalid memory, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure. An attacker could craft a malicious network-status document with exactly 2 arguments on the network-status-version line and an invalid flavor name to trigger this out-of-bounds read.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when bin_obj is NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function checks that bf and bf->o are non-null, but does not check bf->o->bin_obj before dereferencing it as qo. If bin_obj is NULL, the subsequent access to qo->fixups will dereference a null pointer, leading to a crash or potential denial of service.

commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: mixer is deleted then dereferenced
vulnerabilities.long_description: The destructor deletes `mixer` first, then in the `if (player)` block it dereferences `mixer` by calling `mixer->isActive()` and `mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player)`. Since `mixer` was already deleted, this is a use-after-free. If `mixer` was non-null and `player` is also non-null, the code accesses freed memory, leading to undefined behavior which could cause crashes or potentially be exploited for code execution.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when "set" handler re-interprets arg1/arg2 that were already heap-allocated by percent escape processing
vulnerabilities.long_description: At the top of the function, if percent escape interpretation is enabled, arg1 and arg2 are allocated via InterpretImageProperties. Later, the "set" option handler unconditionally calls InterpretImageProperties again, overwriting arg1 (and possibly arg2) with new heap allocations without first freeing the previously allocated strings. The original heap-allocated pointers are lost, causing memory leaks. Similarly, the "clone" path with '+' option does `arg1=AcquireString("-1")` which also overwrites a potentially heap-allocated arg1. In a long-running process or when processing many operations, an attacker could trigger repeated memory leaks leading to memory exhaustion and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer passed to DestroyString when arg2 is NULL in the "option:" set path
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the "set" handler's "option:" branch, arg2 is initialized to NULL. If IfPlusOp is true (the '+' variant), the InterpretImageProperties call for arg2 is skipped, leaving arg2 as NULL. Then `arg2=DestroyString((char *)arg2)` is called with a NULL argument. Depending on the DestroyString implementation, this could be benign or could trigger a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, when the IfNormalOp InterpretImageProperties call for arg2 fails (returns NULL), CLIWandExceptionBreak is invoked, but arg1 that was allocated within the "set" handler is not freed before the break, creating another leak path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Inconsistent cleanup of arg1/arg2 between "set" handler internal frees and bottom-of-function cleanup
vulnerabilities.long_description: The "set" handler frees arg1 and arg2 internally (setting them to NULL via DestroyString), then breaks to the bottom cleanup code which checks `if (arg1 != arg1n)`. After the internal free, arg1 is NULL. If arg1n was non-NULL, the condition `NULL != arg1n` is true, causing DestroyString(NULL) to be called again. While DestroyString may handle NULL gracefully, this inconsistent ownership pattern is fragile. More critically, the original heap-allocated arg1 from the percent escape processing at the top of the function has already been leaked (overwritten in the "set" handler), so the cleanup code never frees the correct original pointer. This pattern could lead to double-free issues if the code is modified or if DestroyString behavior changes.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of previously percent-escape-interpreted arg1/arg2 strings in the 'set' handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: At the top of the function, arg1 and arg2 may be allocated via InterpretImageProperties (percent escape interpretation). Later, the 'set' handler unconditionally overwrites arg1 (and conditionally arg2) with new InterpretImageProperties calls using arg1n/arg2n, without first freeing the previously allocated strings. The old dynamically-allocated arg1/arg2 pointers are lost, causing a memory leak. In the cleanup code at the bottom, the check `if (arg1 != arg1n)` cannot recover these strings since arg1 now points to the new allocation (or NULL after DestroyString). An attacker who can trigger repeated '-set' operations can cause unbounded memory consumption, leading to denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when '+clone' overwrites previously allocated arg1 from percent escape interpretation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the '+clone' option is processed, arg1 is unconditionally overwritten with AcquireString("-1"). If arg1 had been previously allocated during percent escape interpretation at the top of the function (i.e., arg1 != arg1n), the old allocation is leaked. While the new AcquireString allocation is properly freed in cleanup (since it differs from arg1n), the original interpreted string is lost. Repeated invocations could lead to memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential double-free if DestroyString does not safely handle NULL on all platforms
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 'set' handler's 'option:' and 'registry:' sub-paths, arg1 and arg2 are freed via DestroyString, which typically returns NULL. After breaking to the cleanup code, the condition `arg1 != arg1n` evaluates to true (NULL != non-NULL arg1n), so DestroyString is called again on the already-freed (now NULL) pointer. While ImageMagick's DestroyString typically handles NULL safely, this pattern is fragile and error-prone. If the DestroyString behavior ever changes, or if the pointer is not set to NULL correctly, this becomes a double-free vulnerability allowing potential code execution.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow: post-hoc overflow check for \<> special keys does not prevent the overwrite
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing \<...> special key sequences, trans_special() writes directly into the heap-allocated buffer. The code checks *after* the write whether the end pointer has exceeded the buffer bounds (end >= rettv->vval.v_string + len), but only emits a warning message via iemsg() without taking any corrective action (e.g., returning an error or truncating). By that point, the heap buffer has already been overflowed. If trans_special() produces more output bytes than the 5 extra bytes reserved per \< during the first scanning pass, heap memory beyond the allocation is corrupted, which could lead to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when computing buffer length can cause undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable len is computed as (int)(p - *arg + extra). On 64-bit platforms, p - *arg is a ptrdiff_t (64-bit), but it is truncated to int (32-bit). For very long input strings (>2GB), this truncation can result in a small or even negative len value. If len becomes a small positive value, alloc() allocates an undersized buffer, and the subsequent copy loop writes far beyond it, causing a heap buffer overflow. Additionally, the extra variable (also int) accumulates +5 for each \< sequence and could itself overflow on extremely large inputs, further corrupting the len calculation.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.
The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap out-of-bounds read when colormap_length is 0 but colormap_type is non-zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a TGA image has colormap_type != 0 (e.g., for TGAColormap/TGARLEColormap), the code sets image->colors = tga_info.colormap_index + tga_info.colormap_length. If both colormap_index and colormap_length are 0, image->colors becomes 0. AcquireImageColormap is then called with 0 colors, which may allocate a zero-length or minimal colormap. During pixel decoding, when bits_per_pixel is 8 and colormap_type != 0, the code accesses image->colormap via ConstrainColormapIndex with image->colors potentially being 0. If image->colors is 0, ConstrainColormapIndex may cause a division by zero or return an invalid index, leading to a heap out-of-bounds read. A crafted TGA file with colormap_type=1, colormap_length=0, and colormap_index=0 can trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior and potential excessive memory allocation from shifting by bits_per_pixel
vulnerabilities.long_description: When colormap_type is 0 and the image is PseudoClass (Monochrome), image->colors is computed as 1 << tga_info.bits_per_pixel. The bits_per_pixel validation allows values of 24 and 32 in addition to 2-16. On a 32-bit platform, 1 << 32 is undefined behavior. On 64-bit platforms, 1 << 32 results in 4 billion colormap entries, and 1 << 24 results in 16 million entries. The overflow check against (~0UL)/sizeof(*image->colormap) may pass on 64-bit systems, leading to allocation of hundreds of megabytes to gigabytes of memory for the colormap. A specially crafted TGA file with a Monochrome image type and high bits_per_pixel value can cause denial of service through excessive memory consumption or trigger undefined behavior.

commit_message: Bug: Lua can generate wrong code when _ENV is <const>
cve_desc: singlevar in lparser.c in Lua from (including) 5.4.0 up to (excluding) 5.4.4 lacks a certain luaK_exp2anyregup call, leading to a heap-based buffer over-read that might affect a system that compiles untrusted Lua code.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation for surfaceData allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic, which can overflow if width and height are sufficiently large (e.g., width=65536, height=65536 would overflow). This would result in a much smaller buffer being allocated than expected, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when the surface data is written. An attacker who can control the width and height parameters (e.g., via a crafted video presentation) could exploit this to achieve remote code execution.

commit_message: Bug: Lua can generate wrong code when _ENV is <const>
cve_desc: singlevar in lparser.c in Lua from (including) 5.4.0 up to (excluding) 5.4.4 lacks a certain luaK_exp2anyregup call, leading to a heap-based buffer over-read that might affect a system that compiles untrusted Lua code.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on alloc_workqueue return value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of alloc_workqueue() is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, trans_pcie->rba.alloc_wq will be NULL, and subsequent use of this workqueue (e.g., queue_work) will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a kernel crash/panic. The function should check for NULL and jump to the appropriate error cleanup label.

commit_message:
seq_file: disallow extremely large seq buffer allocations

There is no reasonable need for a buffer larger than this, and it avoids
int overflow pitfalls.

Fixes: 058504edd026 ("fs/seq_file: fallback to vmalloc allocation")
Suggested-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Reported-by: Qualys Security Advisory <qsa@qualys.com>
Signed-off-by: Eric Sandeen <sandeen@redhat.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: fs/seq_file.c in the Linux kernel 3.16 through 5.13.x before 5.13.4 does not properly restrict seq buffer allocations, leading to an integer overflow, an Out-of-bounds Write, and escalation to root by an unprivileged user, aka CID-8cae8cd89f05.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in scrollbar event coalescing loops due to missing bounds check against `len`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loops that coalesce consecutive scrollbar events access `tp[j]`, `tp[j+1]`, and `tp[j+2]` without verifying that `j`, `j+1`, and `j+2` are within the valid buffer length `len`. The variable `j` is incremented across iterations and can grow beyond the available input data. While `get_bytes_from_buf` and `get_long_from_buf` may return -1 for insufficient data, the loop condition itself (`tp[j] == CSI && tp[j + 1] == ...`) reads from memory beyond the buffer before those checks occur. An attacker providing crafted terminal input could cause reads past the end of the type-ahead buffer, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential stack buffer overflow when building the key code string
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `string` buffer is a stack-allocated array of size `MAX_KEY_CODE_LEN + 1`. The function first calls `modifiers2keycode()` which writes modifier key sequences into `string` and returns the length, then unconditionally appends up to 3 more bytes (K_SPECIAL + two key name bytes) or a multi-byte character via `mb_char2bytes`. If `modifiers2keycode` returns a value close to `MAX_KEY_CODE_LEN`, the subsequent appends could write past the end of the `string` buffer. Since this is a stack buffer, this could allow stack-based buffer overflow leading to code execution if an attacker can craft input that triggers a large number of modifier encodings.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen leads to undersized heap allocation and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t` (max value 65535), but the sum of `strlen(provider_name) + strlen(ttl_class_type) + 4*sizeof(struct SignedCert) + 2` can easily exceed 65535. The guard check only rejects provider names longer than 0xffff0000, which is far too permissive to prevent a uint16_t overflow. If the computed size wraps around, `malloc` allocates a very small buffer, and subsequent `snprintf` calls write far beyond its bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow. This can lead to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing semicolon after `continue` causes a compilation error or unexpected control flow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `continue` statement inside the overflow guard is missing its terminating semicolon. In C, this is a syntax error. If the compiler somehow accepted this (or if the code is slightly altered), the guard would not function as intended, allowing the subsequent code to execute with an overflowed rrlen value, compounding the integer overflow vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of strdup() is not checked for NULL before use
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple calls to `strdup()` are made without checking if they return NULL (out-of-memory condition). The NULL pointers are then passed directly to `cfg_str2list_insert` and `cfg_strlist_insert`, which may dereference them, leading to a NULL pointer dereference crash. This can be used for denial of service under memory pressure.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t`, limiting its maximum value to 65535. If `dnscenv->provider_name` is sufficiently long, the sum of the string lengths can overflow the 16-bit integer, wrapping to a small value (potentially 0). This causes `malloc(rrlen)` to allocate a very small (or zero-sized) buffer. Subsequently, if `rrlen` wraps to 0, the expression `rrlen - 1` evaluates to 65535 (unsigned underflow), and `snprintf` is told it can write up to 65535 bytes into the undersized heap buffer, resulting in a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who controls the provider name could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked strdup return values may lead to NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple calls to `strdup()` are made without checking the return value for NULL. If memory allocation fails, NULL is passed to `cfg_str2list_insert` or `cfg_strlist_insert`, which may dereference the NULL pointer. Additionally, the return value of `cfg_strlist_insert` is not checked, so a failure there goes silently unhandled. Under memory pressure, this could cause a crash (denial of service).

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculations due to mixing signed and unsigned types
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable next_offset is declared as int while req_size is size_t. The addition next_offset + req_size could overflow or produce unexpected results when compared or assigned to new_acts_size (also int). If actions_len is large enough, next_offset itself could overflow as an int. Furthermore, the initial check 'req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)' involves subtracting a signed int from a size_t, which can underflow if next_offset exceeds ksize(*sfa), causing the check to incorrectly pass and leading to an out-of-bounds write at the returned pointer.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow / truncation on 32-bit platforms when computing buffer size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `width * height * 4ULL` is performed in 64-bit (unsigned long long) arithmetic, but the result is stored in `s` which is of type `size_t`. On 32-bit platforms, `size_t` is 32 bits, so the 64-bit result is silently truncated. The subsequent guard `s > INT32_MAX` checks the already-truncated value, which may appear small (e.g., 0) even though the true required size is very large. This would cause `BufferPool_Take` to allocate a much smaller buffer than needed, and later writes to `ret->surfaceData` (e.g., during YUV-to-RGB conversion filling `width * height * 4` bytes) would overflow the heap buffer, leading to heap corruption and potentially remote code execution.

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>

Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds pointer dereference when freeing mdev_types
vulnerabilities.long_description: Due to C operator precedence, `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` (i.e., index first, then dereference) instead of the intended `(*mdev_types)[i]` (dereference the double pointer first, then index into the array). For i=0, the result happens to be the same, but for i>0, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory at offsets beyond the `mdev_types` pointer parameter itself (treating the pointer-to-pointer as an array of pointers), leading to an out-of-bounds read. This can cause crashes or potentially be leveraged to free arbitrary memory locations, resulting in memory corruption. The fix is to write `(*mdev_types)[i]`.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to missing bounds check on 'depth' before incrementing into fixed-size arrays.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'depth' is incremented without checking that it remains within the bounds of the stack-allocated arrays arridx[], curi[], tword[], and wordcount[], all of size MAXWLEN. If the trie being traversed has a path longer than MAXWLEN, depth will exceed the array bounds. After 'tword[depth++] = c', the now-incremented depth is used to index arridx[depth], curi[depth], and wordcount[depth], causing out-of-bounds writes on the stack. An attacker who can craft a malicious spell file with a sufficiently deep trie can trigger this overflow to overwrite stack data, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: Fix crash when mapping a big memory and calling uc_close
cve_desc: Unicorn Engine v2.0.0-rc7 and below was discovered to contain a NULL pointer dereference via qemu_ram_free.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `buf` on multiple early return paths after allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated via `malloc`, several subsequent error-handling paths (when `spname` is NULL, when ESIL re-initialization fails, when `pcname` is NULL or empty) return without calling `free(buf)`. This causes a memory leak each time these paths are taken. In a long-running application or when this function is called repeatedly (e.g., in an analysis loop), this can lead to significant memory consumption and potential denial of service.

code_snippets: if (!r_anal_op (core->anal, &op, cur, buf + i, iend - i, ...))
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when assigning ut64 difference to int variable `iend`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `iend` is declared as `int` but is assigned the result of `end - start` where both are `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). If the difference exceeds INT_MAX but is less than MAX_SCAN_SIZE (assuming MAX_SCAN_SIZE could be large), the value will be truncated or wrap around when stored in an `int`. This could lead to an undersized buffer allocation via `malloc((size_t)iend + 2)`, followed by an out-of-bounds read in `r_io_read_at` and subsequent buffer accesses using `buf + i` with `iend - i` as the length parameter. While the `iend < 0` check catches some cases of wrapping, the truncation from 64-bit to 32-bit signed can produce unexpected positive values for certain input ranges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read when `i` equals `iend`, passing zero-length buffer to `r_anal_op`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check `if (i > iend)` uses strict greater-than, so when `i == iend`, execution continues and `r_anal_op` is called with `buf + i` and length `iend - i == 0`. Passing a zero-length buffer to the analysis function could lead to out-of-bounds reads within `r_anal_op` if it doesn't properly handle zero-length input. Additionally, after `r_anal_op` fails, `i += minopsize - 1` could push `i` well beyond `iend`, and subsequent accesses in the loop iteration (before the next bounds check) could access memory beyond the buffer.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

code_snippets:
end_of_header = find_start_of_next_routerstatus(s);
  if (tokenize_string(area, s, end_of_header, tokens,
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference if find_start_of_next_routerstatus returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: If find_start_of_next_routerstatus(s) returns NULL, end_of_header is set to NULL. This NULL value is then passed to tokenize_string as the end boundary, and later assigned to s before being used in strcmpstart. If tokenize_string doesn't crash first, the assignment s = end_of_header followed by strcmpstart(s, "r ") will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash. An attacker could craft a malformed network-status document that causes find_start_of_next_routerstatus to return NULL, triggering a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in time_t arithmetic for vote interval validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions ns->valid_after + MIN_VOTE_INTERVAL and ns->valid_after + MIN_VOTE_INTERVAL*2 can overflow if an attacker provides a crafted valid_after time value close to the maximum representable time_t value. On systems where time_t is a signed 32-bit integer, this overflow would wrap around to a negative value, causing the sanity checks to pass when they should fail. This could allow acceptance of malformed consensus/vote documents with invalid timing parameters, potentially disrupting the Tor directory authority protocol.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to unchecked depth increment when traversing the trie
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `depth` is incremented without any bounds check against MAXWLEN before being used to index into the stack-allocated arrays `tword`, `arridx`, `curi`, and `wordcount` (all of size MAXWLEN). If the spell trie contains a path deeper than MAXWLEN levels, `depth` will exceed the array bounds, causing out-of-bounds writes on the stack. An attacker who can craft a malicious spell file with a sufficiently deep trie can overwrite adjacent stack memory, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing bounds check on s->reg index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function accesses s->edid_blob[s->reg] without verifying that s->reg is within the valid bounds of the edid_blob array. Since s->reg is incremented on every call to i2c_ddc_rx and there is no bounds check or wrap-around logic, repeated reads will eventually cause s->reg to exceed the size of the edid_blob buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. This could leak adjacent memory contents to the caller or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Multiple early return paths leak allocated memory (buf, spname, pcname).
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated via malloc, several early `return` statements execute without freeing `buf`. Similarly, after `spname = strdup(kspname)` and `pcname = strdup(kpcname)` are called, subsequent early returns do not free these allocations. Specifically: (1) If ESIL initialization fails, `buf` is leaked. (2) If kspname is empty, `buf` is leaked. (3) If kpcname is invalid, both `buf` and `spname` are leaked. Repeated calls to this function with triggering conditions could exhaust memory in long-running processes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when assigning ut64 subtraction result to int variable `iend`.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `iend` is declared as `int` but is assigned the result of `end - start` where both are `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). If the difference exceeds INT_MAX, the value is truncated. While there is a negative check (`iend < 0`) and a MAX_SCAN_SIZE check, certain large ut64 differences could truncate to small positive int values, causing `buf` to be allocated much smaller than the actual address range. Subsequent operations that iterate using `i` up to the logical end of the range could then read past the buffer boundary. Additionally, the cast `(size_t)iend + 2` in malloc relies on the already-truncated value.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential null pointer dereference of `core->anal->cur` without null check.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code accesses `core->anal->cur->arch` in two places without first verifying that `core->anal->cur` is not NULL. If no analysis plugin is loaded (cur is NULL), this would result in a null pointer dereference, causing a crash. While there is one guarded access (`if (core->anal->cur && arch == R2_ARCH_ARM64)`) elsewhere, the two strcmp calls on `core->anal->cur->arch` are unguarded.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of tp and tpn when both S4U2Self and constrained delegation code paths execute
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a request triggers both the S4U2Self path (PA_S4U2Self padata) and the constrained delegation path (cname_in_addl_tkt), the tp principal and tpn string are first set to new allocated values in the S4U2Self block, then overwritten with new allocations in the constrained delegation block without freeing the previous values. The cleanup code only frees the final tp/tpn values (checking tp != cp and tpn != cpn), so the intermediate allocations from S4U2Self are leaked. A malicious client could repeatedly send crafted TGS requests to trigger this, causing memory exhaustion on the KDC.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing return value check on krb5_make_principal in capath traversal
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the capath loop handling (when the krbtgt for the target realm is not found), the return value of krb5_make_principal() is not checked. If this call fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), sp could be set to NULL or left in an invalid state. The code then proceeds to call krb5_unparse_name() on sp and jump to server_lookup, potentially causing a NULL pointer dereference or use of an uninitialized principal. This differs from the other krb5_make_principal calls in the function which do check the return value.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when parsing verbose level
vulnerabilities.long_description: The verbose level is parsed using atoi(), which does not perform overflow checking. If a user supplies a very large numeric value (e.g., near INT_MAX), atoi() itself has undefined behavior for out-of-range values, and adding 1 to the result can cause a signed integer overflow. This could lead to unexpected verbose level values (e.g., wrapping to a negative number), potentially altering program behavior in security-relevant ways such as suppressing or enabling diagnostic output unexpectedly.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4218: illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode

Problem:    Illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode.
Solution:   Reserve space for the trailing NUL.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation from untrusted num_queues and queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables num_queues and queue_size are read directly from the vhost-user message payload without validation. The multiplication `num_queues * pervq_inflight_size` (both derived from attacker-controlled inputs) can overflow a uint64_t or, more practically, produce a small value when the true product is large. This results in a smaller-than-expected memory allocation via inflight_mem_alloc, while the subsequent loop iterates num_queues times, writing beyond the allocated buffer in the packed-queue initialization loop. An attacker controlling the vhost-user frontend could craft values that trigger a heap buffer overflow via memset or the descriptor initialization loop, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of num_queues and queue_size allows denial of service or memory corruption
vulnerabilities.long_description: Neither num_queues nor queue_size are checked against zero or reasonable upper bounds. A num_queues of 0 causes mmap_size to be 0, leading to a zero-length allocation followed by memset of size 0 (benign but unintended). A queue_size of 0 could cause get_pervq_shm_size_packed/split to return unexpected values. Excessively large values could cause enormous allocations (DoS) or, combined with the overflow above, undersized allocations leading to out-of-bounds writes in the packed descriptor initialization loop where `inflight_packed->desc[j]` is accessed for j up to queue_size-1.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image dimension sanity check allows bypass
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sanity check for image dimensions multiplies image->columns by image->rows as size_t values before casting to MagickSizeType. If both values are large (they come directly from attacker-controlled file data via cin.image.channel[0].pixels_per_line and cin.image.channel[0].lines_per_image), this multiplication can overflow, wrapping to a small value. Dividing by 8 further reduces it. This allows the check to pass with crafted enormous dimensions, potentially leading to excessive memory allocation in SetImageExtent/QueueAuthenticPixels or out-of-bounds access during pixel processing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Image depth set from untrusted input without validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: image->depth is set directly from cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel (a single byte read from the file) without any validation. A value of 0 or an unusually large value could cause undefined behavior or incorrect calculations in GetBytesPerRow and subsequent quantum pixel import operations. If depth is 0, the bytes-per-row calculation could return 0, leading to reading 0 bytes per row but still attempting to import pixels, potentially reading uninitialized memory. An excessively large depth could cause buffer over-reads during ImportQuantumPixels.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled large memory allocation via cin.file.user_length
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cin.file.user_length value is read directly from the file and used to allocate memory via BlobToStringInfo. While there is a check against GetBlobSize, an attacker can craft a file with a large blob size value and a correspondingly large user_length. Since GetBlobSize may return a large value for network streams or specially crafted files, this could lead to denial of service through excessive memory allocation controlled by the attacker.

commit_message:
io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
Reported-and-tested-by: syzbot+9671693590ef5aad8953@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Pavel Begunkov <asml.silence@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.
