commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Weak integer overflow check allows excessive memory allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sanity check divides the computed image size by 8 before comparing against the blob size. This means an attacker can craft a CIN file with columns and rows values whose product is up to 8x the actual file size, bypassing the check. This could lead to allocation of excessive memory in subsequent operations (SetImageExtent, quantum pixel allocation, row reads), potentially causing denial of service. Additionally, on 32-bit platforms, the multiplication `image->columns*image->rows` could overflow before the cast to MagickSizeType takes effect on the intermediate result.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated image depth from untrusted input may cause undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: The image depth is set directly from `cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel` (an unsigned char read from the file) without validation that it is a supported, non-zero value. A zero or unexpected depth value could cause division-by-zero or incorrect calculations in GetBytesPerRow and ImportQuantumPixels, leading to crashes or memory corruption. An attacker can craft a CIN file with bits_per_pixel set to 0 or an unsupported value to trigger these conditions.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that image dimensions are non-zero before use
vulnerabilities.long_description: The image columns and rows are set directly from attacker-controlled file data without checking they are non-zero. Zero dimensions could cause unexpected behavior in subsequent operations like GetBytesPerRow (which computes row length), QueueAuthenticPixels, and the pixel processing loop. A crafted CIN file with zero dimensions could trigger undefined behavior or bypass size checks.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220311080208.45047-1-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marc Kleine-Budde <mkl@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: mcba_usb_start_xmit in drivers/net/can/usb/mcba_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.17.1 has a double free.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using untrusted num_queues without bounds check against dev->nr_vring
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues is read directly from the client message payload and used as the loop bound when indexing into dev->virtqueue[i]. There is no check that num_queues <= dev->nr_vring. A malicious vhost-user client can supply a large num_queues value, causing the loop to read (and potentially write through) dev->virtqueue entries beyond the allocated array, leading to out-of-bounds memory access, information disclosure, or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that num_queues * pervq_inflight_size fits within the mmap'd region, allowing out-of-bounds writes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function mmaps a region of mmap_size bytes but then iterates num_queues times, advancing the addr pointer by pervq_inflight_size each iteration and writing to it. There is no check that (num_queues * pervq_inflight_size + mmap_offset) <= mmap_size. A malicious client could supply a small mmap_size with a large num_queues or queue_size, causing writes beyond the mapped memory region. This could corrupt adjacent memory or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow when computing pervq_inflight_size from untrusted queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The queue_size value is taken directly from the client message and passed to get_pervq_shm_size_split/packed. Depending on those functions' implementations, a large queue_size (up to 65535 as a uint16_t) could cause an integer overflow in the per-vq size calculation, resulting in a smaller-than-expected pervq_inflight_size. This would cause the loop's addr pointer to not advance enough, leading to overlapping inflight regions or out-of-bounds accesses within the mmap'd area.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one buffer overflow when null-terminating the line buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: After copying data into `line` (of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE), the code null-terminates at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. When `gopherState->len` has leftover data, `llen` is adjusted so that `gopherState->len + llen` can be at most `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`. After `llen += gopherState->len`, llen can be `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, and then `line[llen + 1]` writes to `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`, which is one byte past the end of the buffer. This is a classic off-by-one heap/stack overflow that can lead to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Format string argument mismatch: 5 specifiers but only 3 arguments provided
vulnerabilities.long_description: The format string contains five format specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only three arguments are passed (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). The missing arguments for `%c` (gtype) and the host `%s` mean the function reads uninitialized stack data. This is undefined behavior that can lead to information disclosure, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution depending on what values are found on the stack.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the HTML_CSO_RESULT parsing, `strtok` is called three times. Only the third result (`result`) is checked for NULL before use. If the line contains no colon delimiter, `s_code` could be valid but `s_recno` could be NULL. The code then passes potentially NULL pointers to `atoi()`, causing a null pointer dereference and crash. An attacker controlling gopher server responses could craft a malicious CSO result line to trigger this.

code_snippets: outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, host, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector + 5), html_quote(name));
outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, host, gtype, escaped_selector, html_quote(name));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Server-controlled 'host' and 'port' values injected into HTML without sanitization
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `host` and `port` values are parsed from the gopher server's response and inserted directly into HTML href attributes without any HTML escaping or validation. A malicious gopher server can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript by crafting host values containing special characters (e.g., quotes to break out of the attribute, followed by event handlers). This constitutes a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability that could be exploited to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's browser session.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using attacker-controlled num_queues without bounds check against dev->nr_vring
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues is read directly from the incoming message payload (ctx->msg.payload.inflight.num_queues) and used as the loop bound when indexing into dev->virtqueue[i]. There is no validation that num_queues <= dev->nr_vring. A malicious or buggy vhost-user frontend can supply a num_queues value larger than the allocated virtqueue array, causing out-of-bounds reads (dereferencing dev->virtqueue[i]) and out-of-bounds writes (setting inflight_packed/inflight_split on arbitrary memory). This can lead to information disclosure, heap corruption, or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that the mmap'd region is large enough to cover num_queues * pervq_inflight_size, risking out-of-bounds access within the mapped memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code maps mmap_size bytes but then iterates num_queues times, advancing addr by pervq_inflight_size each iteration, and writes to the memory at each offset (e.g., vq->inflight_split->desc_num = queue_size). There is no check that mmap_offset + num_queues * pervq_inflight_size <= mmap_size. A malicious frontend can craft values where the total inflight size exceeds the mapped region, causing writes past the end of the mmap'd area. Depending on the platform, this could trigger a SIGBUS or corrupt adjacent memory mappings.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in pervq_inflight_size computation from attacker-controlled queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: queue_size is a uint16_t read from the message payload and passed to get_pervq_shm_size_split/packed which likely multiplies it by a per-descriptor structure size. The result is stored in a uint32_t (pervq_inflight_size). Depending on the multiplication, this could wrap around, yielding a small pervq_inflight_size value. Subsequent pointer arithmetic (addr += pervq_inflight_size) would then keep addr within the mmap'd region while the code writes to structures expecting a larger layout, potentially corrupting adjacent queue inflight data or accessing memory out of bounds.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation can lead to undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: num_queues (uint16_t, up to 65535) and pervq_inflight_size (uint64_t, dependent on attacker-controlled queue_size) are multiplied without overflow checking. If pervq_inflight_size is large enough, the multiplication could wrap around on 64-bit or, more critically, the subsequent memset and packed-descriptor loop operate on the allocated region assuming the non-overflowed size, potentially writing out of bounds. Even without full 64-bit wrap, if the allocation succeeds with a smaller-than-expected size (e.g., via truncation in the allocator), the memset(addr, 0, mmap_size) and the loop writing to desc[j] for each queue would corrupt memory beyond the allocation. An attacker controlling num_queues and queue_size via the vhost-user protocol message can trigger this.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < queue_size; j++)
	inflight_packed->desc[j].next = j + 1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when queue_size exceeds the desc flexible array member bounds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates up to queue_size (attacker-controlled uint16_t) writing into inflight_packed->desc[j]. The pervq_inflight_size is computed by get_pervq_shm_size_packed(queue_size), but there is no validation that queue_size is non-zero or within a reasonable bound. If the size calculation in get_pervq_shm_size_packed does not account correctly for the struct header, or if the multiplication overflow above causes a smaller allocation, this loop writes past the allocated buffer. Additionally, desc[queue_size-1].next is set to queue_size which may be an invalid/out-of-range index used later.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak if inflight_mem_alloc succeeds but replaces a previous allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a new inflight fd is allocated via inflight_mem_alloc, the old fd stored in dev->inflight_info->fd is closed, but the newly allocated fd is stored in ctx->fds[0] = fd after the close. However, if the function were to fail between the allocation and the assignment (or if called repeatedly), the newly allocated fd from inflight_mem_alloc could leak. More specifically, if there is an error path after inflight_mem_alloc but before fd is assigned, the fd is never closed. While the current code path doesn't have an explicit error between these points, the pattern is fragile and the new fd from inflight_mem_alloc is not protected.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3884: crash when clearing the argument list while using it

Problem:    Crash when clearing the argument list while using it.
Solution:   Lock the argument list for ":all".
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Read

code_snippets:
(void)do_ecmd(0, alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i]), NULL, NULL,
		      ECMD_ONE,
		      ((buf_hide(curwin->w_buffer)
			   || bufIsChanged(curwin->w_buffer)) ? ECMD_HIDE : 0)
						       + ECMD_OLDBUF, curwin);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on argument list due to autocommand-triggered size changes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `opened_len` and `count` are set based on ARGCOUNT at the start of the function. Later, the loop iterates `i` from 0 to `count-1` and accesses `AARGLIST(alist)[i]` during the `do_ecmd()` call. However, autocommands triggered by `do_ecmd()`, `win_split()`, `win_close()`, or other operations can shrink the argument list (reduce `alist->al_ga.ga_len`) to fewer entries than the original `opened_len`. Since there is no check that `i < alist->al_ga.ga_len` before accessing `AARGLIST(alist)[i]`, this results in a heap-based out-of-bounds read. An attacker who can trigger specific autocommands (e.g., via crafted modelines or BufEnter/BufLeave autocommands) could exploit this to read memory beyond the argument list array, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one null terminator write causes buffer overflow and leaves line improperly terminated
vulnerabilities.long_description: The null terminator is written at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. This has two effects: (1) it writes one byte beyond the intended position, potentially past the `TEMP_BUF_SIZE` boundary (e.g., when `gopherState->len` was nonzero, `llen` is adjusted to `gopherState->len + original_llen` which can reach `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, making `llen + 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE` — a write exactly one past the buffer), and (2) the actual last character of `line` at index `llen` is left as whatever was previously in the buffer, meaning the string is not properly null-terminated at the correct position, leading to use of stale data in subsequent string operations like `strchr`, `strtok`, `html_quote`, etc.

code_snippets: snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector + 5), html_quote(name));
snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, gtype, escaped_selector, html_quote(name));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Cross-site scripting (XSS) via unescaped host and port values injected into HTML output
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `host` and `port` variables are derived from the gopher server response (attacker-controlled data) and are inserted directly into HTML anchor `href` attributes and surrounding HTML without HTML-escaping. While `name` is passed through `html_quote()`, `host` and `port` are not. A malicious gopher server could craft a response with a `host` field containing HTML/JavaScript (e.g., `"><script>alert(1)</script>`) to inject arbitrary content into the generated HTML page, leading to XSS attacks against users viewing the gopher directory listing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the CSO result parsing code, `strtok` is called three times to parse `s_code`, `s_recno`, and `result`. Only `result` is checked for NULL before use. If the line content doesn't contain the expected delimiters, `s_code` or `s_recno` could be NULL, and they are passed directly to `atoi()` without null checks, causing a null pointer dereference and crash. A malicious or malformed CSO server response with a line starting with '-' but lacking proper colon-delimited fields would trigger this.

commit_message:
media: rc: prevent memory leak in cx23888_ir_probe

In cx23888_ir_probe if kfifo_alloc fails the allocated memory for state
should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Sean Young <sean@mess.org>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab+samsung@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when kfifo_alloc fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If kfifo_alloc fails, the function returns -ENOMEM without freeing the previously allocated 'state' object, causing a memory leak. Every failed call to this function under memory pressure would leak sizeof(struct cx23888_ir_state) bytes of kernel memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when v4l2_device_register_subdev fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If v4l2_device_register_subdev fails (ret != 0), the code frees the kfifo but does not free the 'state' allocation itself, resulting in a memory leak of the cx23888_ir_state structure.

commit_message:
hamradio: improve the incomplete fix to avoid NPD

The previous commit 3e0588c291d6 ("hamradio: defer ax25 kfree after
unregister_netdev") reorder the kfree operations and unregister_netdev
operation to prevent UAF.

This commit improves the previous one by also deferring the nullify of
the ax->tty pointer. Otherwise, a NULL pointer dereference bug occurs.
Partial of the stack trace is shown below.

BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000538
RIP: 0010:ax_xmit+0x1f9/0x400
...
Call Trace:
 dev_hard_start_xmit+0xec/0x320
 sch_direct_xmit+0xea/0x240
 __qdisc_run+0x166/0x5c0
 __dev_queue_xmit+0x2c7/0xaf0
 ax25_std_establish_data_link+0x59/0x60
 ax25_connect+0x3a0/0x500
 ? security_socket_connect+0x2b/0x40
 __sys_connect+0x96/0xc0
 ? __hrtimer_init+0xc0/0xc0
 ? common_nsleep+0x2e/0x50
 ? switch_fpu_return+0x139/0x1a0
 __x64_sys_connect+0x11/0x20
 do_syscall_64+0x33/0x40
 entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xa9

The crash point is shown as below

static void ax_encaps(...) {
  ...
  set_bit(TTY_DO_WRITE_WAKEUP, &ax->tty->flags); // ax->tty = NULL!
  ...
}

By placing the nullify action after the unregister_netdev, the ax->tty
pointer won't be assigned as NULL net_device framework layer is well
synchronized.

Signed-off-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel in drivers/net/hamradio. This flaw allows a local attacker with a user privilege to cause a denial of service (DOS) when the mkiss or sixpack device is detached and reclaim resources early.

commit_message:
Bug 701815: avoid uninitialised bytes being >7, which broke indexing.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=jetp3852 ../bug-701815.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when padding the end of scan line data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `data` buffer is declared as `byte data[DATA_SIZE]`. The pointer `end_data` is initialized to `data + line_size` and decremented to strip trailing zeros, but can still point close to the end of the buffer. The `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` call writes 7 bytes starting at `end_data`, which can extend up to 7 bytes past `data + DATA_SIZE` when `end_data` is near or at `data + line_size` and `line_size` is close to `DATA_SIZE`. Since the check only ensures `line_size <= DATA_SIZE`, `end_data` can be as high as `data + DATA_SIZE`, causing a write of up to 7 bytes beyond the stack-allocated buffer. This can corrupt adjacent stack variables (e.g., `plane_data`) or the return address, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
rpmsg: virtio: Fix possible double free in rpmsg_virtio_add_ctrl_dev()

vch will be free in virtio_rpmsg_release_device() when
rpmsg_ctrldev_register_device() fails. There is no need to call
kfree() again.

Fixes: c486682ae1e2 ("rpmsg: virtio: Register the rpmsg_char device")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Arnaud Pouliquen <arnaud.pouliquen@foss.st.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220426060536.15594-3-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Mathieu Poirier <mathieu.poirier@linaro.org>
cve_desc: rpmsg_virtio_add_ctrl_dev in drivers/rpmsg/virtio_rpmsg_bus.c in the Linux kernel before 5.18.4 has a double free.

commit_message:
media: rc: prevent memory leak in cx23888_ir_probe

In cx23888_ir_probe if kfifo_alloc fails the allocated memory for state
should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Sean Young <sean@mess.org>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab+samsung@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when v4l2_device_register_subdev fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When v4l2_device_register_subdev() fails (ret != 0), the error path frees the kfifo but does not free the 'state' structure allocated with kzalloc at the beginning of the function, resulting in a memory leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated registration failures could exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220311080208.45047-1-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marc Kleine-Budde <mkl@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: mcba_usb_start_xmit in drivers/net/can/usb/mcba_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.17.1 has a double free.

commit_message: Fix oobread in symbols header parsing ##bin
cve_desc: Heap Buffer Overflow in parseDragons in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of buffer `b` on early return paths after r_buf_read_at failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `b` is allocated via malloc but is not freed before returning NULL when r_buf_read_at fails. This occurs in two places: the first read attempt and the retry read inside the magicCombo != 2 branch. Each call to parseDragons that hits these paths leaks `size` bytes of memory, which could be significant and lead to resource exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed integer type used for size with potential for integer issues when `off` is negative
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameter `off` is an `int` and `size` is `st64` (signed 64-bit). If `off` is negative, the check `off >= size` may pass (negative < positive), and then `size -= off` would increase size beyond the actual buffer, leading to a heap buffer over-read when `r_buf_read_at` is called with the inflated size, or an overly large malloc allocation.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3884: crash when clearing the argument list while using it

Problem:    Crash when clearing the argument list while using it.
Solution:   Lock the argument list for ":all".
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Read

code_snippets:
(void)do_ecmd(0, alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i]), NULL, NULL,
		      ECMD_ONE,
		      ((buf_hide(curwin->w_buffer)
			   || bufIsChanged(curwin->w_buffer)) ? ECMD_HIDE : 0)
						       + ECMD_OLDBUF, curwin);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on argument list due to autocommand-triggered size changes
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the second main loop, the code accesses AARGLIST(alist)[i] without verifying that i is still within the bounds of alist->al_ga.ga_len. The comment acknowledges 'ARGCOUNT may change while doing this, because of autocommands', but only opened_len (captured once at the start) is used to bound the loop. Autocommands triggered by do_ecmd(), win_split(), or win_close() can shrink the argument list. If the argument list shrinks below the current index i, AARGLIST(alist)[i] reads beyond the allocated argument array, potentially leading to a crash or information disclosure. An attacker who can influence autocommands (e.g., via a crafted modeline or BufEnter autocmd) could exploit this to read or corrupt heap memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stale opened[] array can be indexed with outdated bounds after argument list mutation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The opened[] array is allocated with size ARGCOUNT at function entry and never resized. During the first loop, autocommands from autowrite() or win_close() can modify the argument list. If the argument list grows larger than opened_len, subsequent accesses in the first loop's inner iteration (bounded by opened_len) are safe, but the w_arg_idx values set on windows may become inconsistent. More critically, in the second loop the variable 'count' is capped to opened_len which was the original ARGCOUNT. If the argument list shrinks, the loop still iterates up to the original count while accessing both opened[i] (which may reference stale data for indices that no longer correspond to valid arguments) and AARGLIST(alist)[i] (out of bounds). This can cause heap out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message: Fix oobread in symbols header parsing ##bin
cve_desc: Heap Buffer Overflow in parseDragons in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of allocated buffer `b` on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `b` is allocated via malloc but is not freed when the function returns NULL after the r_buf_read_at calls fail. The first early return after `r_buf_read_at` at the initial read, and the second early return inside the magicCombo retry block, both leak the allocated buffer `b`. Additionally, the `file_name` parameter is marked R_OWN (caller transfers ownership) but is also leaked on these error paths. An attacker providing a malformed input that triggers these error paths repeatedly could cause significant memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when computing `off - 8` without bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `magicCombo != 2`, the code reads from `off - 8` without verifying that `off >= 8`. If `off` is less than 8, `off - 8` underflows (since `off` is an `int`, this becomes a negative value), which could cause `r_buf_read_at` to read from an unexpected offset. Depending on the implementation of `r_buf_read_at`, this could lead to out-of-bounds reads or other undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow due to no validation of sizes against destination buffer capacity
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function writes left_width + (num_tiles * src_width) + right_width bytes into dest_strip without any bounds checking. If the caller supplies inconsistent or attacker-influenced values for these parameters, the sequential memcpy calls can write past the end of the dest_strip buffer, leading to heap or stack corruption, code execution, or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from src_strip when left_offset + left_width exceeds src_width
vulnerabilities.long_description: The first memcpy reads left_width bytes starting at src_strip + left_offset. There is no validation that left_offset + left_width <= the actual size of src_strip. If left_offset is too large or left_width extends past the source buffer, this results in an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. Similarly, right_width is not checked against src_width for the final memcpy.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of tp and tpn when both S4U2Self and constrained delegation paths are taken
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables `tp` and `tpn` are initially set to `cp` and `cpn`. In the S4U2Self code path, `tp` and `tpn` are reassigned to newly allocated values. Later, in the constrained delegation code path (which can co-occur when client != NULL, PA_FOR_USER padata is present, additional_tickets are present, cname_in_addl_tkt is set, and enc_tkt_in_skey == 0), `tp` and `tpn` are overwritten again via _krb5_principalname2krb5_principal() and krb5_unparse_name() without first freeing the values allocated in the S4U2Self path. The cleanup code at `out:` only frees the final values of `tp`/`tpn`, so the intermediate allocations are leaked. An attacker could craft requests triggering both paths repeatedly to cause memory exhaustion in the KDC, leading to denial of service.

code_snippets:
ret = check_PAC(context, config, cp, NULL,
		    client, server, krbtgt,
		    &tkey_check->key,
		    ekey, &tkey_sign->key,
		    tgt, &rspac, &signedpath);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference when cross-realm client is not found in database
vulnerabilities.long_description: When _kdc_db_fetch() for the client principal fails (ret != 0) and the client is from a different realm than the krbtgt, the code logs a message but does NOT goto out. Execution continues with `client` potentially being NULL and `ret` being non-zero. The code then calls check_PAC() passing the potentially NULL `client` pointer. If check_PAC or subsequent code dereferences this NULL pointer without checking, it will cause a crash. A cross-realm attacker whose principal intentionally does not exist in the KDC's database could trigger this code path, potentially causing a denial of service via KDC crash.

commit_message:
Bug 701815: avoid uninitialised bytes being >7, which broke indexing.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=jetp3852 ../bug-701815.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when scan line data bytes exceed index 7 in 8-element lookup tables
vulnerabilities.long_description: The lookup tables spr40, spr8, and spr2 each contain only 8 elements (indices 0-7), but they are indexed by byte values from the scan line data (dp[0] through dp[7]), which can range from 0 to 255. While the code comment acknowledges that uninitialized bytes are zeroed to prevent this, the actual scan line data read from gdev_prn_copy_scan_lines is not validated. If any byte in the raster data has a value greater than 7, the array accesses (e.g., spr40[dp[0]]) will read out of bounds, potentially leaking stack data or causing a crash. An attacker who can control the input document rendered by the printer driver could craft pixel data with values exceeding 7, triggering the out-of-bounds read and potentially exploiting the resulting memory disclosure or denial of service.

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in string length rounding and offset calculation can bypass bounds checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing CDF_LENGTH32_STRING/CDF_LENGTH32_WSTRING vectors, the string length `l` is a uint32_t read from the stream. If `l` is 0xFFFFFFFF, the `if (l & 1) l++` causes it to wrap to 0, so `slen += 0` and `o4` does not advance. This causes subsequent loop iterations to re-read from the same position. Additionally, accumulated large values of `l >> 1` added to `slen` can cause `slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` to overflow (especially on 32-bit platforms), resulting in a small `o4` value. This makes the bounds check `o4 + l > left` or `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` pass incorrectly, leading to out-of-bounds heap reads. An attacker can craft a malicious CDF file to trigger this, potentially leaking sensitive heap data or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-write/over-read due to *count not being updated after growing info array for vector strings
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CDF_VECTOR of strings is encountered with nelements > 1, `cdf_grow_info` is called to expand the info buffer by `nelements`. However, `*count` was previously only incremented by `sh.sh_properties` and is never updated to account for the additional elements consumed by the vector. This means subsequent calls or the caller will have an incorrect count, and the inner loop writes to `inp[i]` where `i` indexes based on the outer property loop counter. If a second vector string property is encountered in the same section, the grow/nelem recalculation combined with stale count can lead to writes beyond the properly tracked buffer bounds, causing heap corruption. A malicious CDF file with crafted property counts and vector string elements can exploit this for code execution.

commit_message: don't expand string case folds to alternatives if code length == 1 and byte length is same
cve_desc: Oniguruma through 6.9.3, as used in PHP 7.3.x and other products, has a heap-based buffer over-read in str_lower_case_match in regexec.c.

code_snippets: r = expand_case_fold_string_alt(n, items, p, len, end, reg, &prev_node);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read due to missing bounds check on enclen() result against remaining buffer size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function computes `len = enclen(reg->enc, p)` which returns the expected byte length of a character at position `p` based on the encoding. However, there is no check that `p + len <= end`. If the input string is truncated or malformed (e.g., a multi-byte character that starts near the end of the buffer but whose trailing bytes are missing), `len` may be larger than the remaining bytes (`end - p`). This causes `onig_node_str_cat(snode, p, p + len)` and `expand_case_fold_string_alt(n, items, p, len, end, ...)` to read beyond the allocated buffer, and `p += len` to advance past `end`, potentially skipping the `while (p < end)` termination condition on subsequent iterations. An attacker who can supply a crafted regex pattern with a malformed multi-byte string can trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive heap data or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in alt_num multiplication before threshold check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `alt_num` is multiplied by `(n + 1)` on each iteration where case fold codes are found. Since `n` can be up to `ONIGENC_GET_CASE_FOLD_CODES_MAX_NUM` and `alt_num` is a signed `int`, repeated multiplications can cause a signed integer overflow before the threshold comparison is evaluated. A signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C and could wrap to a small or negative value, causing the threshold check `alt_num > THRESHOLD_CASE_FOLD_ALT_FOR_EXPANSION` to pass when it should not. This could lead to an exponential blowup in the number of alternative nodes created, resulting in excessive memory consumption or other unexpected behavior.

commit_message:
e1000: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
Fix XkbSelectEvents() integer underflow

CVE-2020-14361 ZDI-CAN 11573

This vulnerability was discovered by:
Jan-Niklas Sohn working with Trend Micro Zero Day Initiative

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow check occurs after the write, not before
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function attempts to detect a heap buffer overflow for the \<special_key> case, but the check `if (end >= rettv->vval.v_string + len)` is performed AFTER `trans_special` has already written data into the buffer via the `end` pointer. If `trans_special` writes more bytes than the allocated buffer can hold (e.g., due to a mismatch between the first-pass `extra += 5` estimation and the actual bytes produced), the heap buffer is already corrupted by the time the check fires. The `iemsg` call merely logs a message but does not prevent the overflow or undo the damage. An attacker could craft a string with special key sequences that cause `trans_special` to write more data than the pre-computed `extra` accounts for, leading to heap corruption, potentially enabling code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing allocation size via cast to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer length `len` is computed as `(int)(p - *arg + extra)`. If the input string is very large, `p - *arg` (a `ptrdiff_t`, typically 64-bit on 64-bit platforms) could exceed `INT_MAX` when cast to `int`, causing undefined behavior or a sign-wrap to a negative value. If `len` becomes a small positive value or wraps around, `alloc(len)` would allocate an undersized buffer, and the subsequent copy loop would write far beyond the allocated region, resulting in a severe heap buffer overflow. This could be exploited by providing an extremely long string literal to the parser.

commit_message:
LogFilePrep: add a comment to the unsafe format string.

CVE-2018-14665 also made it possible to exploit this to access
memory. With -logfile forbidden when running with elevated privileges
this is no longer an issue.

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
Reviewed-by: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
(cherry picked from commit 248d164eae27f1f310266d78e52f13f64362f81e)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in xorg-x11-server before 1.20.3. An incorrect permission check for -modulepath and -logfile options when starting Xorg. X server allows unprivileged users with the ability to log in to the system via physical console to escalate their privileges and run arbitrary code under root privileges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: User-controlled format strings passed to asprintf
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameters 'fname' and 'backup' are used directly as format strings in asprintf calls. If a caller passes attacker-controlled data for these parameters, an attacker could supply format specifiers (e.g., %s, %n, %x) to read from or write to arbitrary memory, leading to information disclosure or code execution. While the intent is that these contain a single %s placeholder for 'idstring', there is no validation that the format string is safe.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'suffix' if second asprintf fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the first asprintf (for 'suffix') succeeds but the second asprintf (for 'oldLog') fails, FatalError is called without freeing 'suffix'. If FatalError does not terminate the program, this results in a memory leak. Similarly, 'logFileName' is leaked in several FatalError paths.

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in slen/o4 calculation during vector string parsing can lead to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the CDF_LENGTH32_STRING/CDF_LENGTH32_WSTRING vector processing loop, the variable `slen` is accumulated via `slen += l >> 1` on each iteration. If a crafted file specifies a large number of elements with large string lengths, `slen` can overflow (it is a size_t but on 32-bit systems this is feasible, and the multiplication `o4 = slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` can also overflow). When `o4` wraps around to a small value, the subsequent bounds checks `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` and `o4 + l > left` will incorrectly pass, allowing reads from `q[o4]` that are well beyond the valid buffer region `e`. This can be exploited by a specially crafted CDF file to read out-of-bounds heap memory, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write when multiple vector string properties cause buffer accounting mismatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code initially allocates space for `sh.sh_properties` entries and sets `*count += sh.sh_properties`. When a vector string property is encountered, it calls `cdf_grow_info` to add `nelements` entries. However, if multiple properties in the same section are vector strings, each one calls `cdf_grow_info(info, maxcount, nelements)` independently. The inner loop writes to `inp[i]` where `i` is shared with the outer loop. While the inner loop bounds `i < sh.sh_properties`, the initial allocation only reserved `sh.sh_properties` slots from the `inp` base. After reallocation, `inp` is recalculated but `*count` is not updated to reflect the additional vector elements being written. A carefully crafted file with specific property configurations could result in writes beyond the allocated buffer, causing heap corruption.

commit_message:
LogFilePrep: add a comment to the unsafe format string.

CVE-2018-14665 also made it possible to exploit this to access
memory. With -logfile forbidden when running with elevated privileges
this is no longer an issue.

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
Reviewed-by: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
(cherry picked from commit 248d164eae27f1f310266d78e52f13f64362f81e)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in xorg-x11-server before 1.20.3. An incorrect permission check for -modulepath and -logfile options when starting Xorg. X server allows unprivileged users with the ability to log in to the system via physical console to escalate their privileges and run arbitrary code under root privileges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: User-controlled format string passed to asprintf
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameters 'fname' and 'backup' are used directly as format strings in asprintf calls. Although the comment acknowledges the format string is user-controlled and says elevated privileges shouldn't apply, if this assumption is ever violated or the caller passes untrusted input, an attacker could supply format specifiers like %n or %x to read/write arbitrary memory. This is a classic format string vulnerability. Even without elevated privileges, it can lead to information disclosure or code execution in the context of the running process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'suffix' when second asprintf fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the first asprintf for 'suffix' succeeds but the second asprintf for 'oldLog' fails, the code jumps to FatalError without freeing 'suffix'. Depending on whether FatalError terminates the process or not, this constitutes a memory leak. If FatalError does not terminate (e.g., via longjmp), 'suffix' is leaked.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition between stat and rename
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is a time-of-check-time-of-use race between the stat() call that checks if the log file is a regular file and the subsequent rename() call. An attacker could replace the file with a symlink between these two operations, potentially causing the rename to operate on an unintended target. This could be exploited for symlink attacks to overwrite or manipulate arbitrary files.

commit_message:
memslot: Fix off-by-one error in group/slot boundary check

RedMemSlotInfo keeps an array of groups, and each group contains an
array of slots. Unfortunately, these checks are off by 1, they check
that the index is greater or equal to the number of elements in the
array, while these arrays are 0 based. The check should only check for
strictly greater than the number of elements.

For the group array, this is not a big issue, as these memslot groups
are created by spice-server users (eg QEMU), and the group ids used to
index that array are also generated by the spice-server user, so it
should not be possible for the guest to set them to arbitrary values.

The slot id is more problematic, as it's calculated from a QXLPHYSICAL
address, and such addresses are usually set by the guest QXL driver, so
the guest can set these to arbitrary values, including malicious values,
which are probably easy to build from the guest PCI configuration.

This patch fixes the arrays bound check, and adds a test case for this.
This fixes CVE-2019-3813.

Signed-off-by: Christophe Fergeau <cfergeau@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Frediano Ziglio <fziglio@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Spice, versions 0.5.2 through 0.14.1, are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read due to an off-by-one error in memslot_get_virt. This may lead to a denial of service, or, in the worst case, code-execution by unauthenticated attackers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one errors in group_id and slot_id boundary checks allow out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both boundary checks use '>' instead of '>='. If group_id equals num_memslots_groups or slot_id equals num_memslots, the checks pass but the subsequent array accesses (info->mem_slots[group_id][slot_id]) will read one element past the allocated arrays. An attacker who can control the QXLPHYSICAL address or group_id could trigger an out-of-bounds memory read, potentially leading to information disclosure or code execution.

commit_message:
jfs: prevent NULL deref in diFree

Add validation check for JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap to prevent a NULL deref
in diFree since diFree uses it without do any validations.
When function jfs_mount calls diMount to initialize fileset inode
allocation map, it can fail and JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap won't be
initialized. Then it calls diFreeSpecial to close fileset inode allocation
map inode and it will flow into jfs_evict_inode. Function jfs_evict_inode
just validates JFS_SBI(inode->i_sb)->ipimap, then calls diFree. diFree use
JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap directly, then it will cause a NULL deref.

Reported-by: TCS Robot <tcs_robot@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Haimin Zhang <tcs_kernel@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dave Kleikamp <dave.kleikamp@oracle.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw in diFree in fs/jfs/inode.c in Journaled File System (JFS)in the Linux kernel. This could allow a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table due to missing bounds checks on 'bytes' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'bytes' is used as an offset into 'directory_table' for multiple memcpy operations, but there is no validation that 'bytes + sizeof(...)' or 'bytes + dire->size + 1' stays within the actual allocated size of directory_table. The while loop condition 'bytes < size' only checks against a value derived from inode metadata (which could be corrupted), not the actual buffer size. A crafted squashfs image could cause reads past the end of the directory_table buffer, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculation can bypass the while-loop bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'size' variable is computed as '(*i)->data + bytes', where both values come from potentially attacker-controlled filesystem metadata. If their sum overflows the integer type, 'size' could wrap to a small or negative value, causing the while loop to either be skipped (missing data) or, combined with other issues, lead to unexpected behavior. This undermines the loop's role as a bounds check for directory parsing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential fixed-size buffer overflow when copying directory entry name via strcpy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The directory entry name (up to SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN bytes) is copied into dir->dirs[dir->dir_count].name using strcpy. If the 'name' field in struct dir_ent is a fixed-size buffer smaller than SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN + 1, this will overflow it. Even though dire->size is checked to be less than SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN, the resulting string could still exceed the destination buffer size, leading to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: don't expand string case folds to alternatives if code length == 1 and byte length is same
cve_desc: Oniguruma through 6.9.3, as used in PHP 7.3.x and other products, has a heap-based buffer over-read in str_lower_case_match in regexec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing validation of character length against remaining buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is set from `enclen(reg->enc, p)` without verifying that `p + len <= end`. If `enclen` returns a length that extends beyond the `end` pointer (e.g., a truncated multi-byte character at the end of the string), subsequent operations that use `p` and `len` (such as passing them to `expand_case_fold_string_alt`, `is_good_case_fold_items_for_search`, `onig_node_str_cat`, or `ONIGENC_MBC_CASE_FOLD`) can read past the buffer boundary. Additionally, `p += len` can advance `p` past `end`, causing the `while (p < end)` loop to continue with an invalid pointer, leading to further out-of-bounds reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in alt_num multiplication before threshold check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `alt_num` variable (an `int`) is multiplied by `(n + 1)` each iteration before being checked against THRESHOLD_CASE_FOLD_ALT_FOR_EXPANSION. If `n` is large enough or the multiplication occurs enough times, `alt_num` can overflow, potentially wrapping to a small or negative value. This would cause the threshold check to be bypassed, allowing the loop to continue and potentially generate an exponential number of alternative nodes, leading to excessive memory consumption or other unexpected behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of prev_node when top_root is NULL in error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error handling path at the `err` label, only `top_root` is freed. However, if `top_root` is still NULL (which occurs when `prev_node` has been allocated but never incorporated into a list/tree that sets `top_root`), the allocated `prev_node` is never freed, resulting in a memory leak. This can happen when an error occurs early in the loop before `root`/`top_root` have been set up, but after `prev_node` has been assigned a new node.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0360: crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored

Problem:    Crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored.
Solution:   Do not check breakpoint for non-existing line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0360.

commit_message:
mtab: handle ENOSPC/EFBIG condition properly when altering mtab

It's possible that when mount.cifs goes to append the mtab that there
won't be enough space to do so, and the mntent won't be appended to the
file in its entirety.

Add a my_endmntent routine that will fflush and then fsync the FILE if
that succeeds. If either fails then it will truncate the file back to
its provided size. It will then call endmntent unconditionally.

Have add_mtab call fstat on the opened mtab file in order to get the
size of the file before it has been appended. Assuming that that
succeeds, use my_endmntent to ensure that the file is not corrupted
before closing it. It's possible that we'll have a small race window
where the mtab is incorrect, but it should be quickly corrected.

This was reported some time ago as CVE-2011-1678:

    http://openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2011/03/04/9

...and it seems to fix the reproducer that I was able to come up with.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Suresh Jayaraman <sjayaraman@suse.de>
cve_desc: smbfs in Samba 3.5.8 and earlier attempts to use (1) mount.cifs to append to the /etc/mtab file and (2) umount.cifs to append to the /etc/mtab.tmp file without first checking whether resource limits would interfere, which allows local users to trigger corruption of the /etc/mtab file via a process with a small RLIMIT_FSIZE value, a related issue to CVE-2011-1089.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0360: crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored

Problem:    Crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored.
Solution:   Do not check breakpoint for non-existing line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0360.

size of the file before it has been appended. Assuming that that

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

commit_message:
memslot: Fix off-by-one error in group/slot boundary check

RedMemSlotInfo keeps an array of groups, and each group contains an
array of slots. Unfortunately, these checks are off by 1, they check
that the index is greater or equal to the number of elements in the
array, while these arrays are 0 based. The check should only check for
strictly greater than the number of elements.

For the group array, this is not a big issue, as these memslot groups
are created by spice-server users (eg QEMU), and the group ids used to
index that array are also generated by the spice-server user, so it
should not be possible for the guest to set them to arbitrary values.

The slot id is more problematic, as it's calculated from a QXLPHYSICAL
address, and such addresses are usually set by the guest QXL driver, so
the guest can set these to arbitrary values, including malicious values,
which are probably easy to build from the guest PCI configuration.

This patch fixes the arrays bound check, and adds a test case for this.
This fixes CVE-2019-3813.

Signed-off-by: Christophe Fergeau <cfergeau@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Frediano Ziglio <fziglio@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Spice, versions 0.5.2 through 0.14.1, are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read due to an off-by-one error in memslot_get_virt. This may lead to a denial of service, or, in the worst case, code-execution by unauthenticated attackers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when adding address_delta to the virtual address
vulnerabilities.long_description: The virtual address `h_virt` (an unsigned long) is added to `slot->address_delta` without overflow checking. If `address_delta` is large enough, the addition can wrap around, producing a small value that could pass the subsequent `memslot_validate_virt` bounds check but point to an unintended (attacker-controlled) memory location. A malicious guest could craft a QXLPHYSICAL address that, combined with a particular address_delta, causes this wraparound, potentially leading to arbitrary memory read/write from the host process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow when validating h_virt with add_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `memslot_validate_virt` function receives `h_virt` and `add_size` (uint32_t). If the validation internally computes `h_virt + add_size` without overflow checking, an attacker could supply values where `h_virt` is near the maximum of `unsigned long`, and `add_size` causes a wraparound. This would make the end address appear to be within valid bounds while actually allowing out-of-bounds memory access. The caller has no protection against this overflow before invoking the validation function.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double va_start invocation leads to undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: va_start is called twice on the same va_list without an intervening va_end. Per the C standard, calling va_start a second time without first calling va_end results in undefined behavior. Depending on the platform and compiler, this could corrupt the stack, produce incorrect output, or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one in length tracking can cause out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the message is split across lines, `len -= cut` does not account for the extra +1 character that `ptr` is advanced by (`ptr += cut+1`). Over multiple iterations, `len` becomes larger than the actual remaining string length. Since the loop condition is `while(len > 0)`, the loop may continue iterating past the end of the buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads from `ptr` which could read past the stack-allocated `print_buffer`, potentially leaking sensitive stack data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table due to missing bounds checks on 'bytes' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'bytes' is used as an offset into the 'directory_table' buffer for multiple memcpy operations, but its value is never validated against the actual size of 'directory_table'. The 'bytes' value is derived from filesystem metadata (lookup_entry, (*i)->offset, (*i)->data) which can be attacker-controlled in a malicious squashfs image. The while loop condition only checks 'bytes < size', where 'size' is also derived from filesystem data, so a crafted image could cause reads far beyond the allocated directory_table buffer, leading to information disclosure or crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow when copying directory entry name into fixed-size dir_ent.name field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies dire->name (which can be up to SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN+1 bytes including null terminator) into dir->dirs[dir->dir_count].name using strcpy without verifying that the destination buffer in struct dir_ent is large enough. If the 'name' field in struct dir_ent is smaller than SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN+1, a crafted filesystem with long filenames could overflow the destination buffer, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculation and realloc size computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'size' variable is computed as '(*i)->data + bytes - 3', where both (*i)->data and bytes come from filesystem metadata. If these values are large, the addition could overflow, producing a smaller-than-expected 'size' value, which would cause the while loop to terminate early or behave unexpectedly. Similarly, '(dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent)' in the realloc call could overflow on 32-bit platforms, resulting in a small allocation followed by out-of-bounds writes.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table due to missing bounds validation on 'bytes' against directory_table size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'bytes' is computed from attacker-influenced inode data ((*i)->start, (*i)->offset, (*i)->data) and is used as an index into directory_table for multiple memcpy operations without ever validating that it (plus the size being read) stays within the allocated bounds of directory_table. A crafted squashfs image could set these inode fields to cause reads past the end of directory_table, leading to information disclosure or crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in computing 'size' can bypass the while-loop bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'size' variable is computed as the sum of (*i)->data (from a potentially malicious inode) and 'bytes'. If (*i)->data is large enough, this addition can overflow an int, producing a value smaller than 'bytes'. This would cause the while(bytes < size) loop to terminate immediately (skipping legitimate entries) or, depending on the values, could cause the loop to run with unexpected bounds, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow copying directory entry name into fixed-size dir_ent.name field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The name from dire->name (up to SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN bytes) is copied via strcpy into dir->dirs[dir->dir_count].name, which is a fixed-size buffer in struct dir_ent. If SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN exceeds the size of the name field in struct dir_ent, or if the check `dire->size >= SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN` is insufficient (off-by-one considering the null terminator written at dire->name[dire->size + 1]), this could overflow the destination buffer on the heap, potentially allowing code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in realloc size calculation could lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: If dir->dir_count grows very large (through a malicious filesystem with many directory entries across repeated iterations), the expression (dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent) could overflow, causing realloc to allocate a much smaller buffer than expected. Subsequent writes to dir->dirs[dir->dir_count] would then write out of bounds on the heap, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.
