indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

commit_message:
af_key: Do not call xfrm_probe_algs in parallel

When namespace support was added to xfrm/afkey, it caused the
previously single-threaded call to xfrm_probe_algs to become
multi-threaded.  This is buggy and needs to be fixed with a mutex.

Reported-by: Abhishek Shah <abhishek.shah@columbia.edu>
Fixes: 283bc9f35bbb ("xfrm: Namespacify xfrm state/policy locks")
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A race condition was found in the Linux kernel's IP framework for transforming packets (XFRM subsystem) when multiple calls to xfrm_probe_algs occurred simultaneously. This flaw could allow a local attacker to potentially trigger an out-of-bounds write or leak kernel heap memory by performing an out-of-bounds read and copying it into a socket.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in octet count parsing with no upper bound check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The octet count is accumulated by multiplying by 10 and adding the digit value without any upper bound check during parsing. If an attacker sends a very long string of digits, iOctetsRemain (an int) will overflow. This can wrap to a negative value, which then flows into the octet-counting frame processing logic. A negative octatesToCopy passed to memcpy would be implicitly cast to a very large size_t, causing a massive heap buffer overflow when copying into pThis->pMsg.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow via negative octatesToCopy passed to memcpy
vulnerabilities.long_description: If iOctetsRemain becomes negative (e.g., due to the integer overflow above, or if iOctetsRemain is 0 and still reaches this code path), octatesToCopy will be negative. The signed comparison `buffLen < octatesToCopy` will evaluate to false when octatesToCopy is negative and buffLen is positive. The subsequent check `octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine` may also fail to trigger because of the negative value. The negative octatesToCopy is then passed to memcpy where it is implicitly converted to a very large size_t value, resulting in an out-of-bounds heap write that can be exploited for remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer underflow if iMsg exceeds iMaxLine
vulnerabilities.long_description: If pThis->iMsg somehow exceeds iMaxLine, the expression `iMaxLine - pThis->iMsg` yields a negative value. This results in a negative octatesToCopy being passed to memcpy (interpreted as a huge size_t), causing a heap buffer overflow. While iMsg is normally bounded, the overflow path in the octet-stuffing branch calls doSubmitMsg but does not reset iMsg, and depending on initialization/reuse patterns, iMsg could be at or beyond iMaxLine when entering the octet-counting branch.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when appending the unreplaced portion of a bad word to wcopy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The stack buffer `wcopy` has size MAXWLEN + 2. The code first copies the suggestion word into `wcopy` (up to MAXWLEN bytes), then if `su_badlen > st_orglen`, it appends the remainder of the bad word at offset `stp->st_wordlen`. If `st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN (e.g., the suggestion is a long word) and `su_badlen - st_orglen` is greater than zero, the write at `wcopy + st_wordlen` can exceed the MAXWLEN + 2 byte buffer boundary. An attacker who can influence spell suggestion data (e.g., via a crafted spellfile) could trigger this overflow to corrupt stack memory, potentially achieving code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when appending the unreplaced remainder to wcopy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `wcopy` is declared with size MAXWLEN + 2. The first `vim_strncpy` copies up to MAXWLEN characters of `stp->st_word` into `wcopy`. Then, when `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen`, the code appends the remainder at offset `stp->st_wordlen` with a length limit of `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen`. However, there is no check that `stp->st_wordlen + (sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` fits within the MAXWLEN + 2 bound of `wcopy`. If `st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN (which the first vim_strncpy permits) and the unreplaced portion `(su_badlen - st_orglen)` is non-trivial, the second write will go past the end of the stack buffer. This can corrupt adjacent stack variables, saved return addresses, or other data, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
af_key: Do not call xfrm_probe_algs in parallel

When namespace support was added to xfrm/afkey, it caused the
previously single-threaded call to xfrm_probe_algs to become
multi-threaded.  This is buggy and needs to be fixed with a mutex.

Reported-by: Abhishek Shah <abhishek.shah@columbia.edu>
Fixes: 283bc9f35bbb ("xfrm: Namespacify xfrm state/policy locks")
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A race condition was found in the Linux kernel's IP framework for transforming packets (XFRM subsystem) when multiple calls to xfrm_probe_algs occurred simultaneously. This flaw could allow a local attacker to potentially trigger an out-of-bounds write or leak kernel heap memory by performing an out-of-bounds read and copying it into a socket.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow due to unchecked keylen used in memcpy before sanity check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads `skey->keylen` from attacker-controlled message data and immediately uses it as the length argument to `memcpy` to copy into the heap-allocated buffer `skey` (which was allocated with `size` bytes from `msg_data_sz`). The sanity check comparing `size` against `tipc_aead_key_size(skey)` only happens *after* the `memcpy` has already executed. An attacker can craft a message where the embedded `keylen` field is much larger than the actual allocated buffer size, causing a heap buffer overflow. This can overwrite adjacent heap metadata or objects, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution in the kernel. Additionally, a large `skey->keylen` could also cause an out-of-bounds read from the `data` source buffer.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Guest-controlled `args.nargs` used without bounds checking leads to out-of-bounds stack access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value `args.nargs` is read directly from guest-controlled memory and converted via `be32_to_cpu()` without any validation that it falls within the bounds of the `args.args[]` array. The resulting pointer `args.rets` is then passed to an RTAS handler which reads/writes through it. If a malicious guest supplies a large `nargs` value, `args.rets` will point outside the `args` structure on the host kernel stack. The RTAS handler will then perform out-of-bounds reads or writes on the kernel stack, potentially allowing the guest to corrupt kernel memory, escalate privileges, or cause a host kernel crash.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when constructing user_name in tilde expansion
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling '~user/...' patterns, the code allocates `end_name - dirname` bytes for `newp` (where `end_name` points to the '/' in dirname). In the non-unescape path, it then calls `mempcpy(newp, dirname + 1, end_name - dirname)` which copies `end_name - dirname` bytes (the username characters *plus* the '/' character) filling the entire buffer, and then writes a '\0' terminator one byte past the end of the allocated buffer. For example, if dirname is "~user/foo" (end_name points to '/'), `end_name - dirname` is 5, so 5 bytes are allocated. Then 5 bytes ("user/") are copied via mempcpy, and '\0' is written at offset 5, which is 1 byte out of bounds. This is a classic off-by-one overflow that could corrupt heap metadata or adjacent data, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. The fix should either allocate `end_name - dirname + 1` bytes or copy only `end_name - dirname - 1` bytes (excluding the '/' separator).

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds validation on subrectangle coordinates allows out-of-bounds write via GotFillRect
vulnerabilities.long_description: The subrectangle coordinates (x, y, w, h) are read directly from the server without any validation that they fall within the parent rectangle (rx, ry, rw, rh) or the framebuffer dimensions. A malicious server can supply subrectangle values where rx+x+w or ry+y+h exceed the framebuffer boundaries, causing GotFillRect to write pixel data outside the allocated framebuffer. This is a heap-based buffer overflow that could be exploited for remote code execution by a rogue or compromised VNC server.

commit_message:
cgroup-v1: Require capabilities to set release_agent

The cgroup release_agent is called with call_usermodehelper.  The function
call_usermodehelper starts the release_agent with a full set fo capabilities.
Therefore require capabilities when setting the release_agaent.

Reported-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Fixes: 81a6a5cdd2c5 ("Task Control Groups: automatic userspace notification of idle cgroups")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v2.6.24+
Signed-off-by: "Eric W. Biederman" <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel’s cgroup_release_agent_write in the kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c function. This flaw, under certain circumstances, allows the use of the cgroups v1 release_agent feature to escalate privileges and bypass the namespace isolation unexpectedly.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `in` buffer when mlx5_vector2eqn() fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `in` is allocated via kvzalloc, if mlx5_vector2eqn() fails, the code jumps to `err_cqwq` which only calls mlx5_wq_destroy() but never frees `in`. This results in a kernel memory leak every time this error path is taken. The fix should jump to a label that calls kvfree(in) before destroying the work queue.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer write without bounds check when accumulating octet count digits
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the eInOctetCnt state, each digit character of the octet count is written to pThis->pMsg at offset pThis->iMsg without any bounds check against iMaxLine or the actual buffer size. While the numeric range check (iOctetsRemain <= 200000000) limits the number of digits to roughly 9-10, there is no explicit validation that iMsg stays within the pMsg buffer bounds. If this function is called repeatedly while remaining in eInOctetCnt (e.g., a malformed stream keeps sending digits), iMsg could increment beyond the buffer boundary.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow leading to heap buffer overflow in octet-counting memcpy
vulnerabilities.long_description: If pThis->iMsg is already greater than iMaxLine (which can happen since digits are written to the buffer without bounds checks in the eInOctetCnt state), the expression `iMaxLine - pThis->iMsg` produces a negative value. This negative int is then assigned to octatesToCopy. When passed to memcpy, this negative int is implicitly converted to a very large size_t value, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a TCP message stream that pushes iMsg past iMaxLine during octet count parsing, then triggers the memcpy path to achieve arbitrary memory corruption.

commit_message: libnsgif: fix oob in lzw_decode
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to unbounded writes to the output stack
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function writes to the stack buffer via `stack_pos` without any bounds checking against the allocated size of `ctx->stack_base`. A crafted LZW stream can produce dictionary chains of arbitrary length, causing the while loop (and the initial write in the 'code not in table' case) to write past the end of the stack buffer. This can lead to heap corruption, code execution, or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds or uninitialized table read when code_new equals current_entry at maximum table size
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `code_new == current_entry` (the 'code not in table' case) and `current_entry >= (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX)`, the new dictionary entry is NOT written to the table (due to the size guard). However, afterward, `table[code_new].first_value` is still read. Since `code_new == current_entry` and the entry was never written, this reads from an out-of-bounds or uninitialized table slot, potentially leaking memory contents or causing undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential infinite loop from cyclic dictionary references leading to stack buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop traverses the dictionary chain by following `previous_entry` links. A maliciously crafted input could cause dictionary entries to form a cycle (e.g., entry A points to entry B which points back to entry A). Since there is no cycle detection or iteration limit, this would result in an infinite loop that continuously writes to the stack buffer, causing both a denial of service and a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: libnsgif: fix oob in lzw_decode
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to unbounded writes to the output stack
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function writes to the stack buffer via `stack_pos` without ever checking whether the writes remain within the bounds of the buffer (`ctx->stack_base`). The while loop that walks the dictionary chain (`while (code_out > clear_code)`) can produce an arbitrarily long chain of writes. A maliciously crafted LZW stream can construct dictionary entries that produce very long chains or, worse, form a cycle in the dictionary's `previous_entry` links, causing the loop to run indefinitely. This results in writes far past the end of the stack buffer, leading to heap or stack corruption, denial of service, or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential infinite loop if dictionary entries form a cycle
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop follows `previous_entry` links in the dictionary table to unwind a code into its constituent values. There is no check that the traversal terminates (e.g., by limiting iterations to `current_entry` or by tracking visited entries). If a crafted input causes a dictionary entry's `previous_entry` to point back to itself or to another entry in a cycle, the loop will never terminate, causing a denial of service and unbounded memory corruption as `stack_pos` is incremented on each iteration.

commit_message:
fix(plugin): Add default limits for chunks and message size

Based on a reported DoS vulnerability reported by Team82 (Claroty
Research).
cve_desc: The package open62541/open62541 before 1.2.5, from 1.3-rc1 and before 1.3.1 are vulnerable to Denial of Service (DoS) due to a missing limitation on the number of received chunks - per single session or in total for all concurrent sessions. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending an unlimited number of huge chunks (e.g. 2GB each) without sending the Final closing chunk.

commit_message:
SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards

(cherry picked from commit f3604b901d688c194de5e430c7fbab060c9dc8e0)
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Algorithm name copied from network data without ensuring null-termination
vulnerabilities.long_description: The alg_name field is copied directly from attacker-controlled network message data using a fixed-size memcpy without ensuring the resulting buffer is null-terminated. If the incoming data contains TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of non-null characters, the alg_name string will lack a null terminator. When this field is later used as a C string (e.g., passed to crypto_alloc_aead or similar kernel crypto API functions that expect a null-terminated algorithm name), it can result in out-of-bounds reads, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing a crash.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer passed to function after failed NULL check
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the second call to r_bin_java_get_item_from_bin_cp_list returns NULL for inner_class_info_idx, the code prints an error but still passes the NULL obj pointer to r_bin_java_get_item_name_from_bin_cp_list. If that function dereferences obj without a NULL check, this results in a NULL pointer dereference and potential crash. An attacker can craft a malicious Java class file with an invalid inner_class_info_idx to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when parsing number_of_classes without bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code reads a 2-byte unsigned short from buffer at offset 6 (R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset)) without first verifying that sz >= 8. If the buffer is smaller than 8 bytes, this read goes out of bounds. A crafted input with a small attribute size could trigger a heap or stack buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of icattr->name and icattr->flags_str when freeing icattr directly
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the name lookup fails completely, the code allocates icattr->name via r_str_dup, then calls free(icattr) directly. This leaks both icattr->name (just allocated) and icattr->flags_str (allocated earlier via retrieve_class_method_access_string). The proper cleanup function r_bin_java_inner_classes_attr_entry_free should be used, or the fields should be freed individually before freeing icattr.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing negative return value check for bos_desc() before casting to u16, leading to potential information leak
vulnerabilities.long_description: When bos_desc() returns a negative error code, it is cast to u16 without first checking if the value is negative. A negative int (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12) cast to u16 becomes a large positive number (e.g., 65524). The min() with w_length then selects w_length, and the code proceeds to queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf, which may contain uninitialized or stale heap data. This contrasts with other descriptor cases (e.g., USB_DT_CONFIG, USB_DT_STRING) that properly check 'if (value >= 0)' before the min operation. An attacker could send a crafted USB_DT_BOS GET_DESCRIPTOR request to leak kernel heap memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Casting away const qualifier to modify ctrl->wLength can cause undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code casts away the const qualifier of the usb_ctrlrequest pointer to modify wLength. If the ctrlrequest structure resides in read-only memory (e.g., memory-mapped hardware registers or DMA memory that the hardware considers immutable), writing to it causes undefined behavior, potential hardware faults, or data corruption. Even if in writable memory, modifying a shared const structure can lead to race conditions if other code paths read wLength concurrently, as the modification is not synchronized.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing error check on bos_desc() return value leads to potential information leak
vulnerabilities.long_description: When bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the value is not checked before being cast to (u16) in the min() macro. A negative int (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12) cast to u16 becomes a very large positive number (e.g., 65524). The min() then selects w_length, which is attacker-controlled. The code then proceeds to check_value where value >= 0 is true, and req->length is set to w_length. This causes the response to send w_length bytes from req->buf, which may contain stale or uninitialized heap data from previous requests, resulting in a kernel information disclosure. Compare with the USB_DT_CONFIG and USB_DT_STRING cases that correctly check `if (value >= 0)` before using the result.

vulnerabilities.short_description: list_first_entry on potentially empty list yields invalid pointer that NULL check cannot catch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the USB_DT_OTG case, if cdev->config is NULL, list_first_entry() is called on cdev->configs. If the list is empty, list_first_entry() does not return NULL — it returns a pointer computed from the list head via container_of, which points to an invalid memory location. The subsequent `if (!config)` check will not catch this because the pointer is non-NULL but invalid. The code then proceeds to dereference config->descriptors[0] in the memcpy call, leading to an out-of-bounds memory read or crash. An attacker sending a USB_DT_OTG descriptor request before any configuration is registered could trigger this.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `attr` when the size boundary check fails before the NULL check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function allocates `attr` via `r_bin_java_default_attr_new`, then checks `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz` and returns NULL without freeing `attr`. Since the NULL check for `attr` comes *after* this bounds check, a successfully allocated `attr` object is leaked. An attacker providing a crafted input with a small `sz` can trigger this leak repeatedly.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer passed to function after failed NULL check on `obj`
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the second lookup of `obj` via `r_bin_java_get_item_from_bin_cp_list` returns NULL, the code prints an error but continues to call `r_bin_java_get_item_name_from_bin_cp_list` with the NULL `obj`. If that function dereferences `obj` without a NULL check, this leads to a NULL pointer dereference and a crash. A crafted Java class file with invalid constant pool indices can trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `icattr->name` and `icattr->flags_str` when freeing `icattr` with plain `free`
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the name resolution fails completely, `icattr->name` is allocated via `r_str_dup` and `icattr->flags_str` was allocated earlier by `retrieve_class_method_access_string`. The code then calls `free(icattr)` which frees only the struct itself, leaking both `icattr->name` and `icattr->flags_str`. A crafted input with invalid constant pool indices triggers this path.

commit_message:
sctp: Fix kernel panic while process protocol violation parameter

Since call to function sctp_sf_abort_violation() need paramter 'arg' with
'struct sctp_chunk' type, it will read the chunk type and chunk length from
the chunk_hdr member of chunk. But call to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
always with 'struct sctp_paramhdr' type's parameter, it will be passed to
sctp_sf_abort_violation(). This may cause kernel panic.

   sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
     |-- sctp_sf_abort_violation()
        |-- sctp_make_abort_violation()

This patch fixed this problem. This patch also fix two place which called
sctp_sf_violation_paramlen() with wrong paramter type.

Signed-off-by: Wei Yongjun <yjwei@cn.fujitsu.com>
Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel before 2.6.27 does not properly handle a protocol violation in which a parameter has an invalid length, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) via unspecified vectors, related to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen, sctp_sf_abort_violation, sctp_make_abort_violation, and incorrect data types in function calls.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference: `target` is dereferenced before its NULL check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `target->size` is accessed on the very first line of the function to initialize `newsize`, but the NULL check for `target` doesn't occur until the `if(target && source)` conditional on the third line. If `target` is NULL, this dereference causes undefined behavior, typically a crash. An attacker who can control the arguments to this function could trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when realloc fails: original buffer pointer is lost
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `realloc` fails and returns NULL, the result is directly assigned to `target->str`, overwriting the original pointer to the allocated buffer. This means the previously allocated memory can never be freed, resulting in a memory leak. The correct pattern is to store the realloc result in a temporary variable, check it for NULL, and only then assign it to `target->str`.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size calculation can lead to undersized buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables target->len, add_len, and newsize are all int types. If target->len + add_len + 1 overflows a 32-bit integer, the result wraps around to a small or negative value. This can cause the overflow check to be bypassed or cause a much smaller buffer to be allocated via realloc, while the subsequent memcpy copies add_len bytes, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when target->size is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: If target->size is 0 (e.g., after a previous failed realloc set it to 0, or if simplestring_init_str sets it to 0), then incr = target->size * 2 = 0. The subsequent modulo operation `newsize % incr` triggers undefined behavior (division by zero), likely crashing the program. An attacker who can cause a prior allocation failure could trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when realloc fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of realloc is assigned directly back to target->str. If realloc fails and returns NULL, the original pointer is lost and the previously allocated memory is leaked. The correct pattern is to store the realloc result in a temporary variable and only update target->str if the allocation succeeded.

code_snippets: void simplestring_addn(simplestring* target, const char* source, int add_len)
vulnerabilities.short_description: Negative add_len value leads to heap buffer overflow via memcpy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The add_len parameter is a signed int with no check for negative values. If a negative value is passed, the comparison `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` may evaluate to false (no reallocation), but memcpy interprets its size_t length parameter as a very large unsigned value, causing a massive out-of-bounds read from source and write into target->str, resulting in heap corruption.

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition when checking old_rlim->rlim_max without holding the task lock
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads old_rlim->rlim_max for the privilege check (comparing against new_rlim.rlim_max) without holding the task_lock. The lock is only acquired later when writing the new value. A concurrent thread in the same thread group could call sys_setrlimit simultaneously: one thread could lower rlim_max (which passes the check since it's lowering), and the racing thread's capability check could see the old (higher) rlim_max value, allowing it to pass the check and set a higher rlim_max than should be permitted. This allows an unprivileged process to effectively raise its hard resource limit beyond what should be allowed.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write: window_posn advances by match_length even when copy length is clamped
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the non-wrapping match copy branch (the else case), the number of bytes actually copied is clamped to (window_size - window_posn), but window_posn is always incremented by the full match_length regardless. This means window_posn can exceed window_size, and subsequent iterations of the loop will write to window[window_posn] out of bounds. A crafted compressed stream can trigger heap buffer overflow, leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when storing literal bytes without checking window_posn against window_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a literal symbol is decoded (selector < 4), the byte is written directly to window[window_posn++] with no check that window_posn is within the bounds of the allocated window buffer. If window_posn has been corrupted by the match_length advancement issue or if frame_end is computed to be larger than window_size, this write goes out of bounds, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. An attacker providing a malicious compressed stream could exploit this for arbitrary memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in frame_end calculation can lead to incorrect bounds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The frame_end calculation involves arithmetic on potentially large values (out_bytes is off_t, mixed with unsigned int). If the subtraction or addition overflows or wraps, frame_end could be set to a very large value, causing the inner decompression loop to process far more data than intended and write well beyond the window buffer. This could be exploited via a crafted input to cause heap corruption.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient validation of sca_keylength allows out-of-bounds read of heap memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The validation check compares sca_keylength against the full optlen, but the key data starts at an offset within the struct sctp_authkey (after the header fields sca_assoc_id, sca_keynumber, sca_keylength). The correct check should be `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`. As written, a user can set sca_keylength to a value larger than the actual key bytes copied (up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes beyond what was provided), causing sctp_auth_set_key() to read past the intended key data into uninitialized heap memory. This could lead to information disclosure or use of attacker-influenced key material from adjacent heap allocations.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in bounds checks can bypass buffer boundary validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The additions `buf->offset + aoffset + len` and `buf->offset + len` are performed on `size_t` values and can wrap around to a small value if the operands are sufficiently large. This would cause the comparison against `buf->maxlen` to pass, bypassing the bounds check and allowing `memmove` to read/write out of bounds. For example, if `buf->offset + aoffset + len` wraps around zero, the check `> buf->maxlen` would be false, leading to an out-of-bounds memory operation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior when offset is INT_MIN due to abs(INT_MIN)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `offset` equals `INT_MIN`, calling `abs(INT_MIN)` is undefined behavior in C because the positive counterpart of `INT_MIN` cannot be represented as an `int` (due to two's complement asymmetry). This could result in `aoffset` receiving an unexpected value (e.g., still negative when cast, or any other result), which would then corrupt subsequent bounds checks and the pointer arithmetic, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access via `memmove`.

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow due to storing size_t value in int variable
vulnerabilities.long_description: kwset->mind is likely a size_t or similar unsigned type representing the minimum pattern length, but it is stored in an int variable 'len'. If kwset->mind exceeds INT_MAX, the value will overflow or be truncated, potentially resulting in a small or negative value for 'len'. This could cause the subsequent size comparisons (len > size) and pointer arithmetic (text + len, sp = kwset->target + len) to behave incorrectly, leading to out-of-bounds memory reads. An attacker who can control the pattern configuration could trigger buffer over-reads.

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition allows unprivileged threads to raise rlim_max beyond the current hard limit.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The permission check that reads old_rlim->rlim_max is performed without holding task_lock, while the actual write to *old_rlim is done under task_lock. In a multi-threaded process, two threads can race: Thread A calls setrlimit to raise rlim_max to X (currently permitted), and Thread B simultaneously calls setrlimit to raise rlim_max to Y > X. Both threads read the old rlim_max before either writes, so both pass the permission check. Thread A writes first, raising rlim_max to X, and then Thread B writes, raising rlim_max to Y — a value that would have been rejected had the check been done atomically with the write. This allows an unprivileged process to incrementally escalate its hard resource limits beyond the originally configured value.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when substituting macro arguments into tmpFile.line without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding macro argument substitutions, the code copies argument characters into tmpFile.line without checking whether tmpFile.linelen would exceed MAXSTRING (or the buffer size of tmpFile.line). While individual definition characters are checked against MAXSTRING, the argument substitution loop has no such check. If a macro argument is sufficiently long, this will write past the end of the tmpFile.line buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a malicious braille translation table with a macro that triggers this overflow, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak: malloc'd arguments array is never freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `arguments` array is allocated via malloc inside the macro expansion block but is never freed on any code path—neither on the successful return (return 1 at the end of the macro expansion) nor on several error paths (e.g., when compileRule fails recursively or when the line exceeds MAXSTRING). This results in a memory leak each time a macro is expanded. In a long-running process that compiles many tables with macros, this could lead to significant memory consumption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on arguments array via unchecked substitution index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The index `m->substitutions[2 * subst + 1] - 1` is used to access the `arguments` array, which has `m->argument_count` elements. However, there is no validation that this substitution index falls within the valid range [0, m->argument_count - 1]. If the macro definition contains a malformed or out-of-range substitution reference, this results in an out-of-bounds read from the heap, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer type mismatch between signed `len` and unsigned `size` may bypass length checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is declared as `int` while `size` is `size_t` (unsigned). If `kwset->mind` is negative (due to corruption or overflow), the check `len > size` performs a signed-to-unsigned comparison where a negative `len` would be converted to a very large unsigned value, but the `len == 0` check would not catch it. More critically, if `kwset->mind` could be very large (close to or exceeding INT_MAX), assigning it to `int` causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior), potentially resulting in a small or negative `len`. This could bypass the `len > size` guard and lead to out-of-bounds memory reads in subsequent pointer arithmetic (e.g., `tp = text + len`, `sp = kwset->target + len`).

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pos_delta calculation for quantified expressions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The arithmetic expression `-counted * deltanext + (minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount` involves multiple multiplications of SSize_t values that can overflow before the comparison against `SSize_t_MAX - data->pos_delta` is evaluated. Since these are signed integer operations, overflow is undefined behavior in C. The intermediate products `(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount` and `minnext * mincount` can each independently overflow, leading to incorrect overflow checks and potentially corrupted internal state used for regex optimization.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in delta calculation for CURLY quantifiers
vulnerabilities.long_description: When computing the delta (difference between max and min match lengths) for quantified expressions, the multiplication `(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount` can overflow SSize_t. While there is an earlier check for `is_inf` that sets delta to SSize_t_MAX, in the non-infinite case the multiplication can still overflow for large but non-infinite maxcount values (up to REG_INFTY/3 ≈ 21845). This signed integer overflow is undefined behavior and could result in incorrect delta values, potentially causing buffer over-reads or other memory safety issues during regex matching.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SvGROW size calculation for repeated string copying
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a constant string is being repeated for optimization purposes, `mincount * l` is computed to determine the buffer size for SvGROW. If mincount and l are both large values, their product can overflow, resulting in a smaller-than-needed buffer allocation. The subsequent repeatcpy would then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. This could potentially be triggered by crafting a regex with a large fixed-width repeated group.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in delta calculation for CURLY quantifiers
vulnerabilities.long_description: While there is an overflow check for `min += minnext * mincount`, the subsequent delta calculation `delta += (minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount` lacks equivalent overflow protection. When `maxcount` is large (e.g., REG_INFTY) and `minnext + deltanext` is non-zero but `is_inf_internal` is not set (deltanext != SSize_t_MAX and the product doesn't trigger infinity), the multiplication `(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount` can overflow, leading to an incorrect delta value. This could cause incorrect memory allocation sizes or buffer calculations later in regex compilation or execution.

code_snippets:
data->pos_delta += - counted * deltanext +
			(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pos_delta computation for substring tracking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The pos_delta update expression involves multiple multiplications and additions with signed values that can overflow. The guard condition `-counted * deltanext + (minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount >= SSize_t_MAX - data->pos_delta` attempts to check for overflow but the left-hand side expression itself can overflow during computation before the comparison is evaluated, resulting in undefined behavior in C. An attacker crafting a regex with large quantifier bounds could trigger this overflow, potentially corrupting internal optimization data structures.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SvGROW buffer size calculation for repeated string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `mincount` and `l` (string length) are both large, the expression `mincount * l` can overflow, causing SvGROW to allocate a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent repeatcpy then writes beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. This can be triggered by crafting a regex pattern with a large fixed string inside a quantifier with a large minimum count, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

[15840.675016] BUG: unable to handle kernel paging request at fffffffb

[15840.675016]        cab145d0 cab919c0 cab15998 22222222 22222222 22222222 00000001 cab15960
[15840.675016]        000007dd cab0bcf4 cab0bd04 c022cb3a cab0bcf4 cab15a6c ca9290c0 00000000
[15840.675016] Call Trace:
