[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94
[15840.675016] ---[ end trace 4fea40dad6b70e5f ]---

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
	pptr = kmap(page);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of read_mapping_page is not checked for errors before being passed to kmap.
vulnerabilities.long_description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR value (e.g., -EIO, -ENOMEM) instead of a valid page pointer. The function calls read_mapping_page() in three locations and in none of them does it check IS_ERR(page) before passing the result to kmap(). Passing an error pointer to kmap() will dereference an invalid pointer, leading to a kernel oops/panic. An attacker who can trigger I/O errors on the underlying block device (e.g., via a crafted or corrupted HFS+ filesystem image) can cause a denial of service (kernel crash).

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in width*height calculation leads to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of flxh->width and flxh->height (both attacker-controlled from the file header) can overflow a guint, resulting in a much smaller value stored in flxdec->size. Subsequent g_malloc calls allocate undersized buffers, while later operations (memcpy, flx_decode_chunks, flx_colorspace_convert) operate assuming the full width*height dimensions, causing heap buffer overflows.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in flxdec->size * 4 leads to undersized output buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: Even if flxdec->size does not overflow, multiplying it by 4 for the RGBA output buffer can overflow. This creates an undersized buffer, while flx_colorspace_convert writes width*height*4 bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a FLX file with specific width/height values to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when flxfh.size < FlxFrameChunkSize leads to massive allocation or heap overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The frame chunk size (flxfh.size) is read from the file. While there is a check that avail >= flxfh.size, there is no check that flxfh.size >= FlxFrameChunkSize. If flxfh.size is smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize, the unsigned subtraction wraps around to a very large value, potentially leading to excessive memory allocation or other undefined behavior. Subsequently the chunk data is parsed, which can cause out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak on error path: chunk is not freed before early return
vulnerabilities.long_description: When flx_decode_chunks returns failure, the function returns GST_FLOW_ERROR immediately without calling g_free(chunk). Additionally, the allocated output buffer 'out' is also leaked. An attacker can craft a malformed FLX file to repeatedly trigger this error path, causing memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when depth > 8 and colorspace converter is not initialized
vulnerabilities.long_description: The colorspace converter (flxdec->converter) is only created when flxh->depth <= 8, but flx_colorspace_convert is called unconditionally during frame decoding. If the header specifies depth > 8, the converter will be NULL (or uninitialized), leading to a NULL pointer dereference or use of an invalid pointer when processing frames.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in width*height calculation leads to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The size is computed by multiplying width and height from the untrusted file header. Both are cast to guint (32-bit), so their product can overflow, resulting in a small value for flxdec->size. Subsequent g_malloc calls allocate a too-small buffer, while later operations (flx_decode_chunks, memcpy with flxdec->size) may write beyond the buffer if the decoder processes data assuming larger dimensions. An attacker can craft a FLX file with large width/height values to trigger a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size*4 when allocating output buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: Even if flxdec->size doesn't overflow, multiplying it by 4 for the RGBA output buffer can overflow a 32-bit unsigned integer. This results in a smaller-than-expected buffer allocation. The subsequent flx_colorspace_convert call writes width*height*4 bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can set width and height such that width*height fits in 32 bits but width*height*4 does not.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when flxfh.size < FlxFrameChunkSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is no check that flxfh.size >= FlxFrameChunkSize before subtracting. If a crafted frame chunk header has a size value smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction underflows (unsigned), causing gst_adapter_take to attempt an enormous allocation or read. This could lead to denial of service or potentially exploitable memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'chunk' and 'out' buffer on decode error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When flx_decode_chunks fails, the function returns GST_FLOW_ERROR immediately without freeing the allocated 'chunk' (via g_free) or unreffing the 'out' GstBuffer. This causes a memory leak on every failed decode. While not directly exploitable for code execution, an attacker could repeatedly trigger this to exhaust memory.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when substituting macro arguments into tmpFile.line without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding macro arguments into `tmpFile.line`, characters from the argument are copied in a loop without checking whether `tmpFile.linelen` exceeds `MAXSTRING`. While individual characters from `m->definition` are bounds-checked against `MAXSTRING`, the argument substitution loop has no such check. A crafted macro invocation with long arguments could write past the end of the `tmpFile.line` buffer, leading to heap corruption, code execution, or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak: allocated `arguments` array is never freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `arguments` buffer allocated with `malloc(m->argument_count * sizeof(CharsString))` is never freed on any code path — neither on the success path (when `return 1` is reached after the macro expansion loop) nor on error paths (e.g., when `argument_count < m->argument_count` or when `compileRule` returns 0 during expansion). This causes a memory leak on every macro invocation, which could be significant if macros are invoked many times during table compilation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check after malloc for macro arguments array
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of `malloc(m->argument_count * sizeof(CharsString))` is not checked for NULL. If `m->argument_count` is 0, `malloc(0)` returns an implementation-defined result (possibly NULL). If memory allocation fails for any reason and returns NULL, the subsequent loop dereferences the NULL pointer (`arguments[argument_count++] = token`), leading to a crash or undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded prefix table traversal can cause stack buffer underflow via crafted input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop that walks the prefix chain to generate output characters has no bounds checking or cycle detection. A maliciously crafted compressed stream could create a cycle in the prefix table (tab_prefixof), causing the loop to iterate indefinitely. Each iteration decrements stackp and writes a byte, which would write far below the de_stack buffer, resulting in a heap or stack buffer underflow (out-of-bounds write). This can be exploited for arbitrary code execution by carefully crafting prefix table entries that form a cycle.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on outpos before writing to outbuf for first code
vulnerabilities.long_description: When oldcode == -1 (first code processing), the code writes directly to outbuf[outpos++] without checking whether outpos has reached OUTBUFSIZ. While outpos starts at 0 and this is typically the first write, in theory if the buffer state is not as expected, this could write beyond outbuf bounds. More importantly, repeated invocations through the goto resetbuf path could accumulate outpos without flushing, though this particular path is less likely to be exploitable in practice compared to the prefix chain issue.

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing destination bounds check for negative offset leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When offset is negative, the code only validates that the source pointer doesn't go before the start of the buffer (buf->offset < aoffset), but it never checks that the destination write (buf->data + buf->offset for len bytes) stays within buf->maxlen. The positive-offset branch checks buf->offset + aoffset + len > buf->maxlen, which implicitly covers the destination since aoffset >= 0. However, the negative-offset branch has no equivalent check. An attacker could supply a large len value that causes memmove to write past the end of the allocated buffer, resulting in a heap-based buffer overflow. This can lead to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior when offset is INT_MIN due to abs(INT_MIN)
vulnerabilities.long_description: Calling abs() on INT_MIN is undefined behavior in C, because the positive result cannot be represented as an int. If a caller passes INT_MIN as offset, the result of abs() is undefined, and subsequent casting to size_t may produce an incorrect value, potentially bypassing the bounds checks and leading to out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition between umask calls allows temporary global umask change
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code retrieves the current umask by calling umask(0) (which sets umask to 0 and returns the old value), then immediately restores it with umask(mask). Between these two calls, there is a window where the process-wide umask is 0. In a multi-threaded application, any other thread creating files during this window will do so with no umask protection (i.e., overly permissive file permissions). Since umask is a process-global resource, this is a TOCTOU race condition that could lead to files being created with unintended permissions.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting off_t out_bytes to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable out_bytes is of type off_t (typically 64-bit), but it is cast to int (typically 32-bit) in multiple locations. At the end of the function, `i = (int) out_bytes` is used directly as the size argument to mspack_write and for pointer arithmetic on o_ptr. If out_bytes exceeds INT_MAX, the cast wraps around or becomes negative, leading to an incorrect write size — potentially a heap buffer overflow or underflow when mspack_write processes the data or when o_ptr is advanced.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds array access using sym as index into extra_bits/position_base/length_base
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sym value, decoded from compressed input via QTM_GET_SYMBOL, is used directly as an index into arrays qtm->extra_bits[], qtm->position_base[], and qtm->length_base[] without explicit bounds validation. If the model is corrupted or the input is crafted so that sym exceeds the bounds of these fixed-size arrays, out-of-bounds reads occur. The resulting values feed into match_offset and match_length calculations, potentially causing out-of-bounds writes into the window buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient validation of match_offset allows reading/writing uninitialized or out-of-bounds window data
vulnerabilities.long_description: When match_offset > window_posn, the code checks `j > (int) qtm->window_size` but does not verify that the referenced source region (window_size - j) actually contains valid, previously-decompressed data. A crafted match_offset can reference uninitialized window memory, leaking sensitive heap data into the output stream. Additionally, the check uses a signed comparison (`j > (int) qtm->window_size`) which can fail if window_size > INT_MAX, allowing an otherwise invalid offset to bypass the bounds check.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded prefix table chain traversal can cause stack buffer underflow and infinite loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop that follows the prefix chain to decode an LZW code has no cycle detection or iteration bound. A maliciously crafted compressed input can set up a cycle in the prefix table (tab_prefixof), causing the loop to iterate indefinitely. Each iteration decrements `stackp` and writes a byte, so this will write far before the start of the `de_stack` buffer, corrupting adjacent memory. This is a classic heap/stack buffer underflow (write out-of-bounds) that can lead to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Write to outbuf without bounds check when processing first code
vulnerabilities.long_description: When oldcode is -1 (first valid code), the code writes to outbuf[outpos++] without verifying that outpos is less than OUTBUFSIZ. While outpos starts at 0, if the decompressor encounters multiple CLEAR codes resetting oldcode to -1, the outpos value could accumulate beyond the buffer boundary before a flush occurs, leading to an out-of-bounds write on the outbuf buffer.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double free of buffer and path when packet construction fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When any of the buffer_add or sftp_packet_write calls fail, the code frees both `buffer` and `path` inside the if-block, then falls through to free them again unconditionally. This is a double-free vulnerability that can lead to heap corruption, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution. Additionally, the error path is missing a `return -1`, so execution continues into the while loop trying to read a response for a packet that was never successfully sent.

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when find_next_block() returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls find_next_block() in multiple places (both directly and within the COPY_BUF macro) without checking if the return value is NULL. If find_next_block() returns NULL (e.g., due to a truncated or malformed archive), the code immediately dereferences the pointer (b->buffer or blk->buffer), leading to a NULL pointer dereference. This can cause a crash (denial of service) and in some environments could potentially be exploitable for code execution. A crafted sparse tar archive that is truncated at the right point would trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled allocation size can cause excessive memory consumption
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sparse_map_size value is read directly from the archive data and decoded as a size_t. This value is then passed to xcalloc to allocate an array. An attacker can craft an archive with a very large sparse_map_size value (up to TYPE_MAXIMUM(size_t)), causing the program to attempt to allocate a huge amount of memory, leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion. While xcalloc may abort on allocation failure, the lack of a reasonable upper-bound check before allocation allows a malicious archive to consume excessive resources.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds read when p equals end
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `p` equals `end`, the check `p > end` is false, so execution continues to `len = *p++`, which dereferences `p` at the `end` boundary. Since `end` typically points one past the last valid byte of the buffer, this is an out-of-bounds read. An attacker providing a crafted input where the data pointer is exactly at the end of the buffer could trigger a one-byte heap or stack over-read, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing a crash. The fix is to change the check to `if (p >= end)`.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer over-read due to incorrect size calculation passed to uni_utf8_get_char_n
vulnerabilities.long_description: The size argument is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since `p < parser->end` inside the loop, `p - parser->end` is negative. When cast to `size_t` (an unsigned type), it wraps around to an extremely large value. This tells `uni_utf8_get_char_n` that there are far more bytes available than actually exist in the buffer, allowing it to read well past `parser->end`. This can lead to out-of-bounds memory reads, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`.

commit_message:
MDEV-25787 Bug report: crash on SELECT DISTINCT thousands_blob_fields

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash via certain long SELECT DISTINCT statements that improperly interact with storage-engine resource limitations for temporary data structures.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Negative value cast to size_t produces an incorrect (very large) buffer length argument
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `p - parser->end` is always negative when the loop condition `p < parser->end` is true. Casting this negative `ptrdiff_t` to `size_t` wraps it to an extremely large unsigned value. This means `uni_utf8_get_char_n` is told the remaining buffer is enormous, so it may read well beyond the actual end of the buffer. The correct expression should be `parser->end - p`. An attacker sending crafted input could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Logic error: keytab reported as available when the entry is NOT found
vulnerabilities.long_description: When krb5_kt_get_entry returns KRB5_KT_NOTFOUND (meaning the requested principal was not found in the keytab), the function sets rc = 1, indicating the keytab IS available. This is the opposite of the correct behavior—it should set rc = 0 (not available). This logic error means the function will falsely report that a valid keytab entry exists for the service principal even when it does not. In a TLS/Kerberos authentication context, this could cause the server to advertise Kerberos cipher suites or attempt Kerberos authentication when no valid key material is present, potentially leading to authentication failures, denial of service, or falling through to weaker authentication mechanisms.

commit_message:
MDEV-25787 Bug report: crash on SELECT DISTINCT thousands_blob_fields

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash via certain long SELECT DISTINCT statements that improperly interact with storage-engine resource limitations for temporary data structures.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak due to shallow free of sftp_attributes struct
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code uses SAFE_FREE (a simple free/NULL macro) to release errno_attr, which is an sftp_attributes structure that typically contains dynamically allocated sub-fields (strings for owner, group, name, etc.). The correct function to use would be sftp_attributes_free(), which recursively frees all internal allocations. By using SAFE_FREE, only the top-level struct is freed while all internal heap-allocated members are leaked. In a long-running application where mkdir is called repeatedly against existing paths, this leads to unbounded memory growth which could eventually cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds read when p equals end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check uses `p > end` instead of `p >= end`. When `p` is exactly equal to `end`, the check passes, and the code proceeds to dereference `*p++` to read the length byte. This reads one byte past the valid buffer boundary, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker who can control the input data positioning could trigger this to read adjacent memory, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing a crash.

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_sname_to_principal(krb5context, NULL, 
                                     kssl_ctx->service_name ? kssl_ctx->service_name: KRB5SVC,
                                     KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);

    krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of krb5_sname_to_principal is not checked before using princ
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return code from krb5_sname_to_principal() is stored in krb5rc but is never checked before the code proceeds to call krb5_kt_get_entry() using the `princ` output parameter. If krb5_sname_to_principal() fails, `princ` may be NULL or uninitialized, and passing it to krb5_kt_get_entry() could cause a NULL pointer dereference or other undefined behavior. An attacker who can influence the conditions that cause krb5_sname_to_principal to fail (e.g., malformed service name via kssl_ctx->service_name) could trigger a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Function returns 1 (keytab available) when the keytab entry is NOT found
vulnerabilities.long_description: When krb5_kt_get_entry returns KRB5_KT_NOTFOUND, indicating the requested principal was not found in the keytab, the function sets rc=1 and returns, signaling that the keytab IS available. This is a logic error: the function is supposed to check if a keytab entry is available, but it reports success in the exact case where the entry does not exist. This could lead to the caller incorrectly proceeding with Kerberos authentication that will subsequently fail, or could be leveraged to bypass authentication checks that rely on this function's return value.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

vulnerabilities.short_description: DMA read/write of first descriptor uses fixed DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS size instead of actual descriptor length, causing out-of-bounds DMA access.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When writing back the 'used' bit on the first descriptor of a completed packet, the code reads and writes sizeof(desc_first) = DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS * 4 bytes. However, all other descriptor accesses in the function use the dynamic size 'sizeof(uint32_t) * gem_get_desc_len(s, false)', which may be smaller than DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS words. This inconsistency means the write-back operation writes more data than the actual descriptor size, potentially corrupting adjacent descriptors or other guest DMA memory. A malicious guest could arrange memory layout such that this oversized write corrupts critical data structures, or it could leak information from adjacent memory through the oversized read.

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if alloc_root returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: If alloc_root() fails and returns NULL, the code proceeds to use the pointer in memcpy and strcpy without any NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. This could cause a crash (denial of service).

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via unescaped log file content rendered in HTML
vulnerabilities.long_description: The contents of the log file are read and appended directly into an HTML textarea without any HTML entity encoding. If an attacker can influence log file contents (e.g., by sending crafted HTTP requests or triggering log entries containing malicious strings like '</textarea><script>...</script>'), the injected content will break out of the textarea element and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any user viewing the log page. This could lead to session hijacking, credential theft, or other client-side attacks.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing params->base->data[0] without checking params->base->length
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks that params->base->data is non-NULL but never verifies that params->base->length is at least 1 before accessing params->base->data[0]. If an attacker provides a crafted ECPARAMETERS structure where base->data is a valid non-NULL pointer but base->length is 0, the access to data[0] constitutes an out-of-bounds read. This could lead to reading uninitialized or adjacent heap memory, potentially causing information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when dl->length is smaller than the shared object extension length
vulnerabilities.long_description: If dl->length is less than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression dl->length - so_ext_len underflows (since both are unsigned size_t), producing a very large value. This causes dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len to point far before the start of the string buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read in my_strcasecmp. An attacker who can control the plugin name (dl) with a very short string could trigger this to read arbitrary memory or cause a crash.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if alloc_root fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of alloc_root is not checked for NULL. If memory allocation fails, s will be NULL, and the subsequent memcpy and strcpy calls will write to a NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service). While memory allocation failures may be rare, they are possible under resource exhaustion conditions.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Zero-length authentication key can be set, weakening SCTP authentication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The validation checks ensure optlen is strictly greater than sizeof(struct sctp_authkey), but sca_keylength is permitted to be 0. An attacker with local access can set a zero-length authentication key (sca_keylength = 0) for an SCTP association or endpoint, as the check only verifies that sca_keylength does not exceed the available buffer space, not that it is non-zero. This effectively allows setting a trivial/empty authentication key, potentially undermining the integrity of SCTP HMAC-based authentication. The fix should additionally check that sca_keylength is greater than zero before accepting the key.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Zip Slip: path traversal via crafted archive entry filenames
vulnerabilities.long_description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly in getChildFile without validating that the resulting path stays within targetDirectory. A malicious zip entry with a filename like '../../etc/cron.d/evil' would cause files to be written outside the intended extraction directory, potentially overwriting arbitrary files on the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Arbitrary symlink creation allows writing to or reading from unintended locations
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a zip entry is marked as a symbolic link, its content (the symlink target) is read from the archive without any validation. An attacker can craft a zip with a symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary path (e.g., /etc/passwd), and subsequent entries could then follow that symlink to overwrite sensitive files outside the target directory.

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition when modifying a potentially shared SSL_SESSION object in-place (CVE-2015-1791)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function directly modifies `s->session` (freeing the old ticket, allocating a new one, updating session ID) without first checking if the session is shared (i.e., its reference count is greater than 1). If the SSL_SESSION object is shared across multiple SSL connections (e.g., in a multi-threaded server that resumed sessions), another thread may concurrently access the session's `tlsext_tick` pointer after it has been freed but before it is reassigned, leading to a use-after-free. The fix is to duplicate the session via `ssl_session_dup()` before modification when the reference count exceeds 1. An attacker could exploit this by causing a NewSessionTicket to be processed on a shared session, potentially leading to crashes or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in ticket length validation when ticklen is large
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `ticklen` is declared as `int` and is populated from `n2s()` which reads a 2-byte unsigned value (0–65535). The expression `ticklen + 6` is computed as a signed int addition. While the 16-bit range of n2s prevents practical overflow on most platforms, the comparison mixes a signed `int` result with a `long`. If this code were adapted or if `ticklen` were sourced differently, the signed arithmetic could wrap. This is a minor concern in the current context but represents fragile validation logic.

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow via unbounded fscanf %s into fixed-size buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads from the file using fscanf with the "%s" format specifier into the stack buffer `str` of size `GmfStrSiz`, without specifying a maximum field width. A maliciously crafted mesh file containing a very long token (longer than GmfStrSiz) would overflow the `str` buffer on the stack, potentially allowing an attacker to overwrite the return address or other stack data, leading to arbitrary code execution. This occurs in two separate `do...while` loops when parsing ASCII mesh files. The fix is to use a width-limited format specifier, e.g., `fscanf(msh->hdl, "%<GmfStrSiz-1>s", str)`.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

[15840.675016] BUG: unable to handle kernel paging request at fffffffb
[15840.675016] IP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56
[15840.675016] *pde = 00008067 *pte = 00000000
[15840.675016] Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT DEBUG_PAGEALLOC
[15840.675016] Modules linked in:
[15840.675016]
[15840.675016] Pid: 11575, comm: ln Not tainted (2.6.27-rc4-00123-gd3ee1b4-dirty #29)

[15840.675016] EIP is at kmap+0x15/0x56

[15840.675016]        000007dd cab0bcf4 cab0bd04 c022cb3a cab0bcf4 cab15a6c ca9290c0 00000000
[15840.675016] Call Trace:
[15840.675016]  [<c0231cfb>] ? hfsplus_block_allocate+0x6f/0x2d3

[15840.675016] Code: 00 00 75 10 83 3d 88 2f ec c0 02 75 07 89 d0 e8 12 56 05 00 5d c3 55 ba 06 00 00 00 89 e5 53 89 c3 b8 3d eb 7e c0 e8 16 74 00 00 <8b> 03 c1 e8 1e 69 c0 d8 02 00 00 05 b8 69 8e c0 2b 80 c4 02 00
[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94
